1236: dim. of atenamitl. Small parapet, very low ledge.
1237: «at the water's edge,» town near Tetzcuco (Clav.). RR. atentli, co.
1238: pret. OATENCOCONAUI: v. (for nino) — to grope about randomly searching for lice or fleas. RR. atemitl, coconauia.
1239: Sudden, unexpected, that gives no warning. RR. a priv. nemachitia.
1240: Suddenly, unexpectedly; atenemachpan mo-chiua, an event, an unforeseen occurrence, that which happens or takes place suddenly. See ne-
1242: Lousy, lice-ridden, covered with lice. R. atemitl.
1243: pret. OATENO: nitla — to sit, to settle on the banks of a body of water, near a river, etc. R. atentli.
1244: Drowned, flooded, submerged, full of water. RR. atl, tenqui.
1245: Bank, shore of a river, riverbank, water's edge: atentli mic-toca, to skirt, to follow the bank; uei atentli, seashore. With the postposition co: atenco, at the water's edge. RR. atl, tentli.
1246: pret. OATENTOCAC: nitla — to skirt the sea, to follow the banks of a river, etc. RR. atentli, toca.
1247: Willow that grows on the banks of streams. RR. atentli, uexotl.
1248: Goodness, generosity. RR. a priv. teoyeuacayotl.
1249: She who does not value a man's services, an ungrateful woman (Olm.). RR. a priv. teoquichittani.
1051: Pret. OAPATZCAC: n (for ni) — to press, wring a wet cloth, to squeeze the water out of it. RR. atl, patzca.
1052: Herb juice. R. apatzca. APATZCALVINO, n. Weak wine, wine of the second pressing. RR. apatzcalli, vino.
1053: Spring, fountain, water that one causes to flow. R. apatzca.
1054: Pret. OAPAUI: to entangle something (embarbascar pescado).
1055: Large earthenware bowl. RR. apazth, yaualh.
1057: dim. of apazrui. Very small bowl.
1060: Canal, conduit, pipe. RR. atl, piazth.
1061: OR APICHAUHQUI, adj. v. Numb, frozen with cold. R. apichaui (?). nitla — to stop, to hinder.
1062: Pret. OAPICHAUHTICATCA: n (for ni) — to be trembling, frozen with cold; apichauhtica, he is dead, numb with cold. RR. apichaui (?), ca.
1063: n. v. Trembling caused by cold. R. apichaui (?).
1065: Sterile, unable to have children. RR. a- priv., pilhua. — Gluttony, greediness. R. [apizhi].
1066: Pret. OAPILO: n (for ni) — to draw water from a well, a cistern, etc. RR. atl, piloa.
1067: Potter, maker of vessels. RR. apilolli, chiua.
1068: Earthenware pot, small vessel used to draw or carry water; uei apilolli, large earthenware vessel. With the postposition ca: apilolhtica icac in atl (Olm.), in the vessel is the water. R. apiloa.
1152: Ignorant, foolish, imbecile. RR. a priv. mamatcaua.
1153: OR AQUIMAMATQUI, adj. v. Clumsy, unskilled, loutish, foolish, ignorant. RR. a priv. mama.
1155: With ignorance, with stupidity. RR. aquimamatilizthi, ca.
1159: One who denies having done something. RR. a priv. chiualyetoca. AQUIN? interr. pron. usually followed by nel and noço: aquin nel? aquin noco? aquin nel noco? or aquin noço nel? who? to whom? aquin nel nech-palehuiz? who will help me? aquin nel noco yaz? who will go? aquim maca? who not? aquim maca qui-mati y? who does not know that? aquim maca tlatlacole? (Car.), who is not a sinner? aquin, the one who; in çaço aquin, whoever it may be, anyone at all. Plur. aquique? See Ac?
1161: Generous, liberal, beneficent; aqui-tetlaçomaca ytlatqui or yaxca, magnanimous, great in one's generosity; lit. one who does not give the refuse of one's goods. RR. a priv. tlaçolli, maca.
1162: pret. OAQUITI: nitla — to employ merchandise, to make something fit somewhere. R. aqui.
1163: adv. Very little, in very small quantity; in aquimamatqui Esaïü oqui-namacac in tyacapantlatqui in ipampa aquiton epatzactli (Car.), the foolish Esau sold his birthright for a small amount of lentils.
1164: Tuft, lock of hair, tuft of hairs. RR. aquiton, tzontli.
1165: pret. OAQUIXTI: itla — to rinse, to wash laundry, to pass it through water. RR. atl, quixtia.
1166: adj. v. Rinsed, washed. R. aquixtia.
1167: n. of instrument. Pump, or any object suitable for drawing out water. R. aquixtia.
1169: priv. a. quixtilpilli. *AQUIXTILVINO, n. Second wine, small wine, wine mixed with water. RR. aquixtilli, vino.
1111: adj. v. Badly done, bad, evil. R. aquallachiua.
1112: Evildoer, one who acts badly. R. aquallachiua.
1113: A learned, lettered person who is bad, dangerous, wicked. RR. aqualli, tlamatin.
1115: adj. and n. Bad, evil, wickedness; Evil words. The wicked one; pl. aqualtin, the wicked; aqualli nic-chiua, to do evil; aqualli ipan nechicaualizth, hardening, perseverance in evil; oc ualca inic aqualli, worse, more wicked. RR. a priv., qualli.
1118: Wickedness, malice; cenca aquallotl, very great, excessive wickedness. R. aqualli.
1121: n. Blister, bubble, bleb. RR. atl, quaqualaca.
1122: Canal, conduit, aqueduct. RR. atl, quauitl.
1123: Wooden bridge. RR. atl, quauhpanauaztl. AQUEHUATL? See Ac?
1125: At no time. RR. a priv., quemman.
1126: Nothing, not at all, in no way; Wickedness, malice. R. aquen nic-mati, I do not concern myself with it, I take no care of it; aquen mo-mati (Car.), he takes no account of anything, he is shameless; aquen nic-chiua, acan achi nech-cocoa in motetlaiyohuiltiliz (Car.), I feel nothing, your torments do not move me in the least; aquen nic-notza, he is nothing to me, he is not my relative; lit. I call him by no name, I do not call him my relative; aquen nopan, that does nothing to me; aquen topan, that does not concern us, we have nothing to do with it; aquen ancom-mati, you do not feel it, you do not concern yourselves with it; aquen ca, he cares about nothing; aquen ca in noyollo, my heart is perfectly calm, nothing troubles it; aquen ca iyollo, gentle, peaceful; aquen nechiualizth, calm, tranquility, inner peace; aquen mochiuani, lasting, durable; aquen techiuani, harmless, inoffensive; aquen tlathuiztli or tlattalizth, shamelessness, impudence. RR. a priv., quen.
1127: pret. OAQUEQUEZ: nitla — to tamp, beat the earth to construct a wall, without digging a trench or laying a foundation. RR. (?), quequeca.
1128: pret. OAQUETZ: to raise one's head, to be shameless; to make cacao, to make water rise (colgar el agua); aquetza or aquetzca ciuatl, shameless, immodest woman. RR. atl, quetza.
1129: With head raised, proudly, shamelessly. RR. aquetzahiztli, ca.
1130: 5. n. Action of raising the head, effrontery. RR. aquetza.
1132: pret. OAQUETZALTI: nite — to make someone raise their head. R. aquetza.
1133: Or AQUETZQUI, n. v. One who makes cacao. R. aquetza.
1135: Pret. OAQUETZTIMANCA, v. n. To have the bung or opening facing upward, speaking of a barrel. RR. aquetza, mani.
1136: Pret. OAQUETZTINEN: n (for ni) — to go with head raised, head held high. RR. aquetza, nemi.
1137: Pret. OAQUETZTOCA or OAQUETZTOYA: n (for ni) — to be lying on one's back. RR. aquetza, onoc. AQUI? See Ac?
1138: Pret. OIC: n (for ni) or non — to enter a place, a hole, etc.; to sell, to be sold; to contain, to flow, to be exhausted, speaking of merchandise; aqui in tonatiuh, the sun sets; oon-ac in tonatiuh, the sun has set; notech aqui, it penetrates into me; itech aqui, to penetrate, to enter into something; teilan n-aqui, to have relations with a woman during sleep; etlan n-aqui, to apply care or heart to something; ie non-aqui, to seek a dishonorable end (echar el cuervo); with the neg. an-aqui or anon-aqui, pret. aon-ac or aonon-ac, not to enter, etc. Nitla — to bear fruit; itech tla-aqui, it bears fruit, it has fruit, speaking of a tree. Impers. acoa, pret. oacoac, one contains, one can enter; often preceded by the particle on: aocmo on-acoa, there is no room, not everyone can enter. See Ac.
1139: Pret. OAQUI: nino or nono (for nino or nonno) — to dress oneself, to put on, to wear a tight-fitting, closed garment. Nite or nice — to support, uphold, favor someone; tepan nite-aquia, to substitute someone for another, to replace them; tetlan nite-aquia, to favor, to support someone; çan oc niman petlatitlan icpaltitlan nimitz-aquia (Olm.), for today I cover your fault; with the neg. anonte-aquia, to harm, to be contrary to someone, to think little of them. Nitla or nice — to transplant, to plant, to take cuttings, to thrust in, to put a thing into a hole, etc.; atlan nitla-aquia, to put, to plunge a thing into water; tzatzalan nic-aquia, to put a thing in the middle of others; etlan c-aquia itlauelilocayo, he is dissembling, hypocritical, he hides his faults; ipan nitla-aquia, to employ one's goods or wealth toward something; nociyacac nitla-aquia, to hide an object in one's bosom; niqual-aquia, to add to, to increase something, to amplify an account, etc.; oc no ixquich niqual-aquia, to add yet as much again. Impers. aquiua: inic acan temac aquiuaz (Olm.), so that one may not be caught.
1140: 5. n. Hole, opening, place where one enters or hides; tonatiuh iaquian, the west: lit. the sun's hiding place. With the postposition pa: tonatiuh iaquiampa ehecatl, west wind. R. aqui.
1141: adj. v. Dried up, or which is sometimes dry, speaking of a stream. RR. atl, quica.
1142: Restless, agitated, without repose. RR. a priv. yeuailhuia.
1143: 5. n. Astonishment, rapture. RR. a priv. yeuailhuiliztli.
1144: Enraptured, transported, beside oneself. RR. a priv. yeuailhuia, itta.

890: Before a vowel, pronoun of the 2nd person plural. You: an-nemi, you live; am-aci (Olm.), you approach; before w and a, the nasal sometimes disappears: aualazque (for an-ualazque), you will come; ayazque (for an-yazque), you will go. Joins with nouns and adjectives: amehuan or amehuantin antlatlacoanime, you (are) sinners. In an also serves as a vocative: in annoleopixcahuan, to my priests, you my priests. With the particle on: amon; amon-tlaçotla in motatzin, you love your father.
891: Pret. OAN: ain (for nino) — to grow, increase, sprout, lengthen, develop, abstain, deprive oneself, restrain oneself; m-ana, it extends, lengthens, speaking of a cord or thread; textlam m-ana, he is shameless, indiscreet, importunate in speech. Nite or nonte — to take, seize, direct, accompany someone. Nitla or nic and, with the particle on, nocon — to take, remove, carry away a thing, to reach elevated objects, receive, pull, bring out, etc.; nic-ana in espada, to draw, unsheathe the sword; nic-ana notequiuh, to take up a burden, enter into a function, undertake a task; fig. to be at the last extremity; tech nic-ana, to copy, to derive one thing from another; tetech nitla or nic-ana, to imitate someone, to take them as an example; itlan or itzalan nic-ana, to choose a thing, to take it from among others, to extract it. Passive, analo or ano (Par.); notech ano, to edify, to serve as an example, to be taken as a model. Impers. teanalo (Olm.), all take.
893: n. Near water, the sea, etc.; used to designate either the valley of Mexico, where there were formerly considerable lagoons, or the seashore — in short, any region where water is abundant. RR. atl, nauac.

896: (?), adv. On the water's edge, on the seashore, on the other side of the river, etc. See Anazu.

1299: Water, urine, sinciput, head, brain, war; atl cecec, cold water; atl tlatzacutli inic mo-coneuaz, dam; lit. water stopped so that it may rise; atl yotlatocayan, stream; lit. place where water runs; ei atl, sea, great water; atl, tlaqualli, nourishment, food; fig. oc atl, child, infant; lit. still water; atl, tlachinolli, war, battle; atl cecec tetech nic-pachoa, to correct, to chastise someone; atl cecec itech tlapacholli, chastised, reprimanded. Cal., name of the ninth day of the month (Clav.). In comp. nauh (for no-auh), my water, my brain, my head, etc.; mauh (for mo-auh), your water; iauh, his water; iauh itlaqual, his food; iauh itlaqual in tanima, the nourishment of our soul; tauh (for to-auh), our brain, the brain in general. With the postp. itic, pan, tlan, uic: atliuc, in the water; apan, on the water, in the water; apan omic in noteachcauh (Par.), my elder brother drowned; atlan or atitlan, in the water, near the water; atitlan tlatlahtli, put in the water; atlan nitla-tlalia, to put a thing in the water; atlan temictiani, pirate, corsair; atlan micqui, drowned; auic (Par.), toward the water. Rev. atzintli; in comp. natzin (for no-atzin), my water; tatzin, his water; in oyuh nic-celi in iatzin totecuiyo Dios, when I received the water of Our Lord, i.e. baptism; plur. natzitziuan (Olm.). See A.

1459: ATLEY or ATLEIN, adv. Nothing: atle ay or ani, idle, lazy, one who does nothing; atle ailiztli, idleness, laziness; atle c-aci, nothing equals it, nothing approaches it; atle ipan, in no way; atle ipan ni-tlachia, to despise, to disdain; lit. I see, I consider it as nothing; atleimma illa, none of that; atleimma ca onic-chiuh, I did not remain without doing anything; atle imma quimo-panauiha, nothing surpasses it; atle yuhqui or yuhquin, nothing like it, nothing similar; atle yuhquin nican, different, extraordinary, foreign; lit. nothing like this here; atle niman, not at all, in no way; in atle or atley, without, when there is nothing. Plural: atleitin or atleme; inserting the pers. pron. ni, ti, an: ani-tlei, I am nothing; atitlei, atlei, atitleitin, amatleitin, atleitin or atitleme, antleme, atleme (Olm.). 1594: OR AUIYAYALIZTLI, n. Sweetness of smell, delicious taste, exquisite flavor; teca ou tepan auializth, mockery, ridicule, contempt, joy, pleasure taken in another's misfortune. R. auia.
1595: Joyful, pleasant; ica auiall, mocked, despised, humiliated. R. auia.
1596: OR AHUIANI, adj. and n. v. Immodest, indecent, prostitute; content, satisfied, rejoiced; auiani call, house of prostitution; n-awiani, to be a prostitute; teca auiani, pleased at another's misfortune, mocker, scoffer. R. auia.
1597: Pret. OAUIANITIC: (for mi) — to be a prostitute, a harlot. R. auia.
1598: AUICCAMPA or AUICPA, adv. Here and there, from one place to another; auic ou auiccampa tlachia (Par.), he looks here and there; ahuic yayatinemi (Car.), he runs in all directions; mace ximo-tlali, inic auic t-iauh (Car.), stop going from one side to the other; lit. stay still, for you go in all directions; auicpa nech-totoca in tlatoque, aquemman nech-caua in nino-cehuiz (Car.), the chiefs lead me from one place to another, they do not let me rest for a moment; auic yani or auic nenqui, vagrant, wanderer, who lives by going in all directions. RR. a priv. uic.
1599: Fresh, recent, tender, new, that which is pleasant. R. auia.
1601: Pret. OAUICHI: nic — to sprinkle, to wet someone. R. atl.
1602: Pret. OAUICYA: (for ni) — to wander, to be a vagabond, to go in all directions; one also says without comp. auic niauh; auicyauh, he is agitated, one who has no rest. RR. auic, yauh.
1603: With oars; auictica ni-pano, to navigate by means of oars. RR. auictli, ca.
1604: Pret. OAUICTLAZ: in (for nino) — to stir, to agitate oneself, to be restless, speaking of the sick. Nite — to push someone away. RR. auic, tlaça.
1605: Pret. OAUICTLALO: nin (for nino) — to run here and there. RR. auic, tlaloa.
1607: Pret. OAUICTOPEUE: nite or nic — to push away, to reject someone. RR. auic, topeua.
1609: Pret. OAUICUETZ: n (for ni) — to stagger, to waver, to oscillate, to go from side to side. RR. auic, uetzi.
1610: Vagrant, wanderer, staggering, going from side to side. R. auicuetzi.
1634: Or AHUILNEMI, pret. OAUILNEN, v. n. (for mi) — to fornicate, to live in debauchery; ca amo t-ahuilnemiz (Par.), you shall not fornicate; auilnemi, he or she is dissolute. RR. auiliui, nemi.
1635: Or AUILNEMILIZYOTL, n. v. (Par.). That which relates to lust, to a life of debauchery. R. auilnemiliztli.
1636: With lust, with debauchery. RR. auilnemiliztli, ca. 1) DICTIONARY OF THE NAHUATL LANGUAGE.
1637: Corrupt life, life of debauchery, of pleasure. R. auilnemi.
1638: Or AUILNENQUI, adj. and n. v. Vicious, carnal, debauched; pl. auilnenque; imix- pampa æ-ehua in auilnenque (Par.), flee the presence of corrupt people. R. auilnemi.
1639: Pret. OAUILO: nino (for mino) — to debase oneself, to plunge into disorder. Nitla — to shame, to cause to blush, to catch, to put at fault. R. aa.
1640: Pret. OAUILPAUI: nite — to seduce, to lead someone astray through caresses, flattery, etc.
1642: Pret. OAUILPEUALTI: to provoke through jokes in order to deceive someone. RR. auiltia, peualtia.
1643: Pret. OAUILPOPOLO: nitla — to be prodigal, to squander, to lose, to destroy one's property. RR. auiliui, popoloa.
1644: Pret. OAUILQUIZ: n (for nino) — to lower oneself, to debase oneself through vice, to lose esteem and regard. RR. auiliui, quiça.
1645: Vice, corruption, depravity, bad reputation. R. auilquiça.
1646: Pret. OAUILQUIXTI: nino (for nino) — to humble oneself, to lower oneself, to dishonor oneself. Nite — to debase, to dishonor someone. Nitla or nic — to deteriorate a thing; fig. to fail in one's duty; c-auilquixtia in petlatl, in icpalli (Olm.), he governs his state badly. Pass. auilquixtilo, to be defamed, to have a bad reputation. RR. auiliui, quixtia.
1647: Pret. OAUILTELPOGATIC: nino (for nino) — to give oneself over to debauchery, to sensual pleasure, to be a libertine. RR. auiliui, telpocatl.
1648: Pret. OAUILTELPOGNTIC: n (for nino) — to give oneself over to debauchery, to libertinism. RR. auiliui, telpochtle.
1649: dim. Young debauchee, little libertine. RR. auiliui, telpochtle.
1650: Pret. OAUILTI: nino (for nino) — to recreate oneself, to pass the time merrily; acaltica m-auiltia, he sails for amusement; tlaçolli, teuhtli quim-auilhtia (Olm.), he delights in, persists in vice; teca m-auiltia, he mocks, laughs at someone, mimics them. Nite — to amuse, to entertain, to joke with, to tickle someone. Nitla or nic — to ride a horse at a gallop. R. aua.
1651: n. Ceremonial envoy. RR. auiltia, titlantli.
1652: Pret. OAUITEC: nitla — to plaster, to whitewash a wall with lime. RR. atl, uitequi.
1653: Aunt. In comp. naui (for no-aui), my aunt; nauihuan, my aunts; teaui, someone's aunt. Rev. auitzintli; in comp. nauitzin, or, as women say, nauiticatzin, my aunt; in nauilzin ye namique (Par.), my aunt is already married.
1574: OR AUELITILIZTLI, n. Impossibility, powerlessness. R. aueliti.
1575: Stutterer, one who is hindered in speech, who does not speak well. RR. auel, tlatoa.
1576: Pret. OAUELLAMAMANCA, v. n. To be disturbed, speaking of the weather. RR. auel, mani.
1577: n. Level of still water, tranquility of water, liquid plain, vast expanse of water. RR. ail, uel, man.
1578: Pret. OAUEUETILI: nimo (for nimo) — to compare oneself to the cypress, to grow tall, to elevate oneself, to think highly of oneself; with the neg. anim-aueuetiha (Olm.), I think little of myself. R. aueuetl.
1579: Or AHUEHUETL, n. Deciduous cypress, com. bald cypress, a large American tree belonging to the genus taxodium; fig. chief, lord (Olm.); ma thuicpatzinco tihuian in tlatocacihuapilh, yuhquim ma, or ma yuhqui, or yuhquin pochotl, ahuehuetl (Par.), let us place ourselves under the protection of the Virgin as under a cypress, etc. RR. ail, ueue.
1580: Or AUEXUTL, n. A kind of willow; fig. in aueæutl totomolini (Olm.), he is rich, he is comfortable; lit. the willow is budding. RR. atl, uexoll.
1581: conj. used to join clauses. And, but, yes: ca cenca nimitz-tlaçotla, auh inic uel moyollo pa-chiuiz in ca uel neltiliztl, n izca in nimitz-maca, I love him greatly, and so that you may be convinced of it, I give you this; auh? what? what? why? how? auh quen ti-huitzt? auh tlein tiqu-itoa? why do you come? what do you say? auh quen? and how? auh quen ca o? and how is that? Loc. adv. auh ye, and: auh ye ti-ceyaz (Chim.), and you will wish, yes you will consent; auh in axcan, and now, today, at this moment; auh in ye imman, it is the moment; auh in ye imman in otlaimmantie, now has come the fixed, settled, agreed time; auh ini or imn, that is it, moreover, but; auh in oc cequi or aquexquich, but, moreover; auh intla, and if; auh intla uallaz, and if he were to come; auh intlaca or intlacamo, and if not: auh in-tlaca no çac, and if there were no one, if he were absent; auh intlacamo ciaz, and if he does not wish to; auh niman, and immediately, at once; to indicate more clearly the immediate succession one says auh ca niman: auh ça niman ihcuac oncan in hual motlahtocatlalli in Moteuheçomatzin Aocoyotl tlah-tohuani mochiuh Tenuchtitlan (Chim.), immediately afterward Moteuhecomatzin Xocoyotl was installed as king of Tenochtitlan. Rev. auhtzin: auhtzin, tlatoanie, ca omic-chiuh, ohi, seigneur, je l'ai fait, oui, my lord, I have done it, I have done that.
1582: situated in the province of Colima, near the coasts of the Pacific Ocean (Clav.).
1583: Pret. OAUIX, etc.: n (for ni) — to have what one needs, to be content, satisfied; aic n-auia, to suffer always, never to be satisfied; teca or tepan n-auia, to mock someone, to rejoice at the misfortune that befalls them; auia in patolli, the game goes well; amo auia patolli, the game is bad; ma oc ton-auiacan, let us rejoice a little longer; with priv. an-auia, to suffer, to not have what one needs, to be discontented.
1584: Pleasant, sweet, of good taste, good smell, musky; lit. that which gives pleasure; centlali mani ic auiyac or centlali mo-teca ic auiyac, or further centla mamani ic auiac, which spreads a good smell everywhere, on all sides. R. auia.
1585: Pleasantly, in a sweet manner. R. auiac.
1586: Sweetness, fragrance, good smell; used in comp. iauiaca, its smell; uacqui xochitl iauiaca, smell, fragrance of dried flowers; totonqui tlaqualli iauiaca, smell of hot food. R. auiac.
1587: Or AUIYACAYOTL, n. Sweetness, gentleness, good taste, good smell. In comp. iauiacayo, rev. iauiacayotzin: acan quenami iauiacayotzin (Par.), nothing equals its sweetness. R. auiac.
1588: Pleasant to the taste, to the smell, that pleases the palate, etc. R. auiacamati.
1589: Or AUIYACAMATI, Pret. OAUIACAMA, etc.: nino — to find good, sweet, excellent (a dish, a smell, etc.). RR. auiaca, mati.
1590: Or AUIYAYA, Pret. OAUIAX, AUIAYAC OR
1591: : n (for ni) — to have, to give off a good smell; to smell good or bad (Olm.); achi or çan quenin n-auiyaya, to give off a slight and gentle fragrance.
1592: Or AUIYALIA, Pret. OAUIALI, etc.: nino (for nino) — to savor food. Nitla or nite — to perfume, to embalm a thing, to give good taste, to properly prepare dishes, to make them good and savory. R. aa.
1593: With joy, pleasure, etc.; teca auializtica, with joy at another's misfortune, with mockery. RR. auializtli, ca.
1734: Or AZCAPUTZALLI, n. Anthill. With the postp. co: azcaputzalco, in the anthill. RR. azcatl, puçaua.
1738: Toltec princess, daughter of Pochotl, who married the Chichimec chief Nopaltain (Clav.). RR. azcatl, xochitl. *AZEITECHIUHQUI, n. v. Oil maker. RR. azeite, chiua. *AZEITECOMITL, n. Vessel used to hold oil. RR. azeite, comitl. *AZEITECONTONTLI, n. dim. of AZEITECOMITL. Oil cruet, small vessel intended to hold oil. *AZEITEYO, adj. Oily. R. azeite. *AZEITENAMACAC, n. v. Oil merchant, one who sells oil. RR. azeite, namaca.
1740: (Clav.). Quadruped of the anteater species. RR. aztatl, coyotl.
1742: pret. OAZTAYOAG, v.n. To fill up, to become covered with herons, speaking of a place or a tree. R. aztatl.
1744: Mountain (Clav.). — Town of the Campine (Torq.). C, CO, postp. In, on, upon, through: c is joined to nouns ending in a vowel: ahuicatl, the sky, tlhuicac, in the sky; and co, to nouns ending in tl, li, in: tlapamitl, terrace, tlapanco, on the terrace; acalli, ship, acalco, in the ship; capulin, plum, capulco, in the plum. The monosyllabic tletl, fire, also takes co: tleco, in the fire. These postpositions also serve as suffixes for many place names: Chapultepec, Quauhnahuac, Mexico, Tetzcuco, etc. C, QU, QUI, QUIN, rel. pron. joined to verbs when accompanied by their complement: 1° c, qu, qui with a singular complement; 2° quin or quim with a plural complement. 1° c and qui before consonants: nic-tlaçotla in notatzin, in cuicatl, I love my father, the song; tic-tlaçotla, qui-tlaçotla, anqui-tlaçotla in notatzin, in cuicatl, you love or we love, he loves or they love, you love my father, the song; c before the vowels a, o, u: nic-ana, tic-ana, c-ana, anc-ana in ichtequini, I take, you take, etc. the thief; nic-ololoa in ichti, I wind the thread; nic-uicaltia in tlatoani, I accompany the sovereign; tic-uitequi in ipiltzin, you strike his son; qu before the vowels e, i: niqu-echuia in noma, I put blood on my hand; tiqu-icça in nicxi, you press my foot; etc. The complement is sometimes a verb: nic-mati n-amapohuaz, I know how to read, lit. I know this, I will read. — 2° quin or quim before m and the vowels, except u: niquin-cui in ichtequinine, I take the thieves; niquim-itia in tohuam-
1746: Heron, egret; nette aztatl, stork. In comp. yaztauh, its egret plume; fig. tlacotoniilli yaztauh, ymecaxicol, Free, exempt, independent.
1749: n. plur. Aztecs who left Aztlan in the 11th century to settle later in the Anahuac (Clav.).
1753: Town originally occupied by the Aztecs, whose location, the subject of numerous investigations, is still unknown. It is generally placed north of the Gulf of California (Clav.). dim. of AZTLAPALLI. pohuan, I see our relatives; tiquim-mauiztiha in tohuampohuan, you honor our relatives. Before i, quin changes to qui: niqui-uapaua, tiqui-uapaua in tlatlacoanime, I support, you support the sinners. If the noun forming the complement takes the plural marker, quin is also used: axiquin-cui uapaltin, take some planks. — With two complements of which only one is expressed: nicte-cuilhia in totolin, I take from someone the hen; niquinte-cuilia in totoltin, I take from someone the hens; nictla-cuihia in notatzin, I take something from my father; miquintla-cuilia in maceualtın, I take something from the subjects. — With two complements both expressed: 1° both in the sing., nic-cuilia in Pedro itotol, I take from Peter his hen; 2° both in the plur., niquin-cuilia in maceualtin in intotolhuan, I take from the subjects their hens; 3° one in the sing. and the other in the plur., niquin-cuilia in Pedro ymaceualhuan, or more rarely nic-cuilia in Pedro ymaceualhuan, I take from Peter his subjects. — With reflexive pronouns, the relative is inserted: sing., nicno-, ticmo-, quimo-, ticto-, anquimo-, quimo-cuitia (in notlatlacol), I confess, etc. (my fault); plur., niquinno-, tiquinmo-, quimmo-, tiquinto-, anquimmo-, quimmo-cuitlahuia in icnotlaca, I take care, etc. of the unfortunate.
1754: To be, to find oneself; pret. ocatca; fut. n-iez; imp. man-ie; opt. man-ieni; ye ic ni-ca, to be dying, to be at one's last extremity; amo uecan ni-ca, to be near, not to be far; achi tetlan ni-ca or tetech ni-ca, to be close to someone; noyollo iuh ca, to be assured of something, to know it; lit. my spirit is thus, such is my feeling, my opinion; ipan ni-ca, to be at the top of something; ma nic-chiuhtie, do not concern yourself with what I have done; lit. do not be at what I have done; in ocatca or in oyeco, that which was, that which is past. — Accompanied by the particle on, this verb means to have, and is used only in the 3rd person: onca, he has, there is something; onca ixoxal, he has a hernia; oncatca, there was; oncate, they are there, there are people; oc onca yta, he still has his father; onca tlaxcalli, there is bread; atle onca, there is nothing; onyez tlaolli, there will be maize. In comp. ni-tlachixtica (Olm.), to be watchful. Rev. etztica, yelitica or yetzinolica: ommo-yetztica in totecuiyo Dios, God our Lord is present who; campa mo-yetz-tica in tlatoani? where is the governor? Impers. yeloua or yeloua, pret. oyeloac, etc., there are people (in the house, in the houses); itlan yeloac, accompanied; lit. there is someone, there are people near him; fig. nocuitlapan, noteputzco yeloac, to have the care, to be charged with directing, governing; lit. on my back, on my shoulders there are people. Olmos gives for the impers. eloac, pret. oeloaca.
1618: Pret. OAUILGEMILHUITI: # (for ni) — to waste one's day, to spend it in trifles.
1620: Pret. OAUILGHIUH: nite — to make someone laugh, to tickle someone. RR. auiltia, chiua.
1621: Pret. OAUILI: nitla — to water plants. R. ail.
1622: n. A corrupt old woman, given over to pleasures. RR. auiliu, ilama.
1623: Pret. OAUILIUH: # (for ni) — to ruin oneself, to be destroyed by vice. Pass. auililo: oauihloc in petlail, in icpalli (Olm.), he has fallen, he has lost his dignity: lit. the mat, the seat have been destroyed. R. auia.
1624: Pret. OAUILIUINTIC: # (for ni) — to drink heavily and in various places, so as to become drunk. RR. auihia, tuintia.
1625: Pret. OAUILIZITTAC: nitla — to spend foolishly, to squander one's wealth in pleasures. RR. auiliui, itta.
1626: Pret. OAUILLACANECQUE: # (for tito) — to insult one another, to exchange mutual insults and taunts. RR. auiliui, tlacanequi.
1627: Pret. OAUILLANAUHTIA, v. intr. sometimes preceded by the adv. yuian. To worsen, to go from bad to worse. R. auillanaui.
1628: Pret. OAUILLANAUE: # (for ni) — to worsen, to deteriorate, to become gradually more ill. RR. aulia, tlanau.
1629: adj. and n. Vain, boastful, one who tells jokes. RR. auiltia, tlatoani.
1630: Language, reasoning full of vanities, trifles, and jokes. RR. auiltia, tlatohizthi.
1631: Vain, useless words, nonsense, jokes. RR. auiltia, tlatolli.
1632: n. A corrupt, debauched old man.
1775: Pret. OCACAYAGHILI: nitetla — to undo, crumble something for someone. R. cacayaca.
1776: or CACAYACTLI, adj. v. Rare, sparse, not thick, not dense. R. cacayaca.
1777: Pret. OCACAYAQUILI: nitetla — to undo, crumble something for someone. R. cacayaca.
1778: Pret. OCACAYAUH, freq. of CAYAUA: nino — to mock; eca nino-cacayaua, I laugh, I play with someone, I deceive them; noca mo-cacayaua, he mocks me.
1779: Pret. OCACAILPI: nite — to cause, to give trouble to someone (dar fudo). RR. çacatl, ilpia.
1780: Pret. OCACAYOTILI: nitetla — to let someone's field become overgrown with weeds, to neglect to work it; from çacayotia. R. çacatl.
1781: Pret. OCACAYOTICA: ni — to be negligent, coarse; yean çacayotoc (Olm.), he is perverse, scatterbrained. RR. çacayotia, onoc.
1782: Uncultivated, overgrown with weeds. R. çacaixtlauatl.
1783: Uncultivated plain, overgrown with weeds, savanna. RR. çacatl, ixtlauatl.
1784: Pret. OCACALAGAC, v. n. To make noise, to resonate, said of cracked vessels.
1785: Noise, sound of cracked objects. R. cacalaca.
1787: Pret. OCACALAC, freq. of CALAQUI: ni — to enter in several places; tepan ni-cacalaqui, to go from one person to another, from house to house.
1788: Pret. OCACALAQUI, freq. of CALAQUIA: nitla — to transport, bring in, store away a thing.
1789: Action of entering; tepan cacalaquiliztli, action of going from house to house. R. cacalaqui.
1790: One who enters, penetrates often; tepan cacalaquini, one who goes from house to house. R. cacalaqui.
1791: Pret. OCACALATZ: nitla — to make noise either by shaking objects, or by opening or closing a door, a window, etc.
1792: Pret. OCACALÇALHUI, freq. of ÇALHUIA: nitetla — to glue, arrange, mend something for someone; to graft one's trees.
1793: Pret. OCACAL: nitla — to shoot, to launch arrows at someone.
1352: Despised, blamed; ye no ye allayequitolli, deprecated after having been praised.
1354: Impassibility of a glorified body. RR. a priv. tlaihiouhztli.
1355: Coarse, material, speaking of persons. RR. a priv. tlayolizmatcachiuani.
1356: adj. Material, coarse, speaking of objects. RR. a priv. tlayolizmatcachuhtl.
1357: n. v. Immoderate, dissolute, spendthrift. RR. a priv. laixryeyecoani.
1358: With intemperance, with excess, without moderation. RR. atlaixyeyecohztli, ca.
1361: Intemperate, immoderate. RR. a priv. tlaixyeyecoqui.
1363: One who has received the order not to do something. RR. a priv. tlaixquauhuwiani.
1364: adj. and n. v. Immoderate, dissolute, spendthrift. RR. a priv. tlaixtamachiuani.
1365: n. Gosling, small goose. RR. atlalalacatl, conetl.
1367: Reservoir, cistern, fish pond, pond, accumulation of water, etc. RR. atl, tlahilli.
1369: Lack of memory, weakness of mind. RR. a priv. tlalnamiquihzthi.
1370: Forgetful, one who has no memory. RR. a priv. tlalnamiquini.
1371: In heaps, in large quantities, etc.
1333: Or ATLACOMULATL, n. Well water. RR. atlacomolli, atl.
1334: "in the well," v. located in the valley of Toluca and conquered by Axayacatl (Clav.). Crudely.
1337: etc.: nin (for nino) — to fall into a well, into a ditch, to get stuck somewhere.
1339: Or ATLACOMULLI, n. Well, hole, ditch containing water. RR. atl, tlacomolh.
1340: Or ATLACOMULOA, pret. OATLA—comolo, etc.: n (for ni) — to dig a hole, a ditch, a well, etc. RR. atl, comoloa.
1341: Curb, rim of a well. RR. atlacomulli, tentli.
1343: located about 4 leagues from Oaxaca and renowned for its beautiful cypresses or ahuehuetl (Clav.). RR. a, tlacuechahuayan.
1344: Water lily or water lotus. RR. atl... (r).
1345: Pret. OATLAGUIC: ni (for ni) — to draw water; nino — to go fetch, to go draw water; on-atlacuito, he went to fetch water; non-atlacuiz, I will go fetch water. RR. atl, cui.
1346: n. v. Water carrier. R. atlacui.
1347: place now called Tacubaya; Atlacuihuayan chane (Par.), inhabitant of Atlacuihuayan. R. atlacui.
1348: Pret. OATLACUITIA: to take water. R. atlacui.
1349: Bucket for drawing water. R. atlacu.
1350: Despised; ye no ye atlayectencualli, depreciated, blamed after having been praised. nino — to go
1856: (Olm.). Hard, poor straw; fig. çan nic-toplema in tetl, in quauitl, in cacaquall (Olm.), I endure adversity and reprimands. RR. cacatl, aqualli.
1857: freq. of caque. One who wears shoes; ni-cacaque, I wear shoes, I make use of footwear. Pl. cacaqueque.
1858: Someone, anyone, whoever, no matter who. RR. çaçan, euatl.
1859: pret. ocacac, freq. of caqui: to listen, to spy on someone.
1860: Pret. OCACAQUIXTI: #i — to introduce, to drive straws into the body as a sign of humiliation or penance, to remove them and offer them in sacrifice; Mic — to let a field become overgrown with weeds, to neglect to work it. RR. çacatl, quixtia.
1861: Basket, rush basket. RR. cacatl, tanathi.
1863: Capital of a state subjugated by Cortés (Clav.). RR. çacatl, tepetl, c.
1866: One of the chiefs of the Mahuatlaca who arrived under Acolotl in the Anahuac (Clav.). RR. çacatl, cochi.
1867: Straw, grass, rush. — One of the chiefs who led the Toltecs from Huehueitlapallan to Tollantzinco (Clav.).
1870: and State to the N. of the republic of Tlaxcala (Olm., Clav.). RR. çacatl, tlan.
1871: (Clav.). Herb used for the treatment of ulcers. RR. çacatl, tletl, patli.
1872: Maritime province to the S.E. of the province of Colima; capital of the same name.
1873: pret. OCACATZAC: mitla — to bind tightly, to squeeze, to press, to tamp down, to pile up something.
1874: and n. Black man, Black woman. R. cacatza.
1373: pret. OATLAMAOHTI: nite- — to praise, flatter someone; nech-atlamachtia, it flatters me to the point of being proud, arrogant; with the neg. anite-atlamachtia, to afflict, torment someone, to be importunate, to cause pain or grief. R. atlamati.
1374: Disordered in what one does. RR. a priv. mamat.
1376: Pret. OATLAMATI: nino- (for nino-#) — to become arrogant; atlamati, he is presumptuous, proud; tetech n-atlamati, to have pride, presumption due to the protection of a powerful person.
1378: With pride, with presumption; atlamatiliztica mo-cemati, he holds himself in extremely high esteem, he is excessively proud. See Gececamari. RR. atlamatilizth, ca.
1379: Presumption, pride, arrogance, disordered self-esteem; tetech atlamatilili, audacity, effrontery, presumption that comes from the support of a highly placed person. R. atlamati.
1380: Or ATLAMATQUI, adj. v. Proud, arrogant. R. atlamati.
1382: With boldness, with extravagance. RR. atlamauhcaittaliztli, ca.
1383: Boldness, extravagance. RR. a priv. tlamauhcaittalizth.

1755: With, by, by means of, through; joined: 1° to nouns: etica, with a stone; oflica, by the road; quauhtica omic-uitec, I struck him with a stick; atlamatiliztica, with presumption; neltiliztica, with truth. With names of days and numbers, ca indicates the time, duration, and price: yeihuitica omo-zcalitzino in totemaquixticatzin, on the third day our Savior rose again; cetica æochiqualli onic-couh, I bought a franc's worth of fruit; ometica, yetica, etc., two, three, etc.; — 2° to possessives no, mo, t, etc., of, for, thanks to: noca li-huelzi, you mock me; ayac noca, no one takes notice of me; moca nicno-chiua, I take care of you; moca omic-chiuh (Olm.), I did it for you; ica, of him, for him; ica tetl, with a stone; ica quauil, with a stick; rev. icatzinco in totecuiyo, for the love of our Lord, or thanks to our Lord; ioca, of us; amoca, of you; inca, of them; teca, of someone (masc. or fem.).
1756: or conj. Already, where, certainly, because, why, since; ca ye, already; ca ye amo, already not; ca ye qualh, it is well, it is good, so be it, yes; ca yauh in Pedro (Olm.), where is Peter going; ca uepa? where? which way? on which side? in what place? to what extent? ca amo oniqu-ito (Olm.), certainly I did not say it; ca amo ma, but no, certainly not: ca amo ma oc ca tlacatl itech am-poui, you do not depend on any other person; ca amo maca nic-nequi, not because I do not want it; ca aoctle, there is nothing left; ca aoctle ipatica, there is no longer any remedy, it is finished; ca çaçan quenin ti-qualani? (Par.), why do you get angry over nothing? ca yuhquim ma, it is so much, it is thus; ca yaca, there it is, it is here. Ca sometimes serves to add greater emphasis: nehuatl ca nitlatlacoani (Par.), I am a sinner; ca nelli, true, certain, positive.
1757: reduplicative syllable in frequentatives: cacalaqui, to enter frequently; cacaxtolli, by fifteens; etc., etc.
1758: particle serving to link: for example, nic-qualancaitta in noyaouh (Par.), I look upon my enemy with anger (from qualani and itta); 1° two verbs; 2° a verb with a noun indicating possession: nic-topilecatlalia (Gar.), I appoint him constable (from topile and tlalia); etc., etc.
1759: suffix of the pluperfect: onite-anca, I had taken someone; otla-pixca, you had kept something; opololoca, it had been lost; otetlaçotlaloca, all had loved; etc., etc.
1760: reduplicative syllable in frequentatives: çcaçalhuia, to glue, to re-glue; etc., etc.

980: pret. OANTIA: (for nino) — to grow, to put on weight; m-antiuh, he is growing. R. ana.
981: pers. an joined to the indef. pron. tla, you it, you them; antla-uitequi, you strike it or you strike them. See G, Qui.
983: pret. OANTOCA: (for nino) — to be lying full length, to be stretched out. RR. ana, onoc.
984: or AOCMO (for amo-oc), adv. Not yet, no longer: aoc ac (Par.), there is no one left; cuix aoc tlatoa in cocoxqui? aoc, aocmo uellatoa (Car.), is the sick person not speaking yet? no, he can no longer speak well; cux oc tipiltontli? ca aocmo tipiltontli, ca ye tiueue (Car.), are you still young? certainly you are no longer so, you are already old; aocmo mo-tlahia, troubled, tormented; aoc mo-tlalia yyollo, dissolute, corrupt; aoc notlaqual nic-mati, to forget oneself at table, to eat without restraint; lit. to no longer know one's food. Loc. adv. aoc aya or aoc axcan, with difficulty, hardly; aoc campa or aoc can, nowhere any longer, in no place; campa otimoyetzticata, ca ye izquilhuitl in aoc can, or in aoc campa nimitznо-tlha? where did you go, that for several days I have not seen you anywhere? aoc ca ueli (for aoc can ueli), there is no more room; lit. nowhere is it possible; aoc can ca iyollo, dissolute, corrupt, speaking of a woman; lit. her heart is nowhere; aoc can cecni, nowhere, from no other place, in no other place; aocmo can appears synonymous with aocmo canin or aoc can, aoc campa (Crist. del Castillo); aocmo huecauh, soon, without delay: aocmo huecauh in aciquiuh in notitlan (Car.), my messenger will not be long in arriving; aocmo tle, or aoc tle, aoc tlei, nothing, not anything, no reason, etc.; there is nothing left, nothing more; aocmo tle ic nino-tzinquixtia, I have nothing with which to excuse myself, to refuse; aoc tle ic tito-tzinquixtizque or aoc tle itech tito-tlamuzque, we will soon have no more excuse; aoc tle imma itla? is there nothing else? aoc tle ueli, tired, exhausted, who can no longer work; aoc tle ypan itto, defamed, who is no longer cited in anything; aoc tle yuan or yuhqui, nothing similar, equal, as (great, etc.); aoc tle yuan or yuhqui inic tlacotli, there is nothing more precious; lit. nothing equal in being precious; aoc tle yuhqui inic ni-tzatzi, to cry out extremely, to shout loudly; aoc tle yuan inic temauhti in miquiztli, there is nothing as frightening as death; aoc tle yuhqui inic iztac in cepayahuitl, there is nothing as white as snow; aoc uel or ueli, impossible.
985: AYOCQUIC or AOQUIC, adv. Never again: aoquic ompa n-iaz, I will never go there again; aocquic mc-chiuaz, I will not do it again; n ompa nochan nino-yolcuitiaya, auh in ye ixquich cahuitl in nican ni-nemi, aoquic nino-yolcuitia, quin ic ceppa nino-yolcuitia (Car.), in my country I used to confess, but since I have been living here I have never confessed again, this is the first time I am confessing. RR. aoc, aic.
986: Mead, a drink made of honey and water. RR. atl, octli.
1555: Hairy worm, caterpillar that lives on the oak. RR. auail, tecolotl.
1557: Holm oak, oak. RR. auatetz, mul. Oak, holm oak. RR. auail,
1558: Oak, holm oak; sharp thorn; caterpillar that lives on shrubs. In comp. yauauh, its thorn.
1561: Pret. OAUATZ: v. (for mi) — to drain, to remove water. RR. atl, uatza.
1563: Drained, dried, having no more water. R. auatza.
1564: 5. See. Drainer, one who draws off water. R. auatza.
1565: Pret. OAUATZETZELIUH, v. n. To drizzle, to rain in small drops. RR. auachtli, tzetzeliui.
1567: Pret. OAUAUI: nin (for nino) — to prick oneself on thorns. Mie — to prick someone with thorns. R. auatl.
1568: A type of pastry. RR. .. (?), tamall.
1569: n. Abyss, water, deep sea; amo cenca auecatlan, shallow sea; uei auecatlan, very deep sea. RR. atl, uecatlan.
1572: or AUELI, adv. Impossible, not absolutely; auh auel poliuh in Tlatilulco, auel qui-poloque, but Tlatilulco could not be destroyed, they could not destroy it; auel miquini, immortal; auel mo-notza, incorrigible; auel mo-quetza, weak, feeble, unable to stand upright; auel motla-caualtia, incontinent, dissolute, unable to abstain from something; auel ni-tlatoa, I cannot speak. RR. a priv. wel.
1573: or AUELITINI, adj. Impossible, that which cannot be done. RR. a priv. ueliti.
2075: Pret. OCALPATLAC: nino— to change dwelling, to move house. RR. calli, patla.
2076: Pret. OCALPIX: nite— to guard, govern, manage the house. RR. calli, pia.
2077: (Par.). Lord who has stewards. R. calpixqui.
2078: Stewardship of a house. R. calpia.
2079: (Olm.). Place where things are kept; pl. cacalpixcan. RR. calpia, can.
2080: Used in comp. only, nocalpixcapo (Par.), house steward like me.
2082: Pret. OCALPIXCAT: nino— to be or become a majordomo (Olm.). R. calpixqui.
2083: Pret. OCALPIXCATILI: nite— to replace a house steward, to carry out his duties. R. calpixcati.
2084: Steward, majordomo; pl. calpixque and cacalpixque to refer to various stewards. In comp. nocalpixcauh, my steward; pl. mocalpixcauan, your stewards. R. calpia.
2085: Pret. OCALPOL- ur, etc.: nite— to convoke, to gather the inhabitants of a district. R. capolli.
2086: and mountain to the east of Mexico (Clav.). RR. calpolli, tlall, pan.
2087: augm. of CALLI. Large house, vast hall, district, suburb, hamlet, borough, district; pl. calpoltin or calpolti; cecen calpultin, in each group, in each district. With the postp. pan: calpolpan or calpulpan, in a district, a suburb; cecen calpolpan, in each district, in each suburb, hamlet, etc.; calpolpam poui, neighbor of a borough or that which concerns a district.
2088: Roofing, ridgeline of a house. RR. calli, quaitl.
2089: Pret. OCALQUECHILI: nino— to build a house for oneself. R. calquetza.
2090: Pret. OCALQUETZ: nite— to construct, to build a house. RR. calli, quetza.
2091: Construction, building of a house. R. calquetza.
2092: n. v. Mason, house builder. R. calquetza.
2094: Wall, surface of a wall; caltechtli ic nino-motla, to strike oneself, to knock against a wall. RR. calli, tech.
1534: Stake, pile, piling used to secure the foundations of a construction in a marshy location. R. atzotzona.
1535: freq. of arzan. Very often, many times; atztzan nitla-inamiqui, to think very often about something, to reflect a great deal; atztzan tlalna-miquiliztli, deep meditation.
1536: Master, possessor of water; pl. auaque (Om.); joined to the word tepeua to mean: city-dweller, inhabitant of a town; lit. one who has the water and the mountain. The ancient Mexicans established their cities on heights and near springs. With the postposition can: ahuacan, tepehuacan (Par.), from town to town, from country to country. R. ail.
1537: pret. OAUAG, etc.: m (for mo) — to dispute, to quarrel; netech m-aua, to have disputes, to argue. Nite — to scold, to reprimand someone; with the particle on, nimitzon-ahua (Par.), I scold you; annechon-ahua, you scold me.
1539: Pret. OAUACHI: to wet with water. Nite nin (for nino) — to sprinkle oneself, to wet someone. Nila — to water something, to throw water. R. auachtli.
1540: or AUECHO, adj. Covered with dew, having dew. R. auachtli or auechtli.
1541: Pret. OAUACHPIXAUR, v. n. To drizzle, to rain in small drops. RR. auachtli, pixaui.
1542: Pret. OAUACH-QUIAUH, etc. v. n. To rain in small drops, to drizzle.
1545: Pret. OAUACHTZETZELIUH-ROC, v. n. To rain lightly, to drizzle. RR. auachtli, tzetzeliui, onoc.
1546: Flooded, speaking of a field; wet, not dry nor arid. RR. a priv. uacqui.
1547: adj. Thorny, furnished with, full of thorns; auayo coyametl, porcupine; fig. atiuitzo atauayo ipan nimitzno-machitia, I commend myself to you who are fortunate; lit. who is without thorns. R. auatl.
1548: n. One who has thorns. In comp. teauayoa (Olm.), relative of someone. R. auatl.
1549: Weak, without strength. RR. a priv. uapauac.
1550: Bodily weakness. RR. a priv. uapaualiztli.
2176: dim. of canacmic. Very thin, very slender.
2177: n. v. Absence. RR. cana, yauh.
2178: n. Net, duck trap. In comp. nocanamatl, my net. With the postp. c: canamatlaс (Olm.), in the trap. RR. canauhtli, matlatl.
2180: pret. OCANAUH: nitla — to thin, to make a thing thinner; to beat metal.
2181: adj. v. Thinned, made thin, thin, not thick, fine, light; canauac caxitl, thin bowl or vessel; canauac tlaixpechotl, cloth, bed sheet; iztac canauac, very fine and white. R. canaua.
2182: Fineness, delicacy, state of a well-worked, finely made thing. R. canaua.
2183: n. v. Linen or cloth merchant. RR. canauac, namacac.
2184: Temples (of the head). In comp. nocanauacan, my temples; tocanauacan, our temples, the temples in general. RR. canauac, cantli.
2185: Hair, locks that are on the temples. RR. canauacantli, tzontli.
2186: Extremely large and long serpent (Hern., Clav.). RR. canauhtli, coatl.
2187: Duckling, small duck. RR. canauhtli, conetl.
2188: dim. of canauhconetl, very small duck.
2191: Duck or goose egg. RR. canauhtli, tetl.
2193: suburb in the N. E. part of Mexico (Clav.).
2194: CUIX? or CAN CUIXPAN? See Can? *CANDELAYACACOTONA, pret. OCANDELAYACACOTON: nitla — to snuff a candle. RR. candela, yacacotona. *CANDELAYACACOTONALIZTLI, n. v. Act of snuffing a candle. R. candelayacacotona. *CANDELAYACACOTONANI, n. v. One who snuffs the candle. R. candelayacacotona. *CANDELAYOLLOTLI, n. Candle wick.
1897: Foam of cacao or of a similar thing. RR. cacauatl, poçonilloil.
1898: n. (Bél.). Cacao tree. RR. cacauatl, quauitl.
1899: n. v. Bodily disorder, fever, indisposition. R. cacauaca.
1900: from çaçauari. Mangy, scabby; niçaçauati, to have scabies, smallpox.
1902: pret. OCACAUHTIA: nitla— to leave something behind; tetech nitla-cacauhtiuh, to leave one's goods to someone, to make them one's heir. R. cacaua.
1903: pret. OCACAUHTOYA or OCACAUNTOCA: nocon or ye nocon— To be in agony, on the point of dying, at death's door. RR. cacaua, onoc.
1904: Covered with couch grass. R. çacauitztli.
1905: n. Couch grass. RR. çacatl, uitztli.
1906: Loose, poorly woven, poorly fastened, not holding, etc. R. caxaua.
1907: Poorly fastened, loose, poorly arranged. R. caxaua.
1908: adj. v. Loose, poorly woven. R. caxaua.
1909: One who carries loads, a servant. In comp. tecacaxe (Olm.), someone's domestic servant. R. cacaxtli.
1910: A kind of wooden hook for carrying loads; a species of bird.
1911: Every fifteen days. RR. cacaxtolli, ilhuitl, ca.
1912: n. freq. of caxtolli. Every fifteen; cacaxtolli ocecen (for on-cecen), every sixteen; cacaxtolli omoome, every seventeen; cacaxtolli omeei, every eighteen; cacaxtolli onnanawi, every nineteen. With the postpositions can, pa: cacaxtolcan, every fifteen parts; cacaxtolpa, every fifteen times; oc cacaxtolpa, every fifteen times again; cacaxtolpa oceceppa, every sixteen times; and so on.
1913: or CACAXTOLTETL, adj. n. freq. of caxtoltetl. Every fifteen; cacaxtoltetl ocecen (for on-cecen), every sixteen; cacaxtoltetl omoome, every seventeen; cacaxtoltetl omeei, every eighteen; cacaxtoltetl onnanawi, every nineteen.
1914: pret. OCACCAYETOCAC: micno— to pretend to know or understand something; muchi nicno-caccayetoca, to deceive oneself, to pretend to know everything.
2215: Grain; centetl capollotl, one grain (of a grape, etc.). R. capolin.
2217: Cherry orchard, a place planted with cherry trees. RR. capolquauitl, tla.
2218: Cherry tree; uacqui capolquauitl, dry cherry tree. RR. capolin, quauitl.
2220: Zapotec; pl. Capoteca, inhabitants of the province of Capotecapan (Clav.).
2221: a province situated between that of Macatlan and the Pacific (Clav.).
2222: (Bét., Clav.). Sapodilla, American medlar, generally a stone fruit (zapote).
2224: Pret. OCAPUTZAUH, #i — To blacken oneself, to become dirty; to turn black, speaking of an object.
2227: Bird; fig. child, son, beloved lord, cherished one (Olm.).
2228: A very precious feather from the bird çaquantototl (Olm., Aub.).
2229: A bird with yellow and brilliant plumage, the size of a sparrow (Hern.). RR. caquan, tototl.
2230: Pret. OGAQUAUATINEN: mi — to be dying of thirst and hunger; noten çaquauatinemi, my mouth is parched. RR....(?), nemu.
2231: One who wears shoes, who has footwear; pl. caqueque. R. cactli.
2232: As, in the same way, so to speak, half, halfway; çaquem mopopogauhtoc, çaquen çaçauintoc or quimattoc, to be half dead, to be in agony; çaquen yolh, yolloc or itztoc, half alive, half upright; çaquen yattiuh, heavy, weighty, who can barely move. RR. çan, quen.
2233: Pret. OCAQUETINEN: # — to wear shoes. RR. caque, nenu.
2234: Pret. OCAC: nino or nonno — to be satisfied, to approve; amo nino or amo nonno-caqui, to not be satisfied, to disapprove, to challenge a judge or witnesses. Nite or nonte — to listen, to lend an ear, to be on the alert, to obey; ayuh nite-caqui, to guess, to penetrate someone's secret intentions; anite-caqui, to disobey, to not listen. Nitla or nice — to hear, to grasp, to understand things, to hold audience; ichtaca nitla-caqui, to spy, to listen in secret; ayuh or aompa nic-caqui, to take things the wrong way, in bad part; in aompa tlacaqui (Olm.), perverse, one who listens to nothing; amtla-caqui, to become brutish, to harden oneself; acon-cuiz, alocon-caquiz? will you not follow, will you not heed the advice? you will surely obey.
2196: Or CANELPA, adv. Yes, it is so, like, since, because, for; canel totech itauhqui, or, it is indeed what was promised to us; canel nimitz-tlaçotla, nimitz-macaz in amatl, as I love you, I will give you the book; achcanel, ach canel noço, synonym of ach canoço, given that, since, because: ca huel nelülizth, ca huel melahuac in oquim-italhui ach canellamatini, where ach canoço tlamatini, it is indeed the truth, it is very exact what he said, given that he is a learned man. RR. ca, nel.
2198: YEPA or YE UEL? See Can?
2199: ? adv. Where? from where? which way? canin mach or canim mach? where? how? how much? canim mach omitz-ienoma in tloque nauaque? what graces has our Lord granted? canim mach ti-nenentinem? (Car.), where on earth are you going? See Can? Canin is also used without interrogation: canim mach nemi noyollo, to reflect; canim mach nemi yyollo, reflected; in canin, where.
2200: Only; çanio mo-chiuaz y (Olm.), only that will be done.
2202: or GANNELPA? adv. Where? nican teopan nino-maquixtico, cannel n-iaz? (Car.), I came to take refuge in the church, where then must I go? cannel or cannelpa t-iazque? where shall we go, where must we go? See Cawpa? RR. can, nel.
2203: (for ca-anoço), adv. It is so, that is exactly it. Rev. canoçotain.
2204: Chubby-cheeked, having fat cheeks. RR. cantli, tepunaçoa.
2205: Chubby-cheeked, having firm, hard cheeks. RR. cantli, tetc.
2206: Cheek. In comp. nocan, my cheek, my cheeks; tocan, our cheeks, cheeks in general.
2209: pret. OGaPan, v. n. To crack, said of the knuckles when one stretches them.
2210: pret. OGapani: nino — to hurt oneself; ic timo-capaniz, you will hurt yourself. nila — to make noise while walking. R. capani. *CAPAQUECHQUEMITL, n. Cloak hood. RR. capa, quechquemutl. *CAPATEPITON, dim. of capa. Travel garment. *GAPATOSCHIUA, pret. OGAPATOSCHIUR: nila — to make shoes. RR. çapatos, chiua. *GAPATOSCHIUALOYAN, n. v. Shoemaker's shop, shoe factory. R. capatoschiua. *GAPATOSCHIUHCAN, n. v. Shoemaker's shop. R. çapatoschiua. *GAPATOSCHIUHQUI, n. v. Shoemaker. R. çapatoschiua. *CAPATOSCOCOPINA, pret. OGAPATOSCOGOPIN: nino — to take off one's shoes. nite — to remove someone's shoes. RR. çapatos, Cocopina. *GAPATOSÇOUAYAN, n. v. Shoemaker's shop, workshop. RR. çapatos, çoa. *GAPATOSTOTOMA, pret. OGAPATOSTOTON: nino — to take off one's shoes. nite — to remove someone's shoes. RR. çapalos, totoma. *CAPATZONTECOMATL, n. Cloak hood. RR. capa, tzontecomatl.
2212: Prunus virginiana (O. y B.), American cherry (capulino).
2214: Town situated between Tetzcuco and Tlaxcala (Clav.). RR. capolin, tlalli, pan.
1654: Pret. OAUITOGAG: nino (for nino) — to apply to oneself a reprimand addressed to everyone in general. RR. atl, uitequi.
1655: Pret. OAUITZOCALAC: n (for ni) — to swim underwater, like the auitzotl. RR. awtzotl, calaqui.
1657: Marsh fly, very common, used as food (Clav.). Its eggs, called ahuauhili, are especially sought after. RR. ail, xayacatl.
1658: Son of Teçoçomoc and Matlalatzin, sixth king of Mexico (Chim., Clav.).
1659: n. A type of sand used for cutting precious stones. RR. atl, æalh.
1661: From the Campine region, now Axapuzco (Aub.). AXAXI! interj. expressing lamentation. Ah! alas!
1663: or AXCAN, adv. Now, today; axcan mo-chiua, current, present; lit. which takes place now; ye no uel axcan, at that same time, it is indeed then, right at that moment; axcan ye tlaco tonatiuh (Par.), now it is already noon; in axcan, now, from this moment, from today onward; in axcan æiuitl, this year, the present year; in axcan ce æiuitl, from now in one year, or next year; in axcan or axcam mochipa, henceforth, in the future; one also says: in axcan in oMpa, or in axcan in o6 ompa lizliui, meaning from now until the moment when we will still be; intl çan axcan, if only now: intla on-iani yohuatzinco Tepotzolan, intla çan axcan amo ye nopan oquiauh? (Par.), if I had gone this morning to Tepotzolan, is it not clear that I would now be wet from the rain? RR. aci, can.
1664: Goods, property, possessions, domain; used almost exclusively in comp.: naæca (for no-axca), my property; maxca (for mo-axca), your property; yaxæca, his/her property; taxca (for to-axca), our property; amaxca (for amo-axca), your (pl.) property; teaxca or uel teaxca, someone's property; ni-teaxca, to belong to another, to belong to someone; cuix ütla teaxca tiqu-elehui? (3. B.), did you desire another's property? atle yaxca, needy, poor; lit. his possessions are nothing; imaxca in nocihuamonhuan (Par.), the property of my daughters-in-law; inin call ca amaxca (Par.), this house is your property. With the postposition pan: naxcapan, over my property, on my domain, within my domain.
1665: Quickly, from now on, immediately; axcampa qualcan, it is the opportune moment; can axcampa, immediately, right away, without delay. RR. axcan, pa.
1668: Possession of property. RR. axcatl, pializtli.
1669: Irregular verb conjugated with the possessives no, mo, y, etc.: naxcali, maxcati, yaxcati, etc. (Olm.), to become wealthy. R. axcatl.
1670: Pret. OAXCATI — nicn (for nicno) — to appropriate something for oneself, to take possession of it; to endow, to give property to someone; nic-axcatia in teaxca in nopiltzin (Par.), I give my son another's property. Niteila or nicte — to put someone in possession of something, to make them master of it. R. axcatl.
1672: dim. of axcar. Savings, small means, small property.
1673: Owner, one who has wealth, who is rich, prosperous, happy; n-axcaua, to have great riches; pl. axcauaque. R. axcaül.
1976: n. of instrument. Fly-whisk, mosquito net, curtain to protect against mosquitoes. R. çayolpeuia.
1977: n. of instrument. Mosquito net, curtain to protect against mosquitoes. RR. çayolin, lzaqua.
1981: adj. and n. Entered, introduced, one who penetrates somewhere; atlan calacqui or calaquini, diver; chimaltillan calacqui, protected, defended by a shield; tetlan calacqui or calaquini, submitted to someone, who places himself among people. R. calaqui.
1982: n. Introduction, action of entering somewhere; tetlan calactiuechiztli, action of placing oneself among people. R. calactiuetai.
1983: pret. OCALACTIUETZ: quickly, to hasten to enter; tetlan or tenepanila ni-calactiuetzi — to enter, to introduce oneself, to place oneself among others, in the midst of others; in yuh chauapapalotl yçan iluiz tleco calactiuetzi (Olm.), as the butterfly throws itself heedlessly into the fire. RR. calaqui, uetzi.
1984: One who places himself, introduces himself; tetlan or tenepantla calactiuetzqui, who throws himself, slips in among others. R. calactiuetzi.
1985: pret. OCALACTIA: nino — to introduce oneself, to go placing oneself, inviting oneself; tetlan ni-calactiuh, to meddle in an affair; lit. to place oneself among people. R. calaqui.
1986: Pret. OGALAN, v. n. To tinkle, to resonate, speaking of metals.
1987: Pret. OCALANI: to burnish, to strike one thing against another.
1988: pret. [somewhere]; to sell, to move (said of merchandise); to disappear, to hide; nitla — to burnish, to polish,
1989: ni — to enter, to penetrate somewhere; to sell, to move, speaking of merchandise; to disappear, to hide; calaqui in tonatiuh, the sun sets; ocalac in tonatiuh, the sun has set; acalco ni-calaqui, to embark; aitic or anepantla ni-calaqui, to enter, to be in the open sea; atlan ni-calaqui, to dive, to sink into the water; tetlan ni-calaqui, to submit to someone, to slip in among people; tepeouican or texcalouican ni-calaqui, to withdraw, to take refuge in the mountains; calaquiz (Olm.), he will enter, let him enter; with the particle on: non-calaqui, to surrender, to submit. nitla — to make enter, to introduce something; otla-calac, he has introduced, inserted something. Rev. calaquia or calaquitzinoa (Par.). Impers. calacoa, one enters; calacoaz (Olm.), one will enter, let them all enter. RR. calli, aqui.
1990: Pret. OCALAQUI: nino — to enter a house, to hire oneself out. nite — to enclose someone; teilpiloyan nite-calaquia, to put someone in prison; tetlan nite-calaquia, to support someone with another; atlan nite-calaquia, to plunge someone into the water; inic çacatla, quauhtla te-calaquia (Olm.), so that he leads people astray, throws them into the fields, the woods. nitla or nontla — to bring back in, to enclose something, to pay tribute; acalco nitla-calaquia, to load something onto a boat; atlan nitla-calaquia, to put, to sink something into the water; ipan nitla-calaquia, to pay a debt by incurring another; itzalan or tzalzalan nic-calaquia, to put something in the middle of others. Rev. of calaqui.
1991: n. Place where one enters, hiding place; tonatiuh ycalaquian, west (the place where the sun sets); tonatiuh ycalaquiam mo-chiua, that which concerns the west; ayamo ycalaquian in calaqui (Olm.), he does not enter where one enters, he goes like a madman. RR. calaqui, yan.
1992: That which concerns the entrance; tonatiuh ycalaquianyotl, that which relates to the west. With the postposition c: ycalaquianyoc, at the entrance or the entrance itself of a house. R. calaquian.
1993: Action of entering somewhere; tetlan calaquiliztli, submission. R. calaqui.
1994: Sentinel, watchman; tochcalco calaquini, ferret, one who enters rabbit holes. Plural calaquinime. See calacqui. R. calaqui.
2155: Soft, tender like a sweet potato.
2156: 5. Sweet potato (camote), edible root of the convolvulus family. CAMPA? adv. Where? from where? in what place? which way? campa mochan? (Par.), where is your dwelling? campa ca in namauh? (Gar.), where is my paper? campa ic timo-huicatz, notlatocatzine? (Par.), where do you come from, my lord? campa nel ou uel noço n-iaz? (Car.), where then must I go? Preceded by in or another expression, campa ceases to be interrogative: amo nicte-ilhuia in campa n-iaznequi (Car.), I do not say where I want to go; nimitz-tlatzacuiltiz intla campa t-iaz (Car.), I will punish you if you go somewhere; çan campa ualla, a foreigner, one who comes from outside. RR. can, pa.
2157: which joins with adj. n.: occampa, yexcampa or excampa, from two, from three places; occampa lite-tlayocoltia (Gar.), we come from two places to serve. Plur. campaixti: occan onech-xixilque, auh miec eztli ioccampaixti oquiz (Car.), they struck me in two places, and much blood flowed from both sides. RR. can, pa.
2159: etc.: nitetla or nicte — to take something from someone in order to eat it. R. campaxoa. CAMPANACHIUHQUE, n. v. Bell-maker.
2160: campana, chiua. CAMPANAYOLLOTLI, n. Bell clapper. RR. campana, yollotli.
2162: campana, pitza. CAMPANATZILINIQUI, n. v. Bell-ringer. RR. campana, tzilinia.
2164: Pret. OCAMPAXO: nite — to bite someone without taking a piece away; nitla — to eat, to swallow something. RR. camatl, pachoa.
2165: Pret. OCAMPAXOTIUETZ: nite — to bite, to snap with the teeth while wrestling with someone. RR. campaxoa, uetzi.
2166: adj. See. Chubby-cheeked. RR. cantli, popotzoa.
2167: Chubby-cheeked, having fat cheeks. RR. cantli, ponazçoa.
2168: Pret. OCAMPOTZO cheeks. RR. cantli, potzoa.
2169: One who puffs out their cheeks. R. campotzoa.
2170: Puffing out of the cheeks. R. campotzoa. ni — to puff out the CAN? adv. Where? can mochan? where is your house? Loc. adv. can cuix or cuixpan? where? which way? in what place? cam mach or can mach? where? how? is it possible? cam mach in tixtepetla? are you then so blind? cam mach omitz-icnoma in tloque in nauaque? how have you deserved the kindness that God has granted you? cam mach mocnopil? cam mach momaceual? from where does so much good come to you? can mach nocnopil, can mach nomaceual inic nopan timo-calaquico? (Gar.), how have I deserved that you come to my house? can mach ü-nenemi, in aocmo timo-tlalia? (Car.), where are you going, that you do not stop? can or ca yepa? in what place? where? can yeuel? toward which place? can yuhya or yuhyan? at what time? at what period? when? Preceded by in, can ceases to be interrogative: amo nite-ilhuia in can n-iaznequi (Gar.), I do not say where I want to go. It is often followed by ic or in, see CANIX.
2171: marking place, time, or part. Joins with nouns and adj.: tlaxcalchiuhcan, bakery; qualcan, at a propitious, favorable time; miecan, in many places or parts; with adj. n.: ceccan, in one place; occan, in two places or in two parts; nauhcan xeliuhtica (Car.), it is divided into four parts; macuilpoalcan, in a hundred parts. Plur. canixti: ioccanirti, in two places. Serves as a suffix to many place names: Amaquemecan, Colhuacan, Michuacan, etc.
2172: Only. More exclusive than ça, it is written cam before an n or a p: çam moyollo or moyollotzin tlama, voluntarily, freely, at your pleasure, as you wish; çam pinauizcopa, by shame only, out of shame; before a c the letter n changes to z: çaz ce, one only; çan icel (Olm.), he alone; cenca çan cana, in very few places or parts; çan mach, camache or çan mache, nothing but: çan mach an-tlatoa (Par.), you do nothing but talk; çamache nic-mattica in choquiztli, in tlaocoyoaliztli, I do nothing but weep; lit. I know only tears, sadness.
2173: conj. But; çan ye, but quickly; çan ye iz ti-uitz, but come here, hurry; çan intla, but if.
2174: Somewhere; cuix cana otiqu-ittac in nopiltzin? have you seen my son somewhere? yehua oniqu-ittac in ompa teopan, aço ye cana iz huitz (Gar.), I just saw him at the church, perhaps he is making his way here; cana nite-tlalia, to put a person somewhere; canapa onia, to be absent; çan canapa ualla, a foreigner, come from outside. Accompanied by a numeral, cana means approximately, about: amo uel niqu-ilnamiqui, aço tel cana matlacpa (Car.), I do not remember well, it is perhaps about ten times.
1795: Sticky, gluey, viscous; çagçalic path, a drink or beverage to facilitate childbirth. R. çaliui.
1796: freq. of çanIuuyanTLi. Joints, articulations of the bones. In comp. toçaçaliuhyan, our joints, joints in general.
1797: n. Shell, husk, casing, any kind of envelope or covering. In comp. icacallo, its shell; icacallo in totoltetl, eggshell.
1798: (Clav.). Territory set aside for war provisions. With the postposition pan: cacalomilpan (Bét.), on the supply fields. RR. ... (?), milh.
1799: (Clav.). Raven's stone, black stone. RR. cacaloil, tell.
1800: Raven; tongs for snuffing candles, or for eating grains of maize cooked on embers.
1801: n. (Clav.). Raven's flower, which is white, red, yellow, and very fragrant.
1805: son and successor of Neçahualpill in the state of Tetzeuco (Chim.). Cortès had him put to death. R. cacamatl.
1806: Sad, gloomy, pale, wan, haggard. R. cacamaua.
1808: : #i — to yawn, to open one's mouth frequently.
1810: : tto — to exchange taunts or jokes with one another. nite- — to tell jokes to someone.
1814: Small ear of maize growing beside the main ear.
2015: Pret. OGALCUICHOLOLO: ni — to sweep a chimney. RR. calcuichtli, ololoa.
2016: Pret. OCALCUICHPOPOUR: to sweep a chimney. RR. calcuichtli, popoa.
2017: Pret. OCALGUICHTEPEUH: Ni — to sweep a chimney. RR. calcuichtli, tepeua.
2018: Pret. OCALOUICHTLAZ: to sweep a chimney, to remove soot. RR. calcuichtli, tlaça.
2020: Secret door, back door. RR. calli, cuitlacoyonia.
2021: One who has a house, master, head of household, inhabitant of a locality;
2022: = Quauhtinchan calle (Olm.), inhabitant of Quauhtinchan. Plural calque. R. calli.
2023: Neighbor, house neighbor. In comp. nocalecapo, my neighbor, master of a house like me; tecalecapo, someone's neighbor. RR. cale, poth.
2024: (Olm.). Master, owner of a house. R. calli.
2025: Pret. OCALHUI: nino — to cook, to eat maize cooked in ashes. R. calli.
2026: Pret. OGALHUI: something belonging to someone; to graft their trees. R. caliui.
2029: Furnishings, household furniture, provisions. RR. calli, tlatlaquitl.

2031: Pret. oçALIUH, v. To attach oneself, to fix, to stick, to adhere to something, speaking of objects.
2032: Sticky, viscous; tetech caliuini, one who attaches, sticks to someone. R. çaliui.
2396: Every night; cecenyoal in ni-temiqui (Car.), every night I dream. RR. cecen, yoall.
2397: Pret. OGEGENTELHUI: nino — to play with flowers as with balls.
2398: freq. of oser. Each, to each one; cecentetl totlatol, each opinion, each one of our opinions.
2400: Slowly, step by step, at each step. RR. cecen, icæitl.
2401: In each quarter. RR. cecen, tlayacati, pan.

2403: Apart, separately, by the handful, etc. RR. cecentlamantli, ca.
2404: Pret. OCECENTLAMANTILI: nitla — to distinguish, separate, divide motives, reasons, etc. R. cecentlamantlhi.
2405: Plural of the following, used adverbially. By the handful, in handfuls.
2406: freq. of GENTLAMANTLI. Each thing; cecentlamantli iyeliz nonqua quiçaliztli, a kind of each thing (genero de qualquier cosa). Plural cecentlamantin. RR. cecen, tlamantli.
2407: freq. of cexrcapxz. On one side, each side of a person; mochtin cecentlapal tlana- cazlectin, ma æiqu-itili izcan iz cecentlapal innacaz, cuix ma oc oncaca? (Car.), all have the ear cut on one side, look at their ear on one side, do they still have it?
2408: Son of Axayacatl, king of Tenochtitlan (Clav.).
2409: Pret. OGEGEPANO, freq. of cePANoA: nic — to gather, to bring together motives, reasons, etc.; nic-cecepanoa in tlatoll, to add words.
2410: adj. Very cold, icy; fig. horrible, dreadful, causing trembling. R. cepayaui.
2411: Pret. OcEGEPoAc, freq. of cepoa: ni — to have a part of the body numb, to faint; noma cecepoa, my hand is numb.
2412: CEGEPOCTICG or CECEPOUHQUI, adj. v. Numbed, drowsy, in a faint. R. cecepoa.
2413: Pret. OGECEPOGATIQUIZ: to be suddenly frightened. RR. cecepoa, quiça.
2414: Sudden fright, sudden disturbance. R. cecepocatiquiça.
2055: General of the troops of Prince Maxixcatzin, who distinguished himself in the battle of Otompan fighting for the Spanish (Clav.).
2056: Prolix words, with great circumlocutions; expression used to designate the speeches and fictions of the ancient Mexicans (palabras dichas en corredores largos, e Lomase por los dichos y ficciones de los viejos antiguos). RR... (?), tlatolli.
2057: Large, long hall, corridor. RR. calli, melactli.
2058: Land, field that borders or surrounds a house. RR. calli, milli.

2060: Rooftop, attic, top floor of a house. RR. calli, nepanolli.
2061: High, long hall. RR. calnepanolli, melactli.
2062: Property, public domain. RR. calli, neuianth.
2063: pret. OCALNONOTZ: to confer, to come to an agreement in a matter, a lawsuit, etc. nino — to come to an agreement...
2065: pret. OGALO: to stop walking, to stay in a place, to settle, to attach oneself, to stick, to adhere. nino or nonno — to stop, to attach oneself, to cling. nite — to detain someone, to put back, to set bones; in te-çaloa ipalnemoaloni (Olm.), God governs people. nitla or nic — to glue something, to build a wall, to solder, etc., to catch oneself somewhere while falling; cavallo itlaquayan nic-çaloa, to build a stable; iznacancuitlatica nitla-çaloa, to glue, to make a thing adhere with glue.
2066: Cocoon, shell of a silkworm, etc.
2068: Road, path leading to a house. RR. calli, otli.
2069: House full of twists and turns, where it is easy to get lost. RR. calli, ouican.
2070: Gentleman, nobleman. RR. calli, pan, pilli.
2072: See Cazu. — Name of an ancient province (Bét.).
2073: pret. OGALPANO: nic — to go from house to house. RR. calli, panoa.
2074: pret. OGALPANUI: nite or nitla — to go from house to house for the distribution or collection of taxes, etc. RR. calli, panua.
2416: Articulations, knuckles of the fingers. In comp. tocecepoc, our joints, the finger joints in general. R. cecepoa.
2417: Pret. OCECEPOUALTITICATCA: tito — to act foolishly to no purpose (pausanes estar hechos sin hazer cosa de provecho). RR. cecepoa, ca.
2418: Pret. OCEGEPOUATICATCA: ni — same meaning. RR. cecepoa, ca.
2421: Pret. oceceur, freq. of cEuIA: nino — to rest a moment, to calm oneself. Mite — to intervene, to reconcile, to make peace, to bring concord. Nitla — to cool, to soothe, to calm; yaotica nitla-cecewa, to subdue, to pacify a country by force of arms.
2422: Pret. OCECEUILI: minotla — to come to an understanding, to reach agreement, to be reconciled. Mtetla — to calm someone who is angered, to appease those who are quarreling. R. ceceua.
2423: Every year or from year to year. RR. cecexiuitl, ca.
2424: Used adverbially. Every year. RR. cecen, xiuitl.
2425: A unit of length, a fathom; fig. superior, protector (Olm.). RR. ce, ciyacatl.
2427: Frozen with cold, numb, dying of cold, shivering. R. cecmiqui.
2428: Pret. OCEGMICTIGATCA: ni — to be trembling with cold. RR. cecmiqui, ca.
2429: Pret. OCECMIC: Impersonal. ceemicoa, one dies of cold, all tremble with cold. RR. cetl, miqui.
2431: Seized by cold, numb, dying of cold. R. cecmiqui.
2432: Elsewhere, apart, on another side; ceci ienoxacalco omo-tlacatih in totemaquixticatzin ni — to die of cold. (Car.), in a poor hut our Savior was born; cecni yauh itozqui, out of tune, having lost the pitch; lit. his voice goes to another side; cecni icac, mani, quiça où tlachia, different, particular: cecni mani amall, register, notebook; oc cecni or oc no ceci, to another place: cecni in oquichtli, thuan oc cecni in cihuatl (Pax.), the man will be in one place and the woman in another; oc cecni icac, quite another thing, very different.
2433: v. Part, fragment. In comp. icecniquiçalz, his/her part; icecniquigaliz tonacayo, member, part of the body. RR. ceeni, quiça.
2315: Pret. OCAXAUILI: to reduce, to lighten someone's burden, to diminish nitla— or nicte— the tax, the debt, etc. R. caxaua.
2316: Bird of the starling family (Hern.). RR. ..... (?), tototl.

2318: (Olm., Par.). One who has a vessel, a bowl. R. caxitl.
2319: Bowl, dish, cup, crockery; pl. caztin or caxme (Olm.). In comp. nocax, my bowl.
2320: n. v. Sideboard; lit. place where crockery is set down. RR. caxitl, mana.

2330: Cock, hen (gallo ó gallina de Castilla). CAXTILTECATL, n. Spaniard, Castilian; pl. Caxtilteca; intlamachtilhuan in Caxtilteca (Par.), the disciples of the Spaniards.
2333: n. Three hundred (cloaks, tortillas); lit. fifteen score. RR. caxtolli, ipilhuitl.
2095: 5. Surface of a wall, exterior of a house. With the postposition pan: caltempan, at the exterior of the house, at the door; tecaltempan, at the door, on the threshold, at the entrance of someone's house. RR. calli, tentli.
2096: Foundation of a house. RR. calli, tetzonth.
2097: Pret. OCALTI: nino or nicno — to have a house built for oneself, to have a house constructed; nic-nequi in ma nino-caltia (Par.), I want to have a house built for myself; nicno-caltia in mocal (Car.), I take possession of or I inhabit your house. Nite or nic — to build a house for someone, to provide one for them; nic-caltia in noyacapan (Par.), I provide a house for my eldest son; onech-calti in notatzin (Car.), my father provided me with a house. R. calli.
2099: Pret. OCALTOCAC: nite — to go from house to house. RR. calli, toca.
2100: dim. of calli. Small house, retreat, closet, etc.
2102: Street; lit. space between houses. RR. calli, tzalanth.

2104: n. Hamlet, group of houses. R. caltzaqua.
2105: king of Itzcahuacan, at the beginning of the 14th century (Chim.).
2106: king of Michoacan, who submitted to Cortés.
2107: Floorboard for a house; nic-tlalia in calhuapalitl, to floor a room. RR. calli, huapalitl.
2108: Pret. OCAMACAUH: nic — to let slip some word one would not have wished to have said.

2112: The action of opening the mouth extremely wide. R. camachaloa.
2113: Pret. OCAMAGHALOTINEN: mo — to walk with the mouth open. RR. camachaloa, nenu.
2114: Maxillary bone, jaw. RR. camachalli, quauhyotl.
2135: Stench, bad breath from the mouth. RR. camatl, potonilizth.
2136: Palate, interior of the mouth. In comp. nocamatapal, my palate; tocamatapal, our palate, the palate in general. RR. camatl, tapalli (?).
2137: Pret. OCAMATECAPANI: nino — to strike oneself on the mouth, on the cheeks. nite — to strike someone on the mouth, on the cheeks. RR. camatetl, capania. nino — to close the |
2139: Fat cheeks. In comp. nocamateuh, my cheeks; tocamateuh, our cheeks, the cheeks in general. RR. camatl, tetl.
2140: Mouth, lips, palate, cheek, face. In comp. nocam, my mouth. With the postpositions c, pan: nocamac, at my palate or even my mouth, my palate (Olm., Car.); nocamac micmati, to taste, to savor something; tocamac or tocamapan, in our mouth, at the mouth in general.
2142: Pret. OCAMATLAPOUE: nino — to open one's mouth. RR. camatl, tlapoa.
2143: Pret. OCAMATLATI: to mark someone on the face. RR. camatl, tlatia.
2144: Down, fine hair of the lips. RR. camatl, tochomiotl.
2145: Pret. OCAMATZAYAN: nite — to break, to split someone's jaw, to open their mouth to the extreme. nite — to burn. RR. camatl, tzayana.

2147: Pret. OCAMAUAC, v. n. To turn yellow, to ripen, speaking of maize; to be almost ripe.
2149: (Clav., Bét.). Name under which the Tlaxcalans venerated the god Huitzlopochth.
2150: Brown, taking on color or beginning to ripen. R. camileua.
2151: Pret. OCAMILEUAC, v. n. To ripen, to take on color, speaking of fruits.
2152: Pret. OCAMILIUN, v. n. To begin to ripen, to take on color, speaking of fruits. CAMISACOPINA, Pret. OCAMISATI: nino — to put on one's shirt. nite — to put a shirt on someone. R. camisa.
2153: Brown, dark violet. RR. camotli (?), pa.
2154: Pret. OCAMOPАЛTI, v. n. To become brown. R. camopalli.
2116: pret. OCAMACHICHIAC or OCAMACHICMIX: wi — to have a bitter taste in the mouth. RR. camatl, mi — to open, chachia.
2117: Bitterness of the mouth. R. camachichia.
2118: One who has a large mouth. RR. camatl, coyauac.
2119: Tender, fresh, not yet ripe nor completely dry, half-ripe, etc. R. camaua.
2120: n. odor of the mouth. RR. camatl, yyayalizth. *CAMAIXQUEMITL, n. Bed covering. RR. cama, irquemitl. Stench, bad smell. *CAMAIXXOTL, n. Bed covering. RR. cama, ixæotl.
2121: pret. OCAMANALCHIUH: nitla — to do something as a joke, in jest. RR. camanalli, chua.
2122: pret. OCAMANALHUI: to joke with someone, to make jokes, to laugh; tocamanalhua, pret. otocacamanalhuique, to exchange jokes with one another. R. camanalli.
2123: pret. OCAMANALLITO: ni — to tell jokes, to amuse oneself by laughing. RR. camanalli, itoa.
2124: pret. OCAMANALLATO: ni — to tell jokes. RR. camanalli, tlatoa.
2126: Jest, mockery; aço çan camanalli, tentlamachili ipan otic-ma? perhaps you saw in it only a subject for jest and derision?
2127: pret. OCAMANALO: ni — to tell jokes, to laugh. R. camanalli.
2128: n. v. Joker, jester. R. camanaloa.
