2284: pret. OGAUALOTIQUIZ: n — to be left behind, abandoned. RR. caua, quiça.
2285: pret. OCAUALOTIA: ni — to inherit; ytech caualotiuh, heir, the one to whom something is left or bequeathed. R. caua.
2286: pret. OCAUALTI — to abstain, to deprive oneself of doing something, to repel, to banish from one's thoughts. Mitetla or nicte — to reprimand, to restrain someone, to prevent or dissuade them from doing something. R. caua. : ninotla or nieno —
2287: pret. OCAUAN: # — to distinguish oneself, to make a name for oneself, to leave a lasting memory. R. caua.
2288: Or GAHUANI, n. v. One who fasts; pl. çauani or çauanime (Par.). With the reflexive pronouns nino, timo, mo, etc.: nino-çauam, timo-çcauani, etc., I am, you are a faster, etc. R. çaua.
2289: Scab, mange, smallpox; mo-miquillique in çahuatl (Chim.), they died of smallpox; uey çauatl (Gom.), great leprosy;
2290: tecpatl xihuitl, 1520, ipan in mo-manaco in uey cahuatl, ic micohuac (Chim.), in the year 2 flint, the smallpox spread, which was very deadly.
2292: pret. OCAUHTEUAC: mino — to make one's will, to make one's testament, to stop. Nice — to leave, to quit someone or something and depart. RR. caua, eua.
2293: pret. OCAUHTICATCA — set apart from someone; ueca nic-cauhtica, to be very far from someone; to be spacious, extensive, speaking of a place. RR. caua, ca.
2294: pret. OGAUHTIMANCA, v. n. To be extended, vast, spacious, speaking of a place. RR. caua, mani.
3038: Pret. OGHIAMAPATZGAG: #ù — to make chia oil. RR. chiamatl, patzca.
3039: Maker of chia oil. R. chamapatzca.
3041: Pret. OGHIAMAUII: nitla — to rub something with chia oil. R. chiamatl.
3042: Town near Quauhnahuac (Bét.). RR. chian, mil, pan.
3047: Bird that has the color of the chia seed and lives mainly in the vicinity of Tetzceuco (Hern.). RR. chian, totoil.
3048: Plant used as a remedy against fevers, dysentery, and other illnesses (Clav.). RR. chian, .... (?).
3049: (Hern., Clav.). A very nutritious, pleasant, and highly esteemed beverage. RR. chiantzotzolh, atolli.
3050: River tributary to the Gulf of Mexico, today called Tabasco (Clav.).
3051: Chiapanec, inhabitant of the province of Teochiapan; pl. Chiapaneca (Clav.). R. Chiapan.
3052: Pret. OGHIAUAG, v. n. To become stained, speaking of a thing. Mitla — to stain something, to spill oil on it. R. chia.
3053: Greasy, stained with oil. R. chaua.
3054: n. Grease, oil, stain; *almendras chiauacayotl, almond oil; *nuezes chiauacayotl, walnut oil. In comp. ichiauaca, its oil; quaquaue ichiauaca, beef fat. R. chiaua.
3057: A species of viper. RR. chauitl, coatl.
3198: v. — to make sparks fly (sparks, wood chips, etc.).
3199: Wet nurse who breastfeeds. R. chichi.
3200: Master of the dog (Olm.). R. chichi.
3201: pret. OCHICHIUH, freq. of civa: nino — to adorn oneself, to make oneself beautiful, etc. nite — to adorn, to ornament someone. nitla or nic — to ornament, arrange, well dispose a thing; in chichiua ützcuinton qui-chichiua (Av.), the master of the little dog cleans it, grooms it; nütlatla-chichiua, to do something frequently.
3202: Woman who has large breasts. RR. chichiualli, apilolh.
3203: n. Whey; lit. water of milk. RR. chichiualayotl, atl.
3205: n. of instrument. Vessel for thickening milk; chichiualayotetzaualoni comutl, a bowl, a type of earthenware vessel for thickening milk.
3207: Milk; tlatetzaualh or tlatetzauhili chichiualayotl, cheese, curdled milk. RR. chichiualli, ayotl.
3208: n. Cheese; yancuic chichiualayotlatetzauhtli, fresh cheese, newly made. RR. chichiualayotl, tlatetzauhtli.
3209: Woman who has large breasts. RR. chichiuall, atecomail.
3210: pret. OCHICHIUALCAUALTI: nite — to wean a child. RR. chichiuall, caualtia.
3211: Nipple, teat, the small tip of the breasts. RR. chichiualli, yacatl.
3212: Nipple, the small tip of the breasts. RR. chichiualli, yacauitzth.
3214: Breast, bosom. In comp. ichichiual, his/her breast, his/her breasts; tochichiual, our breast, the breast in general. R. chichi.
3215: Woman who has large breasts. RR. chichiualli, mecapalli.
3216: (Hern.). Extremely milky medicinal plant. RR. chichiualli, meya.
3118: Delirium, frenzy, disordered speech. RR. chichico, tlatoliztli.
3120: Pret. OCHICHICTILI: nitla— to stain, soil, blacken something. R. chichicau.
3121: freq. of chichicazpanqui. Made, composed of various metals (ametalado).
3122: n. v. Stain, blackness, filth, blemish. R. chichicaui.
3123: n. freq. of chichicuey. Eight by eight, or every eight. With the postp. can and pa: chichicueexcan, in each eight parts; chichicueexpa, every eight times; oc chichicueexpa, eight other times, every eight times.
3124: Every eight days. RR. chichicueey, ilhuitl, ca.
3125: n. freq. of chichicuey. Eight by eight.
3129: or CHICHIYUTL, n. Canine or pertaining to dogs, that which concerns dogs. R. chich.
3130: Red or violet savory. RR. chichiltic, uauhtli.
3131: Pret. OCHICHILI: nitla— to make something sour, bitter; to venture, to take a risk. R. chichia.
3133: Pret. to be colored, speaking of a thing.
3137: Pret. OCHICHILO: red, vermilion object, etc. nitla— to render something red.
3259: "in the seven caves" — a famous place reached by the Aztecs after leaving Uei Colhuacan, where they divided into several tribes (Clav.). RR. chicome, oztotl, c.
3260: n. Seven rows, files, lines, furrows, etc. RR. chicome, tepantli.
3261: adj. n. used to count animate beings and flat, thin objects. One hundred and forty; lit. seven-twenties; chicompoalli once, one hundred and forty-one; chicompoalli oncaxtolli, one hundred and fifty-five; etc. RR. chicome, poalli.
3264: The uttermost and deepest place of hell; lit. ninth hell. RR. chiconaui, mictlan.
3267: n. Nine rows, lines, files, furrows, etc. RR. chiconaui, tepantli.
3268: n. used to count animate beings and flat, thin objects. One hundred and eighty; lit. nine-twenties; chiconauhpoalli ommatlactli, one hundred and ninety. Also written chiconauppoalli. RR. chiconau, poalli.
3269: adj. n. used to count round, thick objects. Nine; oc chiconauhtetl, nine others; ic chiconauhtetl, ninth; inic chiconauhtetl, the ninth; also written chiucnauhtetl. Plur. chiconauhteaixtin; in chiconauhteixtin, all nine. RR. chiconaui, tetl.
3271: Market, fair that recurs every nine days. RR. chiconaui, tianquiliztli.
3272: n. Nine equal or different things, parts, pairs. RR. chiconaui, tlamantli.
3273: n. used to count animate beings and flat, thin objects. Nine; lit. five-four; oc chiconaui, nine others; ic chiconaui, ninth; inic chiconaui, the ninth: also written chiucnaui. Plur. chiconauixtin; in chiconauixtin, all nine. In comp. chiconauh: chiconauhxiuhitl, nine years; with the postpositions can and pa: chiconauhcan, in nine parts or portions; oc chiconauhcan, in nine other parts; chiconappa or chicunappa, nine times; oc chiconappa, nine other times; chiconappa ixquich, nine times as much; chiconappa chiconaui, nine times nine.
3275: In nine days, during nine days. RR. chiconauilhuitl, ca.
3277: or CHICUNAUIPILLI, adj. n. One hundred and eighty (cloaks, loaves, etc.); lit. nine score. RR. chiconaui, ipilli.
3078: : mite — to love someone extremely; nimitz-chicauacatlagotla, I love him/her greatly. RR. chicauaca, tlaçotla.
3079: Pret. OCHICAUACATLALI : nitla — to raise, construct, establish, place a building solidly. RR. chicauaca, tlaha.
3081: : M — to believe firmly, to have a strong faith. Pret. OCHICAUACATLAGO- RR. chicauaca, neltoca.
3082: Solid and steadfast faith. R. chicauacatlaneltoca.
3084: With courage, firmness; ayollo chicaualiztica, with cowardice, without courage, without strength of soul. RR. chicaualiztli, ca.
3085: Strength, firmness, effort, courage, old age; ayollo chicaualizth, cowardice, weakness, inconstancy. R. chicaua.
3086: Firm, stable, permanent, that does not vary. RR. chicaua, ca.
3087: Pret. OCHICCALO : nonte — to forcibly remove someone from a house, to send them away violently. RR. chicaua, caloa (?).
3088: Pret. OCHICCANAUX : nite — to send someone away violently, to forcibly throw them out. RR. chicaua, canaua.
3089: (Clav.). A type of wine extracted from the stalk and grain of maize.
3090: Pret. OCHICHAC : to spit on someone. Nitla or nite — to spit. Nite — to expectorate, to produce heavy spittle. See cnica.
3091: n. (Olm.). Master of the dog. R. chichi.
3093: Pret. OCHICUIC : ni or nite — to suckle; oc chicha piltonili, child at the breast, still nursing.
3094: Pret. OCHICHIC : nitla — to mend clothing, shoes; to sew, to darn.
3096: Pret. OCHICHIAC or OCHICHIX : M — to render something sour, bitter; ayamo chichia octli, sweet wine, wine that is not strong. Impers. tlachichia: nocamac tlachichia (Gar.), my mouth is bitter.
3097: Pret. OCHICHIX, freq. of coma: to watch, to observe someone with the intent to harm them. Nitla — to await the orders of one's nite — master, of one's chief.
3059: Greasy, stained with oil. R. chiauiçotl.
3061: 8. Viper, wireworm, caterpillar that gnaws plants.
3062: 1. OCHIAUIZAPATZCAC to extract watery blood. RR. chiauizatl, patzca.
3063: pret. OCHIAUIZAQUIXTI: M — same meaning. RR. chiauizatl, quixtia. Rendered fat.
3067: pret. ONIGHICAC: — to spit; one-chicac (for onech-chicac) (Par.), he spat on me. See cica.
3068: Firm, solid, strong, old. R. cheaua.
3069: adj. Thorny, having thorns. R. chcalotl.
3070: Thorny herb, thorn; also called chichicalotl and used as a remedy (Hern.). With the postposition tlan: chicalotitlan, in the midst of thorns.
3071: Place full of thorns. RR. chica-lotl, tla.
3072: pret. OCHICAUAC: to gain strength, to grow old, to age; pan mi-chicaua in tlauelilocayotl, to persevere, to persist in evil, in vice; ayamo chicaua oi, sweet wine, which is not yet very strong; ochicauac, it is ripe, having reached maturity. nino— to strive, to rouse oneself, to apply oneself; ipan nino-chicaua, to care for, to watch over, to apply oneself to something; tetech nino-chicaua, to lean upon, to rely on the credit of a powerful person; itech nino-chicaua, to lean on something, to count on it. nite— to help, support, encourage, strengthen, urge someone; pan nite-chicaua in aqualli, to harden someone in evil. nitla- or nice— to strengthen, to confirm, to prove something. Impers. nechicaualo (Olm.), one strives.
3073: Strong, firm, solid, courageous, advanced in age, old; ye chicauac, old, ancient; chicauac nacaayotl, strong broth; amo or aocmo chicauac, weak, thin, without strength, slack; niman amo chicauac, feeble, sick, unsteady; amo chicauac oclli, spoiled wine, without strength, flat; amo yollo chicauac, without courage, without energy, inconstant. Plural chicauaque. R. chicaua.
3074: Firmly, strongly, extremely; chicauaca tellatzacuiltiani or tlatzontequini, a great judge. R. chicauac.
3075: pret. OCHICAUACATCA: to be firm, solid. RR. chicaua, ca.
3076: Support, strength. In comp. ychicauaca, his support; tepantli ychicauaca, buttress, support of a wall; lit. of the wall its support. R. chicauac.
3099: Bitter; cenca chichic, very bitter; fig. nicte-ixpiquiltia in cococ, in chichic (Olm.), to set people against one another, to push them to destroy each other. R. chichia.
3100: Bitterness (of bile, aloe, etc.). R. chichic.
3102: Sowthistle, an herbaceous plant. RR. chichicatl, quiitl.
3103: Bile, gall; nicte-ytia in chichicatl (Olm.), I chastise harshly; lit. I make someone drink bile. In comp. techichicauh, someone's bile; tochichicauh, our bile, bile in general; nimochichicauh (Olm.), I am your bile, meaning you treat me as your son.
3104: Stained, blackened, dirtied, spotted, soiled. R. chichicaur.
3105: Pret. OCHICHICAUH, v. to blacken oneself, to become spoiled, speaking of paper (passarse n. to become stained, el papel).
3106: Or CHICHICAUIZTLI, n. v. Stain, blackness. R. chichicau.
3107: Pret. ocHicnionc, freq. of ouioui: nitla— to mend something, such as linen, footwear, etc.
3109: freq. of euico. From one side to the other; chichico yauh inon tlahuanqui (Par.), that drunkard goes from one side to the other.
3110: Pret. OCHICHICO: nitla— to stain, to dirty, to blacken, to spot something.
3111: Or CHICHICUNANAUT, adj. n. freq. of enrconaur. Nine by nine. With the postp. can and pa: chichicunanauhcan, in each nine parts; chichicunanappa, every nine times; oc chichicunanappa, nine other times, every nine times.
3112: n. freq. of cmico-NAUuTErL. Nine by nine.
3113: n. freq. of cniconaur. Nine by nine. With the postp. can and pa: chichiconauhcan, in each nine parts; chichiconappa, every nine times; oc chichiconappa, nine other times.
3114: n. freq. of enrcows. Seven by seven, or every seven. With the postp. can and pa: chichicooccan, in each seven parts or places; chichicooppa or chichicuuppa, every seven times; oc chichi-cooppa, seven other times, seven more times.
3115: Every seven days. RR. chichicoome, ilhuitl, ca.
3116: n. freq. of cHiconrers. Seven by seven.
3479: Small goldfinch; fig. a tattler, troublemaker. This term is often joined to nila or nic — to rasp, scrape. n. Great crier, which word maquizcoatl, two-headed serpent: chiquimolin maquizcoatl mo-chiua, he is a tattler, slanderer; ma yuhqui timaquizcoatl, tichiquimolin timo-chiuhtinen (Olm.), do not be a troublemaker.
3480: pret. OCHIQUIMOLO: nitla— to make notches in wood or in stone, to scrape, to hollow out something. RR. chiqui, oloa (?).
3481: pret. to make baskets, hampers. RR. chiquiuitl,
3483: Maker of baskets, of hampers. R. chiquiuhchua.
3484: dim. of chiquiuitl. Small basket, small hamper.
3485: Large reed basket. RR. chiquiuitl, topilli.
3486: pret. OCHIQUIUI: nitla— to carry something in a basket, a bag, a hamper, etc.; to strain, to pass something through a basket. R. chiquiuitl.
3487: n. in comp. nochiquiuh, my basket.
3488: Small net for carrying food while traveling, or for fishing (Bét.).
3489: pret. OCHITECOLHUI: to bend, to curve something for someone, such as a basket, hamper, bag. In nitetla— a wand or similar thing. R. chitecoloa.
3490: pret. OCHITECOLO: nitla— to make wooden hoops or similar things. RR....(?), coloa.
3491: pret. OCHITECUIN, v. n. To ignite, to shine, to throw off sparks, speaking of fire.
3492: pret. OCHITENCOLO: to limp; freq. chitencololoa. RR....(?), coloa.
3493: pret. OCITON, v. n. To jump, speaking of a splinter of wood or a bead one wishes to thread; to shine, speaking of fire or light.
3494: pret. OCHITONI: nitla or nic— to allow oneself some word to the detriment of someone, to let slip some word inadvertently; to make jump the bead one wishes to thread, wood splinters, etc.; to gain, to realize a profit in a sale. R. chitoni.
3495: pret. OCHITONTIQUIZ: ni— to leap, to jump quickly to do something or to flee; ochiton-tiquiz, he began to run. RR. chitoni, quiça.
3496: n. v. Action of one who rises hastily to do something, or to take flight, etc. R. chitontiquiça.
3497: Twisted, curved, bent, laid down. R. chiteliui.
3138: Worm, bait, or worms found in the human body. RR.... (?), ocuilin.
3139: See OCHICHILTIAC: red, to turn red. R. chichiloa.
3140: Red, vermilion; chichiltic alo, large red parrot; chichiltic pepetzca, silk fabric, satin, shot-color (iridescent); the same meaning is expressed by: chichiltic seda tilmatli; chichiltic tepuztli, — to become copper; lit. reddish-brown, yellow metal; chichiltic tepuzquixtiloyan or tepuzoztotl, copper mine. R. chichiloa.
3142: That which concerns the Chichimecs; cuicatl chichimecayotl (Par.), Chichimec song. R. chichimecatl.
3145: n. (Hern.). Medicinal plant also called yamancapatli, on account of the mild flavor of its root. Mixed with another plant called cocozth, it was used to prepare an excellent purgative. RR. chichimecatl, patli.
3146: n. (Torq., Clav.). "One who sucks or nurses" — Chichimec; pl. Chichimeca, nomadic tribes living off the products of the hunt, who replaced the Toltecs in Anahuac toward the end of the 12th century. The Spanish conquerors designated by the name Chichimecs the savage peoples who lived in northern Mexico. R. chich.
3147: Or CHICHIMECATEUCTLI (Clav.). One of the military chiefs of the army of Tlaxcala, who fought against Cortés.
3148: Country of Tenayocan where the census of the Chichimecs took place (Clav.). See Neponvazco. RR. chichimecatl, tlalli.
3149: pret. OCHICHIN: nite — to strip someone little by little; said especially of sorcerers who abuse their supposed power. Nitla or nic — to suck something, to breathe in odors; to become saturated, speaking of a sponge or a cask. R. chichi.
3150: pret. OCUICUINACAC, freq. of chichinaca: ni — to suffer, to feel pain, distress; to smart, to sting, speaking of a wound; noyollo chichinaca, I have a pain in my heart, I feel sorrow. Nite — to torment, afflict, torture someone.
3151: Suffering, one who has a very painful wound. R. chichinaca.
3152: Used in comp. nochichinacaca, my pain, my sorrow. R. chichinaca.
3153: Pret. OCHICHINACAPOLO: nite — to afflict someone. RR. chichinaca, poloa.
3154: Pret. OCHICHINACATINEN: — to be tormented, afflicted, to live in grief, sorrow, anxiety, etc. RR. chichinaca, nemi.
3156: : nitla — to burn someone's fields, their harvests.
3157: (Torq.). The act of sucking. R. chichina.
3498: Pret. OCHITTOLIUH, See 1. To twist, to bend, to fold.
3499: Pret. ocmiTroLo: nitla — to make circles or something similar.
3500: Pret. ocuiun: nino — to afflict oneself, to torment oneself; to arrive, to occur, to happen, to be done, etc.; amo ic quen nino-chiua, not to torment oneself about what happens, not to make much of it; nitlazcaluilh onino-chiuh (Olm.), I made myself, I became a disciple; tepan nino-chiua, to imitate someone, to play a role; uel nino-chiua, to succeed, to be happy; nopam mo-chiua or itla nopam mo-chiua, something happens to me or takes place in my time; itla tetequipacho tetolini nopan omo-chiuh, something unpleasant and painful has happened to me; ma itla mopan mo-chiuh (Car.), may nothing happen to you; cenca ic quen mo-chiua in noyollo, my heart is troubled, tormented, disagreeably affected; amo oui mo-chiua, to be done easily, without trouble; tepam mo-chiuh, it happened in the time when certain people were alive; itech tla mo-chiua, to bear fruit, to be fertile; cenca itech tla mo-chiua qualli tlall, good land that produces abundantly; tuhquimma tetl yuinti, quauill yuinti mo-chiua, (the crowd) stirs, presses forward, quarreling; wei apan mo-chiua, which concerns the sea, which takes place at sea; tepepam mo-chiua, which takes place on a high point; uelitim mo-chiuaz, to be fitting (compadecerse algo). Rev. chiuhtzinoa: oquitzintli omo-chiuhtzino in totemaquixticatzin (Par.), our Savior became man. Nite — to beget, to produce, to bring forth, to create; uel nite-chiua, to overcome. Nitla or nic — to do something; çan iuh nic-chiua, to say or do something intentionally, as a joke; nic-chiua in tlacalaquilli, to pay the tax, the tribute; ye onic-chiuh in nonahuatil (Par.), I have done my duty; uel nic-chiua, to do something well; achi uel nic-chiua, to do something moderately; oquichiuh juramento, he has taken an oath, he is sworn; ye nic-chiua or itla nic-chiua, to do something, to carry it out; acan uel nic-chiua, to be very uneasy, very affected by something; atle immaca onic-chiuh, I did not remain without doing anything; atle uel nic-chiua, to idle, to wander, to do nothing; ma tic-chiuh, take care not to do it, beware of acting thus; tiçatl yuitl tlapall nic-chiua, to warn, to notify someone; iæquich itlapal qui-chiua, who makes every effort; atle qui-chiua, he does nothing, he is a lazy person; in itla qui-chiua, he who does something; muchi uel qui-chiua, who is skilled at everything; nic-chiuaqui (Olm.), come to do; quh nic-chiuani, to be in the habit of doing something; itla nic-chiuani (Olm.), oh! if only I had done it; freq. nitlatla-chiua, to do something often. Pass. chiuililo; nita-chiuililo (Olm.), something is done to me; lit. I am affected in something.
3501: Pret. OCHIUALYETOCAC: to boast of having done something that one has not done; nicno — anicno-chiualyetoca, not to boast of something, to deny having done it. RR. chiualtia, toca.
3502: Possibility, ease, power, ability; icuhca chiualizth, ease, quickness, lightness. Often preceded by uel, auel; auel chiualizth, impossibility, powerlessness, difficulty. R. chiua.
3503: Pret. OCHIUALLAN: to compel someone to do something. Nitla or nitetla — to force; nic — to desire, to want something to be done; nic-chiuallani in teocalli (Car.), I want the temple to be built. RR. chiua, tlani.
3504: n. v. Place, time, season, epoch when something is done; in comp. ichiualoyan nic-chiua, to do something in its proper time. R. chiua.
3505: Often preceded by the adv. uel, well. Feasible, easy; icuhca chiualoni, easy to do, that can be done quickly. R. chiua.
3506: Pret. OCHIUALTI: to abandon oneself to something; nicno-chiualtia cochiztli, nicno — to give oneself over to sleep, to sleep a great deal, to devote oneself to sleep. Nitetla — to compel someone to do something. R. chiua.
3507: Pret. OCHIUALTILI: to carry out something; nicno-chiualtiha in tlaxcalli in no-mieno — to have the bread made by my mother nantzin (Car.). Rev. of chiualtia.
3508: Pret. OCHIUALTOCAC: Nino — to praise oneself, to boast of having done something that one has not done.
3511: Pret. OCHIUAZNEC: nitla or nic — to want to do something, to consent to carry it out; anic-chiuaznequi, to refuse to do it. RR. chiua, nequi.
3512: n. «on the nine waters». A mythical river that the dead were said to cross (Clav.).
3514: «nine dogs» (Clav.). God of lapidaries, whose feast was celebrated in the month of teotleco. RR. chiucnahui, itzcuintli.
3515: Pret. OCHIUHTICATCA: nitla or nic — to be occupied with something; ma qui-chiuhtie, do not occupy him with it, do not burden him with that. RR. chiua, ca.
3516: Pret. OCHIUNTINEN (Olm.): nino — to give oneself over to something, to become accustomed to something. RR. chiua, nemi.
3517: Pret. OCHIUHTLAN: to send someone to do something. RR. chiua, tlami.
3319: Weekly scheduler; pl. chicueilhuitequip- paneque. RR. chicueilhuitl, tequippane.
3322: n. A period of eight days, octave. RR. chicuelhuil, tlateomatiliztl.
3323: n. One hundred and sixty (cloaks, loaves, sheets of paper, etc.); lit. eight score.
3325: pret. to bend, to make an object crooked (stick, stem, etc.).
3328: n. Eight corncobs. RR. chicuei, olotl.
3329: n. Eight rows or lines of something. RR. chicuer, tepantli.
3330: n. used to count animate beings and flat, thin objects. One hundred and sixty; lit. eight-score. RR. chicuei, poalli.
3331: n. used to count round, thick objects. One hundred and sixty; chicuepoaltetl omei, one hundred and sixty-three; chicuepoaltetl ommacuilli, one hundred and sixty-five; etc. RR. chicuepoalli, tetl.
3333: n. used to count round, thick objects. Eight; oc chicuetetl, eight others; ic chicue- tetl, eighth; nie chicuetetl, the eighth; in chicuetetl centetl, one of eight. Pl. chicueteixtin; in chicueteixtin, all eight. RR. chicuer, tetl.
3334: n. Eight parts or pairs, eight different objects, etc.; ic chicuetlamantli, eighth part; ic chicuetlamantli, the eighth part.
3378: A species of tall, round grass. RR. chili, malacail.
3380: Dish, vessel for holding a stew. RR. chilmolli, caxitl.
3381: n. Sauce or stew of chili pepper. RR. chili, molli.
3382: Pret. OCHILNAMACAC: to sell chili pepper, spices, chilli in particular.

3385: Spice shop, place where chili is sold. RR. chilnamaca, yan.
3387: Medicinal plant also called chilpanxochitl (Hern.). RR. chilpan, tlaçolli.
3388: Plant used to treat abscesses. (Hern., Clav.). RR. chili, patli.
3389: Pret. OCHILTECAC: ni — to sow chili pepper. RR. chili, teca.

3394: Sauce or stew of dried chili pepper. RR. chili, uaqui, molli.

3397: One who makes shields, shield maker. R. chimalchiua.
3559: With flight, withdrawing, retreating; tetech où teixpampa chololiztica, taking flight. RR. cholohiztli, ca.
3560: n. Flight, leap, fall, current: teixpampa chololiztli, flight before people, before the enemy. In comp. ichololiz, his flight; atlichololiz, course, fall, flow of water. R. choloa.

3562: Cholollan inhabitant, resident of Cholollan; pl. Chololteca, populations who were principally devoted to commerce (Clav.).
3563: pret. OGHOLOLTI: nino — to take flight. Nite — to put someone to flight. Nita — to let something escape, game, etc. R. choloa.

3565: pret. OGHOLTI: nino — to run, to flee in general. R. choloa.
3566: pret. OCHONEGOCOX: ni — to be possessed by a demon. RR. ...(?), cocoya.
3569: Cricket that sings like a cicada.
3571: pret. OCHOPINI: nite — to sting someone, said of a snake. Nitla — to peck, to eat, said of birds. Rev. chopinilia: yuhquimma titototzintli iyequin timotla-chopinilia (Olm.), you are like the little bird, like it you peck.
3575: ni — to faint, to swoon, to become pale from weeping. Nino — to lament, to grieve while tearing one's hair. RR. choquiztli, aua.
3576: pret. OCHOQUILI: nite or nic — to weep for someone. Nita or nic — to weep for something; nechoquilia in notlatlacol (Par.), I weep for my sins. Rev. of coca.
3577: Worthy of being wept over, or that which causes weeping. R. choquilia.
3578: pret. OCHOQUILITZATZIC: to cry out, to groan, to lament; ni — to cry out while weeping. RR. choquiztli, tzatzi.
3342: Chia, a type of oily seed (zargatona). See Gaia.
3343: Pret. OCHIENCUEN chia. RR. chien, cuema.
3344: Neighboring the volcano of Popocatepetl (Clav.). RR. chien, tlan. CHIYACTIC, adj. n. Stained with oil or grease, full of grease. R. chiyaua.
3345: Pret. OCHIYALI or OCHIYALTI: to become full, in speaking of female animals; — to sow.
3346: Pret. OCHIYALTI: nic — to wait for someone with something to receive them, to keep something for their arrival.
3348: Pret. OCHIYAUAC: to fill with grease, oil, etc. R. chia.
3350: adj. Greasy, full of grease; cenca chiyauacayo michin, otter, an amphibious animal; nm — to stain oneself, to grease oneself. lit. an extremely viscous fish. R. chiyauacayotl.
3351: Grease, cream. In comp. ichiyauacayo or ichiyauaca, its grease; ichiyauaca in chichualayotl, cream of milk. R. chiyauac.
3352: Rendered fat, lard. RR. chiyauacayotl, tlaatililh.
3353: Greasy, stained with oil. R. chiyauiçotl.
3355: (Car.). Chili seed. In comp. ichilachyo (Par.), the seed of the chili. RR. chili, achtli.
3356: n. District of the province of Cohuixco; capital of the same name (Clav.). RR. chilatl, pan.
3298: Pret. OCHICOTLATETO: % — to be delirious, to have delirium, to speak a great deal and incoherently.
3300: One who is delirious, who raves. R. chicotlatetoa.
3301: Delirium, extravagance of speech. R. chicotlatetoa.
3302: Pret. OCHICOTLATO: #%ù — to be delirious, to blaspheme, to speak maliciously. RR. chico, tlatoa.
3303: n. v. One who is delirious, blasphemer, clever and malicious speaker. R. chicotlatoa.
3305: n. v. Deceitful speech, blasphemy, etc. R. chcotlatoa.
3306: Half-mad, extravagant, senseless. RR. chico, tlaueliloc.
3307: Pret. OGHICOUEYAQUILI: nitla — to make or compose something unevenly. RR. chico, ueyaquilia.
3308: Pret. OCHICOUI: to favor someone, to give them more than others, to share things unequally. R. chico.
3309: Pret. OGHIOTLAPAN: nitla — to make or compose a thing from several parts (ametalar algo de diversos colores). RR. chico, tlapana.
3310: Composed, formed from various pieces. R. chictlapana.
3311: (Clav.). Sap of the chictzapoil while still green, sticky and hardening easily; the Mexicans used it to make small statuettes (chicle).
3313: In general, for words beginning OCHICOTLAMA nite or nitetla in this way, search under chico.
3315: n. used for counting animate beings and flat, thin objects. Eight; lit. five-three; oc chieuei, eight more; ic chicuei, eighth; ic chicuei, the eighth. Pl. chicueixtin; in chicueixtin, all eight. With the postpositions can and pa: chicuexcan, in eight parts or portions; oc chicuexcan, in eight other parts or portions; chicuexpa, eight times; oc chicuexpa, eight other times; chicuexpa ixquich, eight times as much; chicuexpa chicuei, eight times eight. RR. chicu, ei.
3316: Which has or lasts eight days; said especially of a feast whose octave is celebrated. R. chicueilhuitl.
3317: Octave of a feast. RR. chicüeilhuitl, quixtia.
3279: Acolhua chief, who settled at Aaltocan, under Xolotl whose second daughter he married (Clav.). RR. chicome, quauhtli.
3281: n. used to count round, thick objects. Seven; oc chicontetl, seven others; ic chicontetl, seventh; mic chicontetl, the seventh. Plural chiconteixtin; in chicoteixtin, all seven. RR. chicome, tetl.
3282: n. Seven similar or different things, parts, pairs. RR. chicome, tlamanth.
3283: Septennial, seven years old, recurring every seven years, etc. R. chiconxiuitl.
3285: GHICOQUAQUA or CHICOQUATIC, adj. v. Half eaten. RR. chico, qua.
3286: Pret. OCHICOQUIZ: to lean toward evil, to give in to wrongdoing; to deviate; to diminish, to decrease, etc.; chicoquiga in nonетлауhtil, the gifts I receive diminish. RR. chico, quiça.
3287: pp. OCHICOQUIXTI: nitla — to pay part of a debt; to put something outside; to discover by chance someone's fault. RR. chico, quixtia.
3288: Pret. OCHICOQUIZTIA: diminishing; chicoquistiuh tlaxtlauiliztli, tax, tribute. ni — to go as a levy. R. chicoquica.
3289: Pret. OGHICOTAMACHIUH: nitla — to measure something very badly, to give short measure. RR. chico, tamachiua.
3290: One who deposes someone, who deprives them of their office. RR. chico, iquania.
3291: Deposition, deprivation of an office or position. RR. chico, iquania.
3292: That which can be suspected; ütech chicotlamachoni, or by euphony ütechicotlamachoni, suspected, suspect, giving rise to suspicion. R. chicotlamati.
3293: pret.: ni — to be suspicious; tetech ni-chicotlamati, to suspect someone, to distrust them. RR. chico, mati.
3294: Suspicion; tetechicotlamatilizili (for tetech chicotlamatiliztli), suspicion, distrust. R. chicotlamati.
3295: Suspicious, jealous, envious; tetechicotlamatini (for tetech chicotlamatini), jealous of someone. R. chicotlamati.
3358: Atole or porridge made with chilli and corn flour. RR. chili, atolh.
3359: pret. OCHILAUI: nitla — to put chili water into something. R. chilatl.
3360: A drink made with cacao and chili. RR. chilli, cacauatl.
3361: A kind of wild duck or ruddy teal, with a long tail, particular to Mexico (Hern.). RR. chili, canauhtli.
3362: Sauce, stew of pepper or green chili. RR. chilchotl, molli.
3364: pret. OCHILÇOC: nitla — to thread chili peppers.
3366: n. of instrument. Large needle or similar object for threading chili, sewing mattresses, etc. R. chilço.
3367: Edible yellow pepper (Hern.). RR. chilli, cotztic.
3369: pret. OCHILAUI: nitla — to put pep-
3370: : nitla — to sow pepper in a dish, a stew. R. chili.
3375: Pepper of which twelve main species are counted; it is used together with corn to make a highly prized drink, chili atolli (Hern.); Castilian chili, clove (clavo de especie).
3376: That which contains pepper, prepared with chili; chillo cacauatl, a drink made of chili and cacao. R. chili.
3377: pret. OCHILLOTI: to put pepper in a dish, in a stew. R. chili.
2738: Pret. OCENTLAÇOTLAC: to love someone extremely; nota, nonan niquiz-centlaço-tla, I love my father, my mother very much. RR. cen, tlaçotla.
2739: dim. of centlacol. A piece, a bit, a fragment.
2740: A bed of earth (a plot of ground).
2742: A handful, a bundle of small things. RR. ce, tlacuitlalpilli.
2744: A small handful, a small bundle of fine things.
2745: Having the thickness of a fathom; centlacuitlanauatecthi quauitl, a tree the thickness of a fathom. RR. ce, tlacuia, tlanauatecth.
2746: A unit of length, an ordinary pace (passo del que anda). RR. ce, icæitl.
2747: A bundle, everything gathered and tied together; centlayaualolli centli, a bundle of ears of maize. RR. ce, tlayaualoa.
2748: n. v. Half of a thing cut or sawed through the middle. RR. centlacol, ixtlapana.
2749: A thin thing, a leaf of a book. RR. ce, ixtli.
2750: Thinness, the state of a thin thing. R. centlaixth.
2751: Pret. OCENTLALCAUI: nitla or nic — to abandon, to completely forsake a thing.
2753: Pret. OCENTLALI: nino — to unite, to gather together; mo-centlalia, they gather together; it is added up, totaled; teca nino-centlalia, to conspire against someone. ninotla — to examine oneself, to make an examination of conscience. nite or nic — to unite, to assemble persons; niquiz-centlalia (for niquin-centlalia) (Car.), I gather them together. nitla or nic — to amass, to pile up, to collect things; nic-centlalia noyollo or in noyollo, to resolve upon something, to make a resolution to amend oneself. RR. cen, tlalia.
2754: A mound, a heap, a pile (of ears of maize, etc.). R. centlalia.
2755: A ream (of paper, etc.), a handful, a bundle of small things. RR. ce, tlalpilli.
2756: dim. of CENTLALPILLI. A small handful, a small bundle.
2757: A mouthful (a gulp). RR. ce, tlaltecth.
3219: — ni — to have hard, swollen breasts.
3221: See ment of the breasts. R. chichiualnanatziui.
3222: pret. OCHICHIUALPATZGAC: Aitla — to milk. RR. chichiualli, patzca.
3223: n. of instrument. Bucket for milking. R. chichiualpatzca.
3224: pret. OCHICHIUALTOMAUAC: #1 — to have hard, swollen breasts. RR. chichiualli, to- Swelling,
3225: Swelling of the breasts. R. chichiualtomaua.
3227: pret. OCHIOHIUALTZITZITZQUI, etc.: to touch, to feel a woman's breasts. RR. chichiualli, taitzquia.
3228: n. Trimming, set, assortment. In comp. ichichiuhca, its trimming; cauallo ichichiuhca, mile — horse harness. R. chchiua.
3229: pret. OCHICHIUI: wtla — to fill something with saliva. R. chichul.
3230: pret. OCHICHIUILI: to set traps, to harm someone; to betray. R. chichiua.
3231: pret. OCHICHIXTICATGA to await the orders of one's master. RR. chicha, ca.
3232: Screech-owl; a whistle that children play with.
3233: Sideways, backwards, badly, irregularly, unevenly, reluctantly, nitetla — to stretch: nitla — with bad grace; to the side, partly, by fraction, halfway, etc.; chico xiqu-iquani inon tetl (Par.), move aside, put that stone to one side; chico ximo-tlal (Olm.), sit to the side; most often used in composition: nüla-chicoquixtia, I pay a part; nite-chicotlachialtia (Olm.), to give bad advice to someone, to make them wicked; chicoqua, half-eaten; etc. In numeration, chico signifies half the fingers, a fraction, perhaps one quarter of the full count cempoalli, twenty, and combines with the names of the first four numbers to form those of the numbers from six to nine inclusive: chiquace, chicome, chicuey and chiconau.
3234: pret. OGHICOCAG: sideways, nitla or mc — to hear something wrongly, backwards, etc.; to listen reluctantly, unwillingly, angrily. RR. chico, caqui.
3235: n. Quarter or portion of an animal. RR. chico, centlacol.

3237: pret. OCHICOYOLLOAC: ni — to suspect. R. chicoyotl.

2457: Used in comp. iceliloca, rev. icelilocatzin: iceliloca yn inacayotzin totecuiyo Jesu Christo, or iceliloca sacramento, communion. R. ceha.
2458: Pret. OCELMACAC: to procure pleasure for someone. RR. celic, maca.
2461: Pret. CELTI: nite- — to amuse, entertain someone; nitla- — to distract; nite- — to seek to draw others along, to tell jokes. R. celia.
2463: Tenderness of something fresh, new, and green. In comp. tcelticayo or iceltica in quauitl (Car.), the freshness of the tree. R. celtic.
2464: dim. of ceuric. Very tender, very fresh, green.
2465: n. v. Verdure, freshness of a budding plant. R. celha.
2468: Completely great, superior. In comp. tecemacheauh, the greatest of all.

2471: Pret. OCEMACICANAMICTI: nite- — to reward someone generously, completely, entirely. RR. cemaci, namictia.
2472: Pret. OCEMACICAPOTI: nite- — to reward someone generously, completely, entirely. RR. cemaci, potia.
2474: To surround completely, to be spread out; cemacicatzacutimani in cemanauac in mayanalizth, hunger completely surrounds the world. RR. cemaci, tzacutimani.
2475: Pret. OCEMACITICATOA: entire, pure, fine, without blemish. RR. cen, acitica.
3579: n. v. Tears, moaning, bleating, roaring, howling. In comp. nochoquiliz or nochoquiz, my tears; ichoquiliz, his tears; etc. R. choca.
3580: pret. OCHOQUILTI: nite — to make someone cry. Rev. of choca.
3582: pret. OCHOQUITZATZIC: nino — to utter cries, moans; to lament. RR. choquiztli, tzatzi.
3583: n. v. Cries of one who weeps, moaning, lamentation. In comp. nochoquitzatziliz, my lamentations. R. choquitzatzi.

3585: A sad, mournful, lamentable song. RR. choquiztli, cuicatl.
3586: pret. OCHOQUIZNEMMICTI: nino — to weep bitterly, crying out, lamenting, striking oneself. RR. choquiztli, nen, mictia.
3588: pret. OCHOQUIZ-: nino — to weep while striking oneself, to be in despair. RR. choquiztli, quauitl, tetl, tlaça.
3589: While weeping, with cries, moaning, tears, etc. RR. choquiztli, ca.

3595: pret. OCHOQUIZTZATZIC: nino — to moan, weep, cry out, utter moaning. RR. choquiztli, tzatzi.
3597: One who moans, weeps, laments. R. choquiztzatzi.
3598: See tlacnopauia. *CHRISMAYOTIA, pret. OCHRISMAYOTI: nite — to anoint someone, to give them ordination. R. chrisma. *CHRISMATICA, adv. With the chrism; chrismatica nic-macpalalaua in sacerdote, to give ordination; lit. to anoint the hands of the priest with the chrism. RR. chrisma, ca. *CHRISTIANOYOTL, n. Christendom. R. christian.
3660: etc.: » — to remove from a bird the stomach, the breast, etc. RR. ciciyotca, eua.
3661: pret. ocicIvoTon, freq. of crvoTowa: nite — to kill someone by mutilating them.
3662: n. v. Crack, opening, crevice, fissure. R. ciciyotoma.
3663: pret. OCICIYOTON, v. n., freq. of civo-rom. To come apart, to open, to crack a great deal, said of an object.
3665: adj. v. Split, cracked, creviced, open. R. ciciyotomi.
3666: A medicinal plant resembling cimatl, used especially against ulcers, from which it derives its other name palancapatli (Hern.).
3667: pret. OCICINO: nino — to boast, to brag.
3669: or CICIOTCAYEUA. Same verb as Cicivorcaeus, etc.
3670: freq. of cITLALLO. Starry, full of stars.
3672: n. Doublet, corset, jerkin. *CIDRAQUAUHTLA, n. Cedar-lemon orchard, a place planted with citron trees. RR. cidraquauitl, tla. *CIDRAQUAUITL, n. Citron, citron tree. RR. cidra, quauitl.
3673: See Ciuarz; and likewise for all words beginning in the same way.
3674: See CiacaracmiLnua; and likewise for all words beginning in the same way.
3675: or CIOCOPICTIC, adj. v. Pale, having grown pale, with a changed face as a result of fright or illness. R. ciyocopini.
3676: Change of face, etc., pallor, extreme whiteness. R. ciyocopini.
3677: Pret. OCIYOCOPIN, etc.: ni — to change, to grow pale, to become wan from fright or as a result of illness.
3679: adj. v. Pale, wan, changed. R. ciyocopini.
3680: That which belongs to the hare, thing of the hare or of the grandmother. R. cit.
3681: pret. OCIYOTON, etc.: nitla — to unstitch something, to undo a seam.
3682: or CIOTOMI, pret. OCIYOTON, etc.: ni — to come undone, to become unstitched, to split, to break, etc.
3684: pret. OCIN: leaf of the maguey or agave used to draw thread from it. nitla — to arrange, to prepare the...
3685: n. Plant whose root is used in stews.
3686: pret. OCIVAUI: in a food or in the sap of the agave to give it flavor. nitla — to add cimatl to give a good taste. R. cimatl.
3688: Dish, meal of hare; cinacomolli tlatexuihlli, hare stew. RR. citli, nacamolli.
3690: n. Dried ear of corn. See CENTLI.
3691: See Civocoricric; and likewise for all words beginning in the same way.
3692: or CIPATLI, n. Shark, an extremely voracious fish. Cal. First day of the month; ce cipaëtli, "one shark", first sign in judicial astrology (Sah., Clav., Fabr.).
3693: Mexican prince who was put to death in 1520 (Clav.). *CIRUELASQUAUITL, n. Plum tree. RR. ciruela, quauitl.
3694: dim. of citli. Young hare, leveret.

3699: adj. Starry, full of stars. R. citlali.

2477: A measure of length, one arm's length, one fathom. RR. ce, acoll.
2478: Pret. OCEMAN: nitla — to continue, to persevere, to go through to the end. RR. cen, ana.

2482: Simple, which is one. RR. cen, man.
2483: Continuous, which holds together, forming a whole. R. cemana.
2484: Long, extended, forming a whole; cemantoc tlalh, firm land, expanse, continent.
2487: Pret. OCEMA: mino — to be proud; mo-cemat, he is proud, vain. RR. cem, mati.
2488: Plural. A neighborhood. RR. ce, atzaqua (?).

3159: Pret. OCHICHINAQUILTI: nite — to torment, torture someone. R. chichinaca.
3160: Pret. OCMICMNARZ, freq. of the unused form.
3161: nite — to afflict, torment someone, to cause them harm or pain.

3164: Pret. OCHICHINCALIUH: mo — to scrape oneself, to become excoriated; chichincaliui, he is scraped, who is excoriated by sweat.
3166: Confidant of the Texcocan prince Neçahualcoyotl (Clav., Bét.).
3167: n. v. One who suckles. R. chichi.
3168: aug. of chichini. One who suckles excessively, for a long time, even though already grown.
3169: Pret. OCMICUINO, freq. of CHINOA: nino — to burn oneself. Nite — to pass over a flame, to burn someone. Nitla — to dry out, to pass green branches or similar things over fire.

3173: Wounded, having a sore or wound. R. chipeliui.
3174: Pret. OCHICHIPIAGO: nino — to squirt saliva through the teeth. RR. chichil, piaçou.
3175: Pret. OCHICHIPICAC, v. n. freq. of chipin. To drip, to fall drop by drop, speaking of a liquid.

1715: Pret. OAXIXTZACU: nino (for nino) — to have kidney stones, to be afflicted with strangury. RR. axixth, tzaqua. *AXNOCONETL, n. Donkey foal. RR. asno, conetl.
1716: Passive and impersonal form of the 1st entry.
1717: One of the Aztec chiefs who founded Tenochtitlan (Clav.). R. axolotl.

1719: Lagoon on the seashore. RR. atl, xomulh.
1720: A type of bird with white plumage.
1721: Young son of the king of Tetzcuco, Neçahualcoyotl (Clav.). R. axoquen.
1722: Abyss, great depth of water. RR. atl, xoxouia.
1723: Pret. OAXTICATOA: n (for ni) — axtica, to be in the process of working; tlein t-axtica? (Par.), what are you doing at this moment? RR. ay, ca.
1725: Combed, arranged, speaking of a woman's hair. R. axtlaua.
1726: Pret. OAXTOCA: n (for ni) — axtoca, to be occupied, to be in the process of working; tle amazxtoque? (for an-axtoque) what are you doing? what are you in the process of working on? RR. ay, onoc.
1727: Pret. OAXUUI: nitla — to anoint, to rub a thing with ointment or varnish. R. axui.
1729: Full of, covered with ants. R. azcatl.
1730: Pret. OAZCAYOAC: n (for ni) — to be covered with ants. R. azcatl.
1732: or AZCAPUTZALCO, a town near Tenochtitlan which ruined the latter after having long been its tributary; today Escapuzalco (Clav., Aub.).
1513: A very beautiful princess, wife of Huetzin, king of Coatlichan; — mother of the king of Mexico Acamapich (Clav.).
1514: n. Fountain pillar on which jugs are placed. RR. atl, uilzth.
1515: pret. OATTOUI: nino (for nino) — to be the first to file a complaint before the judge. R. achto.
1517: n. v. One who stops water from flowing away. R. atzaqua.
1518: "Place where waters are stopped," one of the districts of Mexico comprising the N.E. part (Clav., Bét.). RR. atzaqua, co.
1519: Often, frequently: atzan nitlaqua, to eat often; atzan or achtza niqu-iloa, to repeat, to say again, to reply; ça or çan atzan, very often, frequently, several times. RR. ach, çan.
1520: Pret. OATZAOU: ni (for ni) — water, to prevent it from flowing. Nino (for nino) — to surround oneself with water, to isolate oneself (aislarse). RR. atl, tzaqua.
1522: Action of stopping water. R. atzaqua.
1523: n. of instrument. Stopper, plug to prevent the flow of water. R. atzaqua.
1524: n. v. One who stops water, prevents it from flowing. R. atzaqua.
1526: Pret. OATZELHUI: nino (for nino) or better nino — to get wet with water. Nite — to stop wetting someone. Nitla — to water, to throw water, to wet something. RR. atl, tzelhuia.
1527: One of the Aztec chiefs who founded Tenochtitlan (Clav.).
1528: n. v. Disorder, agitation, etc. RR. a priv. tintlalteppachiuiliztli.
1529: dim. of atl. Water in small quantity; ma tepitzin atzintl æinechmo-tlaocolili (Par.), give me a little water.
1530: Pret. OATZONUI: nino (for nino) — to stir, to move about in water. RR. atl, çoneua (?).
1531: Lock of hair left on young girls when their hair was cut. See.
1533: Pret. OATZOTZON: nitla — to prepare the foundations of a wall by tamping only the earth without digging a trench; to beat laundry in order to wash it. RR. atl, tzotzona.
3538: 5. v. Extravagance, madness, leap. R. chocholoa.
3539: Pret. OCHOCHOLOLTI: nitla or nic — to make a thing jump, to bounce, to throw the ball. R. chocholoa.
3540: and n. v. Senseless, mad, one who lacks judgment; nichocholoqui, to act the fool, to commit extravagances. R. chocholoa.
3541: Trot of a pedestrian. R. chocholotiuh.
3542: Pret. OCHOCHOLOTIA: #4 trotting, leaping. R. chocholoa.
3545: : ni — to sting oneself, to have itching.
3549: : nitla — to eat, to peck at grains, said of birds.
3551: Pret. OCHOCOIAIC: ni — to drink, to take chocolatl; achtopa ximo-teochihua, auh quin ti-teniçaz ihuan ti-chocolaiz (Par.), pray first, then you will breakfast and take chocolatl. RR. chocolatl, i.
3552: (Clav.). Chocolatl, a food made, in equal portions, from the seeds of cacao and those of the tree called pochotl (chocolate).
3553: Pret. OCUOTI: nic — to make someone cry; nechoctia (for nech-choctia), to cry over something, (such a thing) makes me cry. Rev. of
3555: Pret. OCHOLHUI: nitla or nic — to jump over, to cross a stream, a ditch. R. choloa.
3556: Pret. OGHOLO: ni — to flee, to run, to jump, to absent oneself, etc.; ocholo iyollo, a dissolute, corrupt (woman); lit. her heart has fled; pan ni-choloa, to press, to trample; teuicpa ni-choloa, to leap toward someone; temacpa ni-choloa, to escape, to get away; tepan ni-choloa, to leap upon someone, to run after them; ma cana tepan ti-cholo (Olm.), do not go fleeing with a woman; teixpampa mi-choloa, to escape, to flee before someone, to draw back; non-choloa, to begin swimming; ueca ni or non-choloa, to leap far, to cover a long distance; anocholoayan ni-choloa, to run heedlessly; aycholoayan choloa, he runs or one who runs recklessly.
3557: Place where one runs. In comp. nocholoayan, place where I run. RR. choloa, yan.
3558: Fugitive, runaway, leaper; teixpampa choloani, fugitive, one who takes flight before the enemy. R. choloa.
3700: augm. of citlauix. Dawn of day, great morning star.
3701: n. "smoking star", lord of Quiahuiztlan, one of the four governors of the republic of Tlaxcala at the time of the Spanish conquest, who converted and was baptized under the name of D. Bartolomeo (Clav.).
3703: Lake that produced very beautiful rushes; — located north of Mexico (Clav.); — volcano of Orizaba. RR. citlalin, tepetl, c.
3706: 1° Hare; pl. cicitin; 2° grandmother; pl. citin (Par.). In comp. noci, my grandmother; teci, someone's grandmother; pl. nocihuan, our grandmothers. Rev. citzintli; in comp. mocitzin, your grandmother; ixpan in nocitzin (Par.), before my grandmother. dim. of citlalin.
3707: A mythical figure who is said to have perished fighting against the sun (Clav.).
3710: or CIHUAAYOTL, n. Secretion of the female sexual organs. In comp. tociuaayo, our seed, the seed of woman in general.
3712: (Clav.). Mexican general who was killed in the battle of Otompan. *CIUACAUALLO, n. Mare. RR. ciuatl, cauallo. *CIUACAUALLOPIXQUI, n. Keeper of mares.
3714: "serpent woman", goddess also called Quilaztli, mother of the human race. She always gave birth to twins. King Itzcoatl had a temple erected in her honor (Clav.). — Supreme magistrate who judged as a court of last resort and whose power was nearly equal to that of the sovereign. He has thus been likened to a viceroy or lieutenant; rev. ciuacoatzintli. RR. ciuatl, coatl.
3715: Menstruation, menses; lit. women's illness. RR. ciuatl, cocolli.
3716: Sow, female pig; ciuacoyametl miriuhqui, sow that has given birth. RR. ciuatl, coyametl.
3717: Woman of ill repute, prostitute; pl. ciuacuecuechme. RR. ciuatl, cuecuech.
3718: pret. OCIUACUECUENOT to seek out women, to be greatly given over to them.
3740: Vulva, vagina. In comp. iciuanacayo, her vulva. RR. ciuatl, nacayotl.
3741: n. of instr. Woman's girdle, etc. RR. ciuatl, necuitlalpiloni.
3744: n. woman; ciuanemactli ipan nenamactihzth, betrothal gifts, gift given at a marriage, engagement gift. RR. ciuatl, nemactli.
3745: Attached to women (in a positive sense). RR. ciuatl, nemilice.
3746: n. of instr. Coif, woman's bonnet. RR. ciuatl, nequatlapacholoni.
3748: pret. OCIUANOTZ: ni — to be greatly devoted to women; cenca ni-ciuanotza, to exhaust oneself through lust. R. ciuatl, notza.
3749: n. intercourse with women; cenca ciuanotzalisth, exhaustion caused by frequent intercourse through lust. R. ciuanotza.
3750: n. women; cenca ciuanotzani, one who is exhausted by intercourse with women; one who is given over to commerce with women. R. ciuanotza.
3751: Hermaphrodite, person of two sexes. RR. ciuatl, oquichth.
3752: Eriocoma floribunda (O. y B.). A medicinal plant with several species, whose leaves, prepared as a decoction, are used for childbirth; hence its name. RR. ciuatl, path.
3753: pret. OCIUAPAUN: ni — to be cold, to freeze, to be dying of cold. RR. cetl, uapaua.
3754: n. Lady, legitimate wife of a great personage; ua ciuapilli, great, noble lady, queen; ciuapille, to a lady. Plur. ciuapipiltin, matrons, women who died following their first childbirth and elevated to the rank of goddesses (Sah.). In comp. teciuapil, someone's lady. RR. ciuatl, pilli.
3755: n. lady. In comp. mociuapiltzin, the young lady.
3757: pret. OCIUAPOTI: nieno — to take as a companion a woman, speaking of another woman, to live in intimacy with another. dim. of ciuatl. R. ciuapotli.
3758: (Olm.). Used in comp.: ociuapo, a woman like me, my companion, my relative; teciuapo, the attendant, the sister, the relative of someone. RR. ciuatl, poth.
3759: Mistress of slaves. In comp. nociuatecuyo (Car.), my mistress. RR. ciuatl, tecuiyotl or tecuth.
3940: A type of sensitive plant used in medicine (Hern.). It is claimed to cause sleep. R. cocha.
3941: 5. v. freq. of cocmiuayax. Dormitory, bedroom, lodging, any place where one sleeps.
3943: : mile — to deceive, to seduce a woman.
3945: : wi — to sleep, to lower one's head from drowsiness.
3946: Pret. OGOGOCIUH: nn — to inflame, to sting the mouth, speaking of chili pepper, etc. R. cococ.
3948: A medicinal herb good for menstruation (Hern.). RR. cococ, xihuitl.
3949: pret. ococox: ni — to be suffering, sick; to be pregnant; cocoya in inacayo, weak, ill; lit. his/her body is suffering.
3951: A medicinal plant with several properties (Hern.). R. cocoya. cocoyo. Plural of coyotl.
3952: Hip. In comp. tococoyo-yan, our hip, our hips, hips in general.
3954: : ni — to walk on all fours.
3955: Vein, spring. In comp. icocoyo, his/her vein; atlicocoyo, vein, trickle, spring of water. R. coyoni (?).
3956: A type of very graceful luminous beetle (Clav.).
3957: Pret. OCOCOLCUIC: mi — to become arrogant, to be impatient, hot-headed, to become angry. RR. cocolli, cui.
3958: Pret. OCOCOLOUITI: nite or nimo — to irritate, to make someone angry. R. cocoleui.
3959: Hot-tempered, violent, impatient, wicked, cruel, etc., said of persons and things; achi cocole, somewhat wicked, harsh; amo cocole, gentle, not impatient; nicocole, I am hot-tempered, impatient; amo çan quenin ticocole (Par.), you are truly wicked. R. cocolli.
3820: Pret. OCOAMA: nitla — to receive someone, to offer them hospitality, to treat them affectionately. RR. coatl, mati.
3821: A kind of eel; lit. serpent-fish. RR. coatl, michin.
3823: Son of Neçahualpilli and Xocotin, king of Tetzcuco (Clav.).
3824: Herb used as an antidote; lit. serpent's tongue. It is also called coatli (Hern., Clav.). RR. coatl, nenepilli.
3825: Pret. OCOANOTZ: nite — to invite someone to a meal. RR. coatl, notza.
3826: Pret. OCOAONOCA (Olm.), v. irr. used only in the plural: ti-coaonoque, we are seated in council, at table; impers. coaonoac, all are invited; coaonoaya, all were at the feast. RR. coatl, onoc.
3827: Rue, a medicinal plant used as an antidote (Hern., Clav.). RR. coatl, patli.
3828: Pret. OCOATECAC: nite — to seat, to place the guests at table. RR. coatl, teca.
3829: (Clav.). Wall that surrounded the great temple of Mexico, made of stones in the shape of serpents. RR. coatl, tepantli.
3830: Town neighboring Tetzcuco (Clav.). RR. coatl, tepetl, c.
3831: Pret. OCOATEQUIT: nitla — to work on public or communal projects. R. coatequitl.
3832: Public work, labor done in common. RR. coatl, tequitl.
3833: Serpent's egg; — catfish, a kind of sturgeon (Hern.). RR. coatl, tetl.
3834: Toltec princess, who married the Acolhua chief Tzontecomatl (Clav.).
3835: n. Serpent, snake, navel, belly; twin (coate); iztac coatl, white serpent. Cal. fifth day of the month; ce coatl, "one serpent". Ninth sign in judicial astrology (Sah.). In comp. ycoauh, his serpent; fig. ye nican onotiuitz in ycoauh in itequan in tloque in nauaque (Olm.), already comes here the punishment of God. Plur. cocoa.
3836: (Clav.). A copper farming implement with a wooden handle, a kind of shovel or spade (coa).
3837: Woman, female; a rare form of the word ciuatl. R. çoa.
3838: plur. Assembly, gathering, agglomeration of people. RR. coatl, tlacatl.
3839: Pret. OCOATLALI: nite — to place the guests at table. RR. coatl, tlalia.
3960: pret. ococoluui: nitella or nicle — to bend something for someone; to torment, mistreat someone, to harm, cause harm; to speak obscurely, in a roundabout way, with mistrust, etc. R. cocoa.
3961: pret. ococolt: nite — to hate, detest someone, to wish them ill; nehuatl aic onimitz-cocoli, ye tehuatl mochipa tinech-cocolia (Par.), I have never hated him, but you always detest me. Pass. cocolilo. R. cocoa.
3962: pret. ococoliicailpi: nite — to enchant, bewitch someone. RR. cocolia, ilpia.
3963: Unhealthy, pestilential; uei cocolico, very unhealthy, extremely pestilential. R. cocoliztli.
3964: pret. ococolilma (Par.) to think, believe that one is hated. RR. cocolilo, mati.
3966: pret. ococolilochti: nino — to relapse, to become sick again. nite — to increase, worsen someone's illness, to make them sicker. RR. cocoliztli, ilochtia.
3968: pret. ococoliltocac (Par.): nino — to think, believe that one is hated. RR. cocolilo, toca.
3969: pret. ococolizquic: nitla — to suffer, to be sick, to contract an illness, to be seized by disease. RR. cocoliztli, cui.
3970: pret. ococolizcuiti: nite — to make someone sick. R. cocolizcui.
3971: With suffering, illness, painfully. RR. cocoliztli, ca.
3972: pret. ococoliztlapaliuh: mi — to be enduring, to bear hardship; amo cocoliztlapaliui, delicate, one who cannot endure suffering. RR. cocoliztli, tlapaliui.
3973: pret. ococoliztlapiuili: nite — to aggravate someone's illness. RR. cocoliztli, tlapiuilia.
3974: Illness, ailment, plague, epidemic; ayoui temicti cocoliztli, a swelling, a deadly tumor; uei cocoliztli, great pain; ueiya in cocoliztli, the illness, the epidemic increases. In comp. nococoliz, my ailment; cua incocohz, menstruation; lit. women's illness. R. cocoa.
3975: pret. ococollalili: to give rise to quarrels, disputes; to be the cause of someone's illness, to make them sick. RR. cocoll or cocoliztli, tlahilia.
3976: At the northwestern extremity of the Mexican empire, not far from Lake Chapallan (Clav.).
3977: Charge, affair. In comp. nococol, my charge, my affair, a thing entrusted to me, nite — to do what is under my care; nimococol, I am in your charge, I am entrusted to you so that you may take care of me.
3901: n. v. Dormitory, bedroom, cell for sleeping; inn, lodging. RR. cochi, yan.
3902: (Car.), n. of instrument. Anything that causes sleep or serves for sleeping; somniferous plant. R. cochi.
3903: dim. of the preceding. Small bed covering.
3904: Pret. OCOCHIZMAUH: to sleep, to put someone to sleep, to bring them slumber — to make sleep. RR. cochitli, maua.
3905: n. v. Sleep; cochizth nicno-chiualtia, to sleep a great deal. In comp. nocochiz, my sleep; aoc nocochiz nic-mati, I no longer know sleep. With the postposition pan: nocochizpan, during my sleep; incochizpan, during their sleep. R. cochi.
3906: Pret. OCOCHMAUH: to put someone to sleep. RR. cochi, maua.
3907: Pret. OCOCHMIQ: ni — to sleep deeply, to be in extreme need of sleep, to be overwhelmed by sleep; nic — to put to sleep. RR. cochi, miqui.
3908: adj. v. Drowsy, asleep, overcome with sleep. R. cochmiqui.
3909: or COCHOTL, n. Parrot, of which two species are distinguished (Hern.). Plur. cochome.
3910: A type of fish whose head resembles that of a parrot (Hern.). RR. cocho, michin.
3911: n. v. Heavy sleeper, lazy, carefree, negligent person. R. cochi.
3912: Pret. OCOCHPETLAUH: to cover someone who is sleeping. RR. cochi, petlaua.
3913: n. v. tetlan cochqui, guest, one who receives hospitality. R. nite —; Asleep, resting; cochi.
3914: Pret. OCOCHQUIXTI: to wake someone up. RR. cochi, quixtia.
3915: Pret. OCOCHTECAC: nic — to rock, to lull a child to sleep. RR. cochi, teca.
3916: Pret. OCOCHTICATE (Car.): ni — to be in the process of sleeping; nitla — to wake up. RR. cochi, ca.
3917: Pret. OCOCHTIPILCACA or OCOCHTIPILCAYA: ni — to sleep with one's head drooping, to succumb to sleep. RR. cochi, pilcac.
3918: Pret. OCOCHTIPILCATOCA: to sleep with one's head bowed. RR. cochtipilcac, onoc.
3919: Pret. OCOCHTIA (Car.): ni — to walk while sleeping. R. cochi.
3880: Pret. OCOCHCANENEC: nino — to pretend to sleep; mo-cochcanenequi, he pretends, feigns to be asleep. RR. cochi, nenequi.
3881: Pret. OCOCHCATLAPIQUI: — to pretend to sleep. RR. cochi, piquia.
3882: Pret. OCOCHCAUN: nite — to let someone sleep, to go away, to withdraw. Also, to have neglected something by having fallen asleep. RR. cochi, caua.
3883: Pret. OCOCHEUH: nino — to get up, to get out of bed, to stop sleeping. nite — to wake someone up, to make them get up. RR. coch, eua.
3884: Pret. OCOCU: ni — to sleep; xi-cochi, sleep (imperative); xi-cochican, sleep (plural imperative); itlan ni-cochi, to sleep with a woman, to lie beside her; tepal or tetlan ni-cochi, to receive hospitality at someone's house; ti-cochia, you were sleeping; cochca, he had slept. Rev. cochitia. Pass. and impers. cochiu«.
3885: Pret. OCOCHYAYATICATCA: to lower, to nod the head from sleepiness, to sleep. RR. cochi, yayaticac.
3886: n. v. Bedroom, bed. In comp. nocochian, my bedroom; nononqua incochian in teopixque (Car.), the religious each have a separate cell. R. cochi.
3887: dim. of the preceding. Small room. In comp. nocochiantzin, my small room.
3888: Eyelashes, eyelids. In comp. nocochia, my eyelashes; tocochia, our eyelashes, eyelashes in general. R. cochi.
3889: Pret. OCOCOHIGOLO: to stop sleeping. nite — to wake someone up, not to let them sleep, to deprive them of sleep, to prevent them from sleeping. nino — to wake up. RR. cochi, coloa.
3890: Sleep. R. coch. COCHILLOTENTLI. See CUCUILLOTENTLI.
3891: n. Sleeper, one who loves sleep. R. cochi.
3892: Cocoon, the casing made by caterpillars. RR. cochi, piloa.
3893: A kind of bird that lives on the shores of the Ocean (Hern.). RR. cochi, tenacatl (?).
3894: Pret. OCOCHITI: cochitia in tlatoani (Par.), the lord sleeps. nino — to sleep; mo- nite — to lodge, to receive someone, to put them to bed, etc. Rev. of cochi.
3895: Pret. OCOCHITLEUAC: ni — to leap out of bed, to get up quickly. RR. cochi, eua.
3896: Dream, reverie; can tuhquin cochitleualli, temicthi ipan tic-matizque in iz tlaticpac ic pacoa (Par.), we must regard the pleasures of this world as dreams. R. cochitleua.
3897: A kind of bird that lives in warm climates (Hern.). RR. cochi, tototl.
3898: Pret. OCOCHITTAC: nitla — to see something in sleep, in a dream. RR. cochi, itta.
3899: n. (Clav.). Casimiroa edulis. A tree whose fruit is a berry of very pleasant flavor, which is said to have the property of inducing sleep. RR. cochi, tzapotl.
3980: adv. With rage, with anger. RR. cocollotl, ca.
3982: A very commonly used medicinal plant (Hern.); a shrub used to make cradles (Bét.). RR. coçolli, mecatl.
3983: Death from illness. RR. cocoliztli, micqui. cocoLo. Plural of cocoli.
3985: pret. ococoLo, freq. of coLoa: nitla — to go somewhere by many detours; to sing, to cackle, speaking of a rooster.
3986: pret. oçoçoLocac, freq. of çoLoni: ni — to expire, to breathe one's last breath; to blow, to make noise, speaking of bellows, etc.
3987: Buzzing, noise, blowing, puffing. R. coçoloca.
3990: pret. ococoLocHo: a garment, paper, etc. R. cocoloa.
3992: n. v. Flute player. RR. cocoloctli, pitza; nitla — to roll.
3994: n. v. Weakness, debility, thinness. R. cocolot.
3996: pret. ococoLotic, freq. of coLoa: ni — to become thin, to be dry, weak.
3999: Thinness, weakness. R. cocoloti: nic — to make thin, to emaciate.
4000: Pret. OCOCOLOTIA: nino — to walk with a skipping gait. Nitla — to go somewhere by a roundabout way. R. cocoloa.
4001: Pret. OCOCOLOTZ, freq. of çolon: nitla — to make noise, said of falling water.
4002: Pret. OCOCOLTI: nite — to charge someone with a task. nino — to take charge of something. Nitla — to trouble, to divide, to sow discord. R. cocolh.
4004: Plural of çoun and of çoztli.
4006: Pret. ococomocac, v. n. freq. of cowo. To crackle, to burst, to rumble, said of fire, flame, or a storm.
4007: Pret. ococomonuh, v. n. freq. of comonuh. To be full of potholes.
4008: Uneven, full of potholes. R. cocomoliuh.
4009: Pret. ococomont, freq. of comonna: nino — to savor, to seek the breast; said of a child who is nursing.
4010: Pret. ococomotz, freq. of comoni: nino — to savor the breast, said of a nursing child. Nitla — to stamp the feet, to make noise.
4011: Pret. OCOCONAUH: nite — to win someone over through promises, gifts, etc.
4013: Childishness, puerility. In comp. nococoneyo, my childish behaviors; icoconeyo m-aitia, he behaves childishly. R. conell.
4014: Pret. OCOCONEITTAC, freq. of CONEITTA: nino — to conceal one's age, to make oneself appear younger.
4016: Extremely pungent, burning the mouth intensely. RR. cocotl, pat.
4017: Pret. OCOCOPEUH: nino — to lose the scab or crust of a wound.
4018: n. v. The falling away of the scab or crust of a wound, etc. R. cocopeui.
4019: Pret. OCOCOPIN, freq. of copina: nitla — to unbutton something.
4042: Creasing of a fabric, a garment. R. cocototzaui.
4043: Pret. OCOCOTOTZCATCA: nino — To be crouched, to have one's legs crossed. RR. cocototzoa, ca.
4044: pret. ococororzo, freq. of cororzoa: nitla — to pleat, crease, fold something.
4045: Pleated, folded, creased, curly. R. cocototzoa.
4046: pret. OCOCOTZAGU: nino — to choke, to have one's throat obstructed. RR. cocothi, tzaqua.
4047: pret. OGOCOTZO, freq. of coTzoa: mi — to run lightly.
4048: n. v. Agile runner. R. cocotzou.
4049: One who has a narrow throat and eats with difficulty. R. cocotzaqua.
4050: A man who has large calves. R. cotetli.
4052: pret. ocoçouilt, freq. of çouicra: nitetla — to unfold extensively, to carefully spread out something for someone.
4053: n. Infirmary, hospital, house, establishment for the sick. RR. cocoxqui, calli.
4054: Weakness, exhaustion, debility, languor. R. cocoxqui.
4055: Languid, sick, weak, exhausted. RR. cocoxqui, nacayotl.
4056: dim. of cocoxqui. Deformed, sickly, delicate.
4057: A purgative herb that has the smell and taste of thyme; another plant of the same name, very tall, is used to heal wounds and pains; its flavor is sharp and pungent, and it is therefore also called quauhchilli (Hern.). RR. cococ, æihuitl.
4058: Adam's apple, knot of the throat. In comp. fococoxixipoch, our knot, the knot of the throat in general. RR. cocotl, æixipochoa.
4059: pret. ococoxocac, v. n. freq. of coxoni. To resound, to make noise, speaking of a vessel that is not full, an egg that is not fresh, etc.
4080: 5. v. Salted pork, dried pig; lit. desiccated pork meat. RR. coyametl, nacail, uatza.
4085: 5. dim. of coyametl. Small pig, suckling pig.
4086: Pig, wild boar; coyametl yellzotzol, crop of the pig; coyametl icuitlatecon tlanacatentli, large black pudding; cexiuhcayotl coyametl, piglet, young pig that is only one year old. Plur. coyameme.
4092: dim. of the preceding. Small basket made of palm leaves.
4093: Orchard, place planted with palm trees.
4095: pret. OCOYAUAC, v. n. To widen, to enlarge. nitla — to enlarge, widen a hole; to peel, to remove the bark.
4096: Large, wide, open; coyauac tezcatl, large mirror; amo coyauac, narrow, small, not wide. R. coyaua.
4097: Width, largeness; amo coyaualiatli, narrow space, small space, the state of any object that is deficient in size. R. coyaua.
4098: Pierced, perforated; plur. coyauhque (Olm.). R. coyaua.
4099: v. which was a tributary of the kingdom of Acolhuacan (Clav.).
4060: pret. ococoxowN, v. n. freq. of coxoni. Same meaning as the preceding.
4061: pret. ococoxoni: nitla — to shake, clean a cask. R. cocoxoni. coCOXOTZA, pret. ococoxotz, freq. of coxoni (Car.): nitla — to shake a vessel, eggs, etc.
4062: or CUCUXQUI, adj. and n. v. Sick, infirm, withered, catamite, minion; itic cocoxqui, one who has colic. Plur. cocoxque. R. cocoa.
4063: Medicinal plant also called cocoztic or cocoztli (Hern.). RR. cocoztic, tamatl.
4064: or COCOZTIC, adj. freq. of coztic. Greenish, glaucous; cocoztic ytelolotli, glaucous eyes; cocoztic palancapatli, a kind of sarsaparilla (Bét.). coCOZTLI. See CocozтаматL.
4065: Herb whose root is used against dysentery (Hern.). RR. cocoztic, xochitl, patli.
4066: n. Director of the goldsmiths of Ocolco, appointed by the king of Tetzcuco, Techotlala (Clav.).
4068: One of the chiefs who led the Toltecs to Tollantzinco (Clav.).
4069: Inhabitant of the province of Cohuixco; plur. Cohuixca.
4070: n. Province to the south of that of Tlahuican (Clav.).
4072: Basket made of palm leaves. RR. çoyatl, chiquiuhtl.
4075: Suckling pig, young pig, piglet. RR. coyametl, conetl.
4077: Pork tripe; coyamecuitlaxcolli tlaeztetl, blood sausage; lit. pig's tripe filled with blood; coyamecuitlaxcolli tlanacatentli, sausage. RR. coyametl, cuitlaxcolli.
4078: Double dewlap of the pig. RR. coyametl, eltzotzolli.
4140: 8. Grandfather, grandmother. In comp. nocol or rev. nocoltzin, my grandfather; tecol, the grandfather of someone; ycol in ticmo-namictiznequi (J. B.), the grandfather of the one you wish to marry. Plur. nocolhuan, my ancestors: incal in nocolhuan (Par.), the dwelling of my ancestors. R. coli.
4142: or CULUA, pret. OCOLO, etc.: to bend, to fold, to twist, to wind, to meander, etc. Nitla — to double, to fold a thing; to make circuits, to go somewhere by a roundabout way.
4143: Pret. OÇOLO: old; fig. ta-çoloa (Olm.), he ruins the land.
4144: Pret. OCOLOCHAUH, v. n. To become wrinkled/pleated, speaking of garments.
4145: Pret. OCOLOCUIC: nitetla — to make someone go by a roundabout way. R. coloa. nitla — to render a thing.
4146: Pret. OCOLON, v. n. To flow with a crash, impetuously, speaking of a watercourse or a torrent.
4147: Pret. OCOLONTIA, v. n. Same meaning as the preceding.
4149: Pret. OCOLOTIA: nitlatla — to walk making many detours. R. coloa.
4150: Scorpion; fig. culutl nicte-quaqualhtia (Olm.), to punish someone. Plur. cocolo. In comp. icolouh, his scorpion; ye nican uit: in icolouh (Olm.), his punishment is already coming. R. coloa.
4153: 5. State to the west of the valley of Toluca; capital of the same name (Clav.). RR. çolli, tepetl, c.
4154: Pret. OCOUI, v. n. To twist, to bend, to curve. nitla — to sip, to swallow. R. coloa.
4155: Twisted, bent, curved, sinuous, winding. R. coloa.
4157: Suburb of Mexico City on the N. E. side (Clav.). RR. cote, co.
4158: 5. Medicinal plant; a type of bird, avicula inflexa (Hern.). RR. colli, tototl. coM. See Cox.
4159: Pret. OCOMALI: nino — to get angry, to fly into a rage. R. coma.
4101: pret. OCOYOGAC: MW — to blow, to be windy.
4102: pret. OCOYOCHOCAC: to cry like a fox. RR. coyotl, choca.
4103: Howling, cry resembling that of the fox. R. coyochoca.
4104: adj. v. Pierced, open, perforated. R. coyoni. — COYOHUACAN, n. A small state to the south of Mexico; capital of the same name (Clav.). RR. coyotl, can.
4105: Flank, side of an animal. In comp. tocoyoyan, our flank, the flank in general.
4108: of the province of Capotecapan (Clav.). RR. coyolli, apan.
4109: Bell, fishhook; large tree that produces a kind of nut (Hern.).
4110: n. Case for awls. RR. coyolomitl, calli.
4112: dim. of coyolli. Small bell, etc.
4113: (Clav.). Daughter of Coatlicue. RR. coyolli, xauhqui.
4114: Medicinal plant whose flower is very beautiful (Hern.). RR. coyolli, xochatl.
4116: n. v. Opening, window; used in comp. icoyonca, its opening; — to howl, tlalapantli icoyonca, opening of a canal; nocoyonca, my window, from my window, etc. R. coyoni.
4118: etc.: nino — to walk, to go on all fours. RR. coyotl, nenemi.
4119: pret. OCOYON, etc., v. n. To be pierced, to open, to become perforated, etc.
3920: pret. ococuiuiTza (Car.): nino — to come while sleeping. RR. cochi, uitz.
3921: pret. OCOCHTLAZ: nite — to put someone to sleep by enchantment in order to rob them; to put to sleep well; to lull, to put a child to sleep. RR. coch, tlaça.
3924: pret. OCOCHTOCA or OCOCHTOYA: ni — to be lying down asleep, to sleep lying down. RR. coch, onoc.
3925: pret. OCOCHUETZ: ni — to fall asleep, to fall asleep immediately. RR. cochi, uetzi.
3926: pret. OCOCHUI: nite or nnic — to sleep with a woman, to possess her during sleep. R. cochi.
3927: Servant. In comp. tecocouh, someone's servant, domestic.
3928: pret. ocoçoc, freq. of ço: nitla — to string together, to thread beads, flowers, or other things. Pass. coçolo or çoçoua. cocoA. Plural of coatl.
3929: pret. ococoa: nino — to be sick, to injure oneself, to hurt oneself, to be in danger of death; nitic mo-cocoa, to have intestinal pain, colic; lit. in my belly I suffer; notzontecon mo-cocoa, my head hurts from overwork; ix mo-cocoa, my eyes are sick; intlacayac tlalticpac tlacatl otlatlacoant, intlacayac ic mo-catzahuani in tlatlacolli, ayac miquizquia, anoac mo-cocozquia (Car.), if no man had committed a fault, if no one had defiled himself with sin, no one would die or be sick. Nite — to wound someone, to hurt them; nech-cocoa nocomal, my spleen hurts me; noyollo ixco nech-cocoa, my stomach hurts me; nech-cocoa in cactli, the shoes hurt me; iquac in ila tecoco, tetolini mopan mo-chiua (3. B.), when a misfortune befalls you. Impers. necocolo; in iquac mayanalo, çan no iquac necocolo (Par.), when there is famine, sickness also reigns.
3930: or COCOUA, pret. ococoun, freq. of coa: nitla — to buy several things, to buy often.
3931: Slow, one who eats slowly: nicocoçaçalc, I eat very slowly to better savor the food. RR. cococatl, çaçalic.
3932: Indigence, misery, misfortune, hardship. R. cocoa.
3933: pret. OCOCOCOAPOLO: nite — to afflict, torment, mistreat someone. RR. cocoa, poloa.
3934: A vegetable plant used in medicine (Hern.). RR. cococatl, quilitl.
3935: Goods, food, nourishment, sustenance. In comp. nocococauh, my goods, my property; icococauh, his goods; tecococauh, another's goods; nitecococauh, I am the slave, the property of someone.
3936: pret. OCOCATLATO: nite — to lose all restraint, to keep no measure when speaking to someone. RR. cococ, tlatoa.
3937: n. Owner, one who possesses goods; nicococaua, I am master, I have property. R. cococatl.
3939: freq. of cocnitate. Eyelashes, eyelids. In comp. tococochia, our eyelashes, eyelashes in general.
3720: nephew of the king of Tetzcuco, Itlilxochitl (Clav.). R. ciuacuecuenoti.
3721: Female lamb; pl. ciuaichcacocone (Par.). RR. ciuachcatl, conetl.
3724: Attached to women (in a favorable sense). RR. ciuatl, (?).
3725: One who makes use of women, who has women. R. ciuatl.
3726: adj. Effeminate, cowardly, lacking courage, who has the heart of a woman. RR. ciuatl, yollotl.
3727: Womb, vulva. In comp. iciuayo, her womb, etc. R. ciuatl.
3730: Devoted to women; ni- ciuayuinti, to give oneself excessively to women. RR. ciuatl, yuintia.
3731: pret. oCIUAMA (Olm.): to be grateful toward one's wife; oquichtli aqui- nn — nitla or nic — ciuamati, a man who is ungrateful toward his wife (a man who sets no value on the service or benefit of a woman). RR. ciuatl, mati.
3733: pret. ociuaMIC: ni — to become a widower, to lose one's wife. RR. ciuatl, miqui.
3735: pret. OCIUAMONCAUH: to accompany the bride. RR. ciuamontli, caua.
3736: pret. OCIUAMONTI: nino — to take a daughter-in-law, to marry off one's son. R. ciuamontli.
3737: n. Daughter-in-law, bride. In comp. no- ciuamon, my daughter-in-law; pl. nociuamonhuan, my daughters-in-law. : nic — ac-
3739: Delicate, weak; lit. one who has the flesh of a woman. RR. ciuatl, nacayotl.
4180: often preceded by the adv. ye. One who is in agony, close to death. RR. caua, onoc.
4181: pret. OGONCIIUH: earthenware vessels. RR. comutl, chiua.
4182: Pottery, establishment, house where earthenware vessels are made. RR. con- ni — to make, to fabricate chiuhcan, calli.
4183: n. (Olm.). Pottery factory. In comp. noconchiuhcan, my pot-making workshop; pl. nococonchiuhcan. RR. conchiua, can.
4184: n. Potter, maker of vessels. R. conchiua.
4186: Baby's cradle, jug, old vessel. RR. comutl, çolli.
4189: Small child, infant at the breast. RR. conetl, chichi.
4190: Pierced, perforated, spongy, light. R. çoneua.
4193: n. Child's excrement. RR. conetl, cuitlatl.
4194: Childishness, everything relating to early age, to childhood. R. conetl.
4195: pret. CONEITTAC: nino — to hide one's age; to make oneself appear younger than one is. RR. conetl, itta.
4196: Amnion, chorion, membrane of the fetus. RR. conetl, matlatl.
4197: Fox of the smallest species (Clav.). RR. conetl, epatl.
4198: Swaddling clothes, infant's wrappings, everything used to wrap children.
4240: Pret. OGOQUIALTI: nino — to get muddy, to get dirty, to cover oneself with mud. Nite — to cover someone with mud. Nitla — to dirty, to muddy a thing, to cover it with mud. RR. çoquitl, altia.
4241: to put in the mud. RR. çoquitl, aqui.
4242: Pret. OGOQUIAQUI: nino — to get bogged down. Nite — to put, to sink someone in the mud; fig. te-çoquiaquia (Olm.), he ruins the country. OÇOQUIAG: ni — to fall, to plunge into the mud. R. coquiaqui.
4243: n. v. Bog, muddy place, full of mire. RR. çoquiaqui, yan.
4244: Mud, silt, bog, etc., muddy water. RR. coquil, atl.
4245: Pret. OGOQUIAUILTIA: nino — to delight in, to spend one's time in the mud; fig. mo-çoquiauiltia (Olm.), lazy, idle, one who only takes pleasure in the mud, etc. RR. çoquitl, auiltia.
4247: Pret. OÇOQUICHIUH: ni — to make mud, mortar for construction, for making vessels, etc. RR. çoquitl, chiua.
4248: One who makes clay paste, mortar, etc. R. coquichiua.
4250: ÇOQUIO, adj. Covered in mud, muddy; pl. çoquiyoque. R. coquiyotl.
4251: Pret. OÇOQUIYOAC, etc.: ni — to cover oneself with mud. R. çoquitl.
4252: Pret. OÇOQUIYOTI: nino — to get muddy, to get dirty, to cover oneself with mud. Nite — to dirty someone, to cover them with mud. Nitla — to muddy, to cover a thing with mud. R. çoquiyoa.
4253: Dregs, sediment, filth. In comp. içoquiyo or içoquio; tatacteocuitlatl içoquiyo, silver dross; noçoquiyo, my inner defilement, my body, my flesh; noçoquiyocauh (Olm.), the filth that covers me. R. çoquitl.
4255: Pret. OÇOQUIMOTLAC: nite — to throw, to hurl mud at someone; fig. te-çoquimotla (Olm.), he destroys, ruins the country, governs badly. RR. çoquitl, motla.
4256: Pret. OGOQUINELO: nino — to cover oneself with mud, to get dirty, to wallow in mire; fig. mo-çoquineloa (Olm.), he is perverse, debases himself, defiles himself. Nite — to cover, to dirty someone with mud. Nitla — to cover a thing with mud, to roll it in mire. RR. çoquitl, neloa. See Çoquiyoa.
4257: Pret. OGOQUIPACHO: nitla — to fertilize fields, to spread silt, manure. RR. çoquitl, pachoa.
4258: n. Place where the Franciscans founded a convent in Mexico City; it was so named because of a spring that kept the ground moist and muddy there (Bét.). RR. çoquitl, pan.
4259: Bog, place full of mud. RR. çoquipolaqui, yan.
4200: Pret. OCONETI: nino- or nieno- — to adopt, to take as one's child, when said of a woman. R. conetl.
4201: Generally used by women. Child, boy or girl. Plur. cocone. In comp. noconneuh or rev. noconetzin, my son, my daughter; teconeuh, son or daughter of someone; conetl joins with animal names to indicate their young: canauhconetl, duckling; mazconetl, lion cub; etc.
4202: Dim. of the preceding. Small child. In comp. noconeton, my little child.
4205: Pret. OCONEUH: nino- — to flare up, to stir, to rise up; to grow, to overflow; to be frightened, to bristle. Nite- — to stir up, to rouse people. Nitla- — to stir, to beat, to remake a bed, to make it soft; fig. çaconeua (Olm.), he destroys and ruins the State through his pride.
4206: Rise of waters, flooding, inundation. R. çoneua.
4207: One who desires, covets something; ayachi connectini, indisposed, sickly; lit. softened, tender, soft; one who no longer desires anything. R. nectia.
4208: n. v. One who comes after others, a successor. R. toquilia.
4209: Pret. OCONTLILHUI: nino- — to smear oneself with soot. Nite- — to smear, to blacken someone. R. contlilli.
4210: Soot, from smoke, from a pot, etc.
4212: n. v. Action of following something; teyollo contocaliztli, comprehension, the act of grasping what someone is saying. R. toca.
4213: One who grasps, understands something; iyollo contocani, one who hears and feels things well. R. toca.
4214: Dim. of comitl. Small vessel. copA. See Co.
4215: n. Palate, roof of the mouth. In comp. nocopac, my palate; tocopac, our palate, the interior of the mouth in general. RR. comitl, paqui.
4216: Incense boat, vessel for incense. RR. copalli, acaliotli.
4217: Pret. OCOPALCHIPINI: nite- — to paint someone with copal. RR. copalli, chipinia.
4218: District of Mexico where the remains of Moteucoma II were deposited (Clav.). RR. copalli, co.
4219: Pret. OCOPALHUI: nitla- — to varnish, to wax something with copal. R. copalli.
4120: pret. ocoyoni: nitla — to pierce, bore, dig, hollow out something. R. coyonu.
4121: Case, sheath. In comp. icoyonyo, its case; tepuztepatiloni icoyonyo, case, surgeon's instrument pouch. R. coyoni.
4122: adj. v. Pierced, bored, open, hollowed out. R. coyoni.
4123: A species of tomato, whose extremely bitter root is used in childbirth (Hern.). RR. coyotl, patli or tomatl.
4125: pret. ocoyoquetz: nino — to get on all fours. nite — to have relations with a woman in the manner of animals. RR. coyotl, quetza.
4126: Fox, jackal. Plur. cocoyo or coyome.
4129: pret. ocoyoun: — to cry out, utter cries, howl. R. coyotl.
4132: A certain demon. COLESQUIYOTL, n. Cabbage sprout, shoot. One also says: coles iquiyo. RR. coles, quiyotl. COLESTECTLI, n. Bed of cabbages not yet transplanted. RR. coles, teca.
4133: or culhua, n. Inhabitant of Colhuacan. Plur. Colhuaque or Colhuaca. The latter comes from the sing. Colhuacatl, which is little used (Olm.). R. colli.
4134: Name given to several localities famous in Mexican annals; one of them, situated south of Mexico City, near the strait connecting Lake Chalco to that of Tetzeuco, was the seat of an important lordship. RR. colli, can.
4136: pret. oçoziciui: ni — to become a quail. R. colin.
4137: coastal province on the Pacific slope; capital of the same name (Clav.).
4139: pret. ocoliui, v. n. To lean, to tip over, to bend, speaking of a wall, etc.; tepan coliui yuei tetl, quei quauitl (Olm.), he punishes harshly.
3840: See COATLANTLI, n. Wild mulberry. — to make sit,
3841: Temple built in Mexico under King Axayacatl (Clav.); — a town near Quauhnahuac (Bét.). RR. coatl, tlan.
3843: Canine tooth, tusk. In comp. icoatlan, his tooth; coyametl icoatlan, wild boar's tusk; tocoatlan, our canine tooth, the canine tooth in general; fig. oputstequililoc in icoatlan, inic aocmo tequaz (Olm.), he has been deprived of his power, so that he will no longer be proud. See Coatzantli.
3846: A kind of branch or stem that the Indians steeped in water, in order to then drink the liquid. See Coanexelilui.
3847: divided into two factions, the Tenochca and the Tlatelolea (Clav.). RR. coatl, camatl.
3848: of the state of Acolhuacan (Clav.). See Quauhtlicnac. RR. coatl, chantli. where the Aztecs would have
