3849: Priestess consecrated to the cult of Huitzilopochtli. — Goddess of flowers, who had a temple in Mexico called topico, whose feast was celebrated in the month of tozoztontli (Clav., Sah.). RR. coatl, cueitl.
3850: A species of tomato (Hern.). RR. coatl, tomatl.
3852: Maritime province on the Gulf of Mexico, to the east of the province of Cuetlaxtlan; capital of the same name (Clav.). RR. coatl, tzaqua, co.
3853: Medicinal plant whose root is a bulb; hence its name (Hern.). RR. coatl, tzontecomatl.
3854: Medicinal plant whose spotted flower is very beautiful and prized. It was mixed with dough to make a kind of cake or luxury bread (Hern., Clav.). RR. coatzontecomatl, xochitl.
3855: A kind of worm the size of a finger, whose bite is harmful (Hern.).
3858: Medicinal plant. See Cmaccuitlatl. RR. coatl, xihuitl.
3861: Temple of Mexico, built in the N.W. part of the city, called Tlatelulco (Clav.). RR. coatl, xolotl.
3864: A kind of herb; fig. coaxoxouhqui, nanacatl nicte-ittitinemi (Olm.), to make someone wicked, perverse, to show them the wrong path; ütech oquiz in coaxoxouhqui (Olm.), he is drunk, has lost his reason. RR. coatl, coxouhqui.
3865: on the river of Papaloapan, neighboring the Gulf of Mexico in the province of Cuetlachtlan and conquered by the Mexican monarch Moteuhçoma I (Clav.). RR. coçamalotl, apan.
3868: or CUGATLI, n. Beetle; coçathi coztie, medicinal plant also called çacaxochitl (Hern.).
3869: Yellow, golden, fiery red; tetl coçauhqui, fine gold. R. coçawia.
3871: OCOÇAUIZ: Medicinal plant; ni — to become yellow, to turn yellow; tla-coçauia, to ripen, to turn yellow, speaking of harvests.
3872: pret. OCOÇAUIZTOCA, v. n. To be dry, ripe, speaking of grain. RR. coçauia, onoc.
3873: pret. OCOCHAАН: nino — to stretch oneself, to extend, to lengthen. RR. cochi, aanu.
3875: used in comp. nocochca (Par.), my evening meal. R. cochi.
3876: pret. OCOCHAУOTI: nino — to have supper; mc-nequi nino-cochcayotiz (Par.), I want to have supper. Nite — to give food to, to have someone eat supper. R. cochi.
3877: Supper, food, nourishment, sustenance; anecini in cochcayotl neuhcayotl, extreme misery; lit. there is no food. In comp. nocochcayo, my supper, my food. R. cochi.
3878: pret. OCOCHCAMACHALO: Ni — to yawn. RR. cochi, camachaloa.
4320: pret. OCOTZQUA: nite — to bewitch someone. RR. cotzthi, qua.
4321: Who has large calves, round, fat calves. RR. cotthi, tapayoltic.
4322: Calf, the fleshy part of the leg. In comp. nocotzteuh, my calf; tocotzteuh, our calf, the calf in general. RR. cotztli, tetl.
4323: Or CUTZTLALHUATL, n. Nerves of the calf. RR. cotetli, tlalhuatl.
4324: freq. of the preceding. Nerves of the calf, of the leg.
4325: Calf, the fleshy part of the leg. In comp. nocotz, my calf; tocotz, our calf, the calf in general. R. cotzoa.
4327: cotztli, totomactic. couatl, n. (Olm.). Master, owner of vessels, pots, etc. R. comitl.
4328: See Coa. çoua. Pass. of ço. — See Coa.
4329: Daughter-in-law. In comp. noçouamon, my daughter-in-law. Also written çoamonth. RR. couatl, month.
4330: n., dim. of çouatl. Little woman, little female.
4333: Woman, female; rare form of couatl. R. çoua.
4334: pret. OÇOUHTIMANCA, v. n. To be open, speaking of a book, etc. RR. çoua, mani.
4336: pret. ocouili: ninotla — to buy something for oneself. Nitla or nitla — to buy something. Nitetla — to buy something for someone. R. couia.
4338: A kind of pheasant whose flesh is extremely delicate.
4339: pret. ocoxouh, v. n. To resonate, to make noise, speaking of a vessel that is not full.
4220: 5. (Clav.). Northern province from which the tribes of Anahuac are said to have come. RR. copalli, tla.
4221: Copal, a tree that yields a resin called copal gum; by ext. incense, varnish. Several species of copal are known: copalquauhæiotl, which is very slender; tecopalquauil, or mountain copal; cuitlacopalli, copal excrement; xochicopalli, which flowers; mizquixochicopalli, whose gum is dark brown; etc. (Hern.).
4222: Gummy, having gum, resin, etc. R. copalh.
4223: n. seller of incense, of copal. RR. copalli, namaca.
4225: (Clav.). A moderately large tree, abundant in Michoacán, Yucatán, etc. Seller, merchant, whose pulp has the same properties as soap.
4227: pret. OCOPAMPOUH: to jest, to make laugh. RR. (?), poua.
4228: Toward; ihuicaccopauic (Car.), toward the sky. RR. copa, uic.
4230: pret. OCOPEUH: ni — to joke, ni — to jest, to say pleasantries.
4231: pret. OCOPICHAUH, v. refl. To become hollow, speaking of an object.
4232: adj. v. Hollowed out, grooved. R. copichaui.
4233: pret. OCOPICHO: an object such as a mat, a garment, paper, etc.
4234: (Clav.). A crown resembling a miter, used in the coronation of kings. nitla — to roll something; it was tall and pointed at the front; the back hung down over the neck.
4235: pret. OCOPIN: nitla or nic — to pull one thing from another, to extract, to mold, to copy, to reproduce a piece of writing; nic-copina in espada, to unsheathe, to draw the sword.
4236: n. of instrument. Mold; teixiptla copinaloni, a mold or hollow form for reproducing the likeness of someone; tomin copinaloni, a die used for striking coins. R. copina.
4237: Firefly. coPoLGoO (Clav.), one of the quarters of Mexico City, where Axayacatl had King Moquihuix put to death (Bét.).
4238: copilli, co. çoqauI, province at the southeastern extremity of the Mexican empire; principal town Tecpantla (Clav.).
4239: adj. v. Mired, fallen, sunk into the mud. R. coquiaqui.
4341: (Olm.). Brilliant, precious heart. In comp: ivozcayollo, his excellent heart. RR. cozcatl, yollotl.
4342: n. Cord, string of beads or similar things. RR. cozcatl, mecutl.
4343: (Clav.). Necklace. In comp. nocozcapetl, my necklace. RR. cozcatl, petlatl.
4344: Conquered by King Ahuitzotl (Clav.). RR. cozcaquauhtli, tenamtl, co.
4345: n. Red-collared eagle; vulture, bird of prey of the tzopilotl family. Cal. sixteenth day of the month; Boturini gives temetlatl in its place (Clav.); ce cozcaquauhtli «an eagle», 16th sign in judicial astrology (Sah.). RR. cozcatl, quauhtli.
4346: Brother and successor of Nahuixochitl in the lordship of Tzotzollan (Clav.).
4347: Pret. OCOZCATI: nino — to adorn oneself, to deck oneself with jewels. nite — to adorn someone with jewels. R. cozcatl.
4348: Pret. OGOZCATILI: nite or nic — to be someone's jewel; fig. to please them, to obey them; entla tic-cozcatilizque (Om.), if we obey him. R. cozcatia.
4349: Jewel, precious stone, rosary bead, necklace. In comp. nocozqui, my necklace; nocuzque (Olm.), O my jewel; fig. tecozcauh, someone's jewel, i.e. their son or daughter; pl. tecozcauan. With the postp. teuh: cozcateuh, like a jewel; cozcateuh, quetzalteuh ipan nimitz-mali, I love you, I esteem you extremely; lit. I hold you as a jewel, as a precious feather (words of a father to his son).
4350: One of the Aztec chiefs who founded Tenochtitlan (Clav.).
4351: Or CUZCATLATECTLI, n. Cord of rosary beads, jewels arranged in order.
4354: Same meaning as the preceding. RR. cozcatl, tlauipantli.
4355: n. (Clav.). A type of soldier's boot.
4357: n. A species of nopal (Hern.). RR. coztic, nochtli.
4358: augm. of coztic. Very yellow, strongly colored.
4359: Medicinal plant also called youalxochitl (Hern.). RR. coztic, quauhtli.
4260: Mired, completely covered in mud. R. çoquipolaqui.
4261: Pret. OGOQUIPOLACTI: to get mired, to sink into the mud. nite — to put, mano — to plunge someone into the mud. R. çoquipolaqui.
4262: Pret. OGOQUIPOLAC: ni — to throw oneself, to roll in the mud. RR. çoquitl, polaqu.
4263: Pret. OÇOQUIPOLO: ni — to knead, to prepare clay for making vessels, etc. Nino — to dirty oneself, to roll, to wallow in the mud (Olm.).
4265: Prepared clay for making vessels, pots, etc. R. çoquipoloa.
4267: Bog, place full of mud. RR. çoquil, . .... (?), tla.
4268: Vase, pot, cup, bowl made of clay. RR. çoquitl, tecomatl.
4271: Small ball of clay, pellet. RR. çoquitl, telolotl.
4272: Pret. OGOQUITIO: ni — to get wet, to be covered with water, to be soaked to the skin; to become muddy. R. çoquitl.
4273: Pret. OGOQUITILI: to dirty someone with muddy water. R. çoquitia.
4274: The act of getting wet, of getting dirty. R. çoquitia.
4275: Mud, waterlogged earth, silt, mire, nite — to wet, mortar, cob; fig. slave (Olm.); yuhquin coquitl onotiuitz, storm, hurricane, heavy downpour. In comp. noçoquiuh, my mud, my mortar, etc. See Coquivors.
4276: Pool of water, muddy place. RR. coquitl, tla.
4277: Made of earth, built with mud. RR. çoquitl, tlachiualli.
4278: Prepared clay for making tiles, vessels, etc. RR. coquitl, tlalilh.
4279: Pret. OGOQUITTIUITZA, v. n. To storm, to make a great tempest. RR. çoquitia, uitz.
4280: pret. OÇOQUIUI: nitla — to cover something with mud. nino — to get dirty, to soil oneself. R. çoquitl.
4281: Mud mask. RR. çoquitl, xayacatl. *CORONATIA, pret. OCORONATI: nite — to crown someone. R. corona. *CORONAUA, adj. Crowned, wearing a crown. R. corona. *CORREGIDORYOTL, n. Office, jurisdiction of a corregidor. R. corregidor.
4282: Pret. OCOTALO: ni — to croak, to grunt, to snore.
4283: n. Croak, croaking sound. R. cotaloa.
4284: Grunting, croaking, snoring. R. cotaloa. v. Grumbler, one who snores, who
4285: Fainted, discouraged, weakened, spoiled, etc. R. cotlaua.
4286: adj. v. Fainted, downcast, discouraged. R. cotlaua.
4287: Extremely venomous serpent. RR. cotona, coutl.
4288: adj. and n. v. Defective, incomplete; by ext. fragment, piece, debris. R. cotoni.
4289: Piece of bread given to a poor person. R. coton.
4292: Pret. OCOTON: nite — to pinch, to injure someone. nitla — to cut, to break into pieces something, to pick fruit, to gather ears of grain, to diminish, to thin, to reduce an object, to immolate, to sacrifice, to slaughter animals.
4293: Pret. OCOTONALLAN: nino — to wish to be reduced, diminished; to be generous; ayac mo-cotonallani or mo-cotontlani (Par.), no one wishes to be mutilated, to be deprived of a part, of a limb, that is to say of a relative. RR. cotona, ilami.
4294: Fraction, part, fragment, piece, member; by ext. relative. In comp. icotonca, his fraction, his part, his relative; icotonca or iceliuhca in tlamaceualiztli, a part of penance; tlacoyo icotonca in tonacayo, member, part of the body; nocotonca, my relative, my kinsman, my part, my member; plur. nocotoncauan or rev. nocotoncataitziuan, my relatives; tocotonca, our relative; plur. tocotoncauan, our kinspeople. R. cotoni.
4295: Pret. OCOTON, v. n. To break, to snap, speaking of an object such as thread, rope, etc.; to fall, to end, to break into pieces; oncan in coton (latocayotl Colhuacan (Chim.), then fell the State of Colhuacan.
4296: Pret. OCOTONI: to give someone a part of one's goods. R. coton.
4297: to share a thing with someone; nimitz-cotonilia in momapil or nite — to give pret. OCOTONILI: nite — to share (Par.), I cut off your finger. R. cotonia.
4298: Shared, divided, fractioned; cotonqui metztli, half-moon, quarter moon; cotonqui e amo tzonquizqui tlamaceualiztli, incomplete, unfinished penance. R. cotoni.
4299: Pret. OCOTONTLAN: nino — to be generous, one who lavishes what one has; amo nino or amno-cotontlani, to be miserly, stingy, not to give away one's goods; to be touchy, incorrigible. RR. cotona, tlani.
4461: : mi — to have itching sensations, to be agitated.
4463: pret. OCUEC- xoLHUI, etc.: nitla — to cross a ditch, a stream, etc. See Cuexcnoruura.
4468: (Par.). Woman who has or wears skirts. R. cueil.
4469: pret. OCUEYON: to boil, to swarm, etc. nm — to shine, to gleam,
4470: Skirt, petticoat, dress, etc. In comp. nocue, my skirt; icue, her skirt.
4471: syn. of ya or ye. Already; cuel oti-hualla, you have already arrived. Adv. loc. ye cuel or ye cuel ye, indicating more urgency, energy, or precedence than ye alone: quim otim-euh, auh ye cuel titla-qua (Car.), you have just gotten up, and already you are eating; ye cuel ye ontihuitl in acan ni-quiça (Gar.), it has been two years since I went anywhere; no cuel, çan no cuel or ye no cuel, also, another time; ye no cuel ic nitla-nauatia, to order, to make something known anew; ye no cuel acico, he also came; no cuel ye or ye no cuel ye, but on the other hand; irquich in tomin tic-popoloa, ca titla-calaquia; niman ye no cuel ye ti-ualhuiquixtia, niman ye no cuel ye titetla-qualtia (Par.), we spend a great deal of money, we pay taxes; but on the other hand we hold celebrations, and then we give meals; cuel achic, çan cuel or çan cuel achic, a moment, a brief instant; frequently, very often; çan cuel omic (Par.), soon after he died.
4472: n. ordinarily preceded by çan. Precocity of fruits or of anything else. R. cuel.
4473: sync. of cuel ye, used in optative tenses: ma cuele or ma ye cuele nitla-poa (Car.), would that I might read.
4475: pret. OCUELIUH: nm — to dislocate, to put a limb out of joint.
4476: pret. OQUELO: nitla — to bend, to fold a rod or other thing.
4477: pret. OCUELPACHILHUI: nitetla or nicte — to fold a garment or other thing for someone. RR. cueloa, pachilhuia.
4480: Folded, doubled, etc.; ce amatl cuelpachiuhqui, a folded piece of paper. R. cuelpachiui.
4481: pret. OCUELPACHIUR, v. n. To fold, to double over, speaking of an object. RR. cueloa, pachiui.
4300: Pret. OGOTOTZALHUI: nitetla — to fold, to pleat a thing for someone. R. cototzaw.
4302: Pret. OGOTOTZAUE: ni — To be paralyzed, crippled.
4303: Action of folding, of crumpling. R. cototzaui.
4304: Pret. OCOTOTZCATOA: » — To be seated on one's legs. RR. cototzoa, ca.
4305: Pret. OCOTOTZYETIA: mi — to hold oneself with legs shortened, folded; cototzyetiuh ipan cauallo, a horseman, one who rides on horseback. RR. cototzoa, yetiuh.
4306: Irresolution, indecision, fear, timidity. R. cototzoa.
4307: Pret. OCOTOTZO or OCOTOTZOAG: M — to fall, to weaken; ocototzoac (Olm.), he has fallen into decline. Nino — to crouch, to sit on one's legs. Mila — to fold, to refold, to pleat a thing.
4308: Irresolute, indecisive, full of timidity; crouching. R. cototzoa.
4310: Pret. OCOTOTZTLALI: to crouch, to sit on one's legs. RR. cototzoa, tlala.
4311: adj. v. Withered, wilted, etiolated, dried out. R. cototzuaqu.
4312: Pret. ocoTorTzuac, v. n. To wither, to wilt, speaking of plants. RR. cototzoa, uaqui.
4314: One that habitually withers, wilts, dries out. R. cototzuaqui.
4316: Pret. OCOTZAN: foot. RR. cotztli, ana.
4317: n. Hamstring, back of the knee. In comp. tocotzco, our hamstring, the hamstring in general. R. cotzoa. nino — to crouch; nino — to sprain the foot.
4319: n. v. One who runs with lightness, an agile runner. R. cotzoa.
4381: Gold. In comp. nocozticteocuitl, my gold. RR. coztic, teocuitlatl.
4382: Piece, fragment, sheet, grain, particle of gold. RR. cozticteocuitlatl, tlatemantli.
4383: Goldsmith who works gold. R. cozticteocuitlatl.
4387: Medicinal herb with a yellow flower (Hern.). RR. coztic, tomatl.
4388: Small bird of various colors resembling a canary (Hern., Clav.). RR. coztic, tototl.
4389: For words beginning thus, see under Qua. Grain of gold.
4394: See Coçaura. *CUCHILLOTENTLI or COCHILLOTENTLI, n. Blade edge of a knife. One also says cuchillo itenatca or itenitztica. RR. cuchillo, tentli. *CUCHILLOTZINQUAUHYOTL, n. Knife handle. One also says cuchillo itzitzquiloca; lit. the knife its graspable part. RR. cuchillo, tzinquauhyotl. *CUCHILLOUIA, pret. OCUCHILLOUI: nite— to stab someone with a knife. R. cuchillo.
4396: Impurity of water; edible herbs that grow in water.
4398: pret. to cross a stream, a puddle, etc.
4399: Pret. OCUECUAPATZ: nino— to make noise with one's skirts, said of a woman who
4402: adj. v. Humid, wet. R. cuechaua.
4403: Pret. OCUECHAUN: to be humid, somewhat wet. Nitla — to soften — to get wet, to moisten something.
4405: Pret. OCUECHILHUI: nitla — to grind, to crush something extremely finely for someone. R. cuechoa.
4406: Pret. OCUECHINI: to hurry, to hasten.
4407: adj. v. Fearful, frightened, terrified. RR. cuechoa, miqui. nino — to stir oneself,
4408: Pret. OCUECHO: nitla — to knead, to grind, to stir something extremely.
4409: Well ground, milled, kneaded. R. cuechoa.
4410: Pret. OCUECHTILI: to break an object into pieces, to make numerous fragments of it, to smash it completely. R. cuechoa.
4411: A kind of long shellfish. See Cuacaru.
4412: A bird of prey living near the shores of the Ocean (Hern.). RR. cuechtli, tototl. nitla — to put
4414: Pret. OCUECUECHCAC, freq. of cuechoa: ni — to tremble, to be afraid. [impers. tla-cuecuechca, one trembles, all are afraid (Olm.).
4415: Pret. OCUECUECHCAUH: to frighten someone; te-cuecuecheauh, he frightened the people — it is terrible, marvelous (Olm.). Nitla — to abandon something out of fear. RR. cuecuech, caua.
4416: Pret. OCUECUECHMICTI: nite — to pursue, to torment, to mistreat someone. R. cuecuechmiqui.
4417: Pret. OCUECUECHMIC: ni — to have a fatigued, tormented, preoccupied mind; to be epileptic. RR. cuecuechoa, miqui.
4418: Fatigue, preoccupation, torment of the mind; epilepsy, falling sickness. R. cuecuechmiqui.
4419: Pret. OCUECUEGHO, freq. of cuechoa: nite or nic — to frighten, to terrify someone. Nitla or nic — to grind something extremely.
4441: pret. ocuecuecorz (Car.), freq. of upon: nitla — to cause to bud, to cause to burst open.
4442: Pret. OCUECUEPTINEN: nino — to turn around, to be agitated, to be unable to stay in one place or to sleep. RR. cuecuepa, nemi.
4443: Pret. OCUECUEPTIA: nino — to roll on the ground, to turn over. R. cuecuepa.
4444: Pret. OGUECUETLACAC, v. n. freq. of cuetlani. To make a great deal of noise, to crackle, said of a flame.
4447: nitla — to brandish, to shake a lance or any other object.
4448: adj. v. Tormented, rough, uneven; cuecuetlanqui tepetl, land full of potholes, uneven terrain. R. cuecuetlania.
4450: nitla — to move, to brandish, to shake a lance or other thing.
4451: adj. v. Discouraged, downcast. R. cuecuetlaxiui.
4453: ni — to become discouraged, to lose courage.
4457: nino — to lose courage, to cease, to discontinue, to lack the strength to persevere. Nite — to cause someone to lose courage, to discourage them.
4459: Pret. OCUECUETZO, freq. of cuecuetzoa: nino — to become moved, to be troubled, to worry, to become agitated. Nita — to agitate, to stir something.
4502: Pret. OCUEPCAYOTILI: nitla — to use reprisals, to give something back to someone in return, to avenge an affront, to repel an insult with an insult. R. cuepcayotia.
4503: Return, tour, act of coming back. R. cuepa.
4504: Pret. OCUEPCATLAPALO: nitla — to return a greeting. RR. cuepa, tlapaloa.
4505: Pret. OCUEPILI: ninotla — to excuse oneself, to refuse to do something; oc ceppa ninotla-cuepilia, to refuse, to excuse oneself again. Nitetla — to take revenge, to return or restore what belongs to another; cuix ticte-cuepilizquia in temahuico, auh amo tic-chiuh? Were you to restore someone's honor, and did you not do it? Rev. of cuepa.
4506: Pret. OCUEPILTI: to use reprisals, to respond appropriately; to restore, to repay a loan; nicte-cuepilia in teaxca, to pay a — to take revenge, debt, to return to someone their money, their property, etc. Rev. of cuepa.
4507: Pret. OCUEPONALTI: nino — to esteem oneself; amino-cueponaltia (Olm.), to esteem oneself little. Nite — to make someone shine. Nitla — to cause a flower to grow, to cause it to bloom. R. cueponi.
4508: Pret. OGUEPONALTILI (Olm.): nite — to bestow benefits, favors upon someone; nech-cueponaltili in teotl (Olm.), God has showered me with his graces. R. cueponaltia.
4510: Blossoming of a flower, blooming, act of blooming. In comp. icueponca, its flower; naranjo icueponca, orange blossom, lemon blossom. R. cueponi.
4511: Pret. OCUEPONI, v. n. To make a noise, speaking of an egg or chestnut when cooked; to germinate, to sprout, to blossom, to shine, to burst; fig. in xochitl xotla, cueponi (Olm.), he is rich, who is in comfortable circumstances. Impers. tla-cueponi (Car.), everything buds, everything shines.
4512: Sound of something bursting, blossoming, etc. R. cueponi.
4513: Broken, bloomed, opened, resplendent, brilliant, dazzling. R. cueponi.
4514: Pret. OCUEPONTICACA, v. n. To appear white from a distance, to shine, to gleam, speaking of a house or building. RR. cueponi, icac.
4515: One of the districts of Mexico (Clav.). RR. cuepotli, pan.
4516: Pret. OGUEPOQUETZ: to construct, to raise a causeway. RR. cuepotli, quetza.
4519: Pret. OCUETI: nino — to be a lord, to be honored, esteemed (Olm.). R. cueitl.
4520: See tepanèque, conquered in 1425 by the Mexican king Itzcoatl (Clav.). RR. cue- tlachtli, tepal, c.
4521: Province on the Gulf of Mexico, between the province of Totonacapan and that of Coatzaqualco; capital of the same name (Clav.). RR. cuetlachtli, tlan.
4562: Top, covered with a haversack. R. cuezcomatl.
4563: Rim of a haversack. RR. cuezcomatl, tentli.
4564: Haversack, bread storehouse; top of the head, skull. In comp. nocuezcon, my skull; locuezcon, our skull, the skull in general. With the postpositions c, tlan: cuezcomac, in the haversack, in the granary; cuezcomatitlan, among the haversacks.
4565: Opening of a haversack (air vent of a granary). RR. cuezcomatl, æictli.
4566: Top of the head, of the hair, or the hair itself. In comp. tocuezconqua, our hair, hair in general; tocuezconqua ilacatztli, tuft of hairs or of hair. RR. cuezcomatl, quaitl.
4567: Town near Mexico City (Bét.). RR. cuezcomatl, tlan.
4568: Pret. oouic: mino — to esteem oneself, to consider oneself; teixtlam mo-cui, he is shameless, importunate in speech. Nite or nic — to receive someone, to have relations with a woman. Nitla or nic — to take a thing; nix nic-cui, to depreciate oneself, to recover one's face; tetech nic-cui, to take example from someone; nihio nic-cui or nic-ana, to catch one's breath, to be convalescent, to recover one's strength; can xic-cui, can æic-ana? where do you get, where do you take what is necessary? With the particle on: nocon-cui, to reach, to seize something elevated. Rev. cuilia, cuiltia, cuitia. Pass. cuihua or cuiua.
4569: Pret. ocu1: minotla — to take a borrowed thing, to appropriate it. Nitla — to wind, to wrap a thing. See Tecura.
4570: Pret. ocuicac: ni — to sing, to warble; amix, amonacaz æic-cuican, be prudent and wise; lit. sing to your face, to your ears. Pass. and impers. cuico.
4571: Collection, book of songs. RR. cuicatl, amatl.
4572: Pret. OGUICAAN: ni or nitla — to bring singers into agreement, to set the pitch, to intone, to lead, to conduct the singing. RR. cuicatl, ana.
4573: (Sah.). House of song, dance hall for youth. RR. cuicatl, calli, co.
4574: One who goes off-key, who disrupts the singing. R. cuicachalania.
4575: Pret. OGUICACHALANI: ni — to go off-key, to destroy the harmony. RR. cuicatl, chalania.
4576: Or CUICACHALANIQUI, n. v. One who goes off-key. R. cuicachalania.
4577: The act of destroying harmony, of breaking the singing. R. cuicachalania.
4580: etc.: ni — to compose a piece of song.
4522: Or CUITLACHTLI, n. Mexican wolf, also called xoloitzcuintli (Hern.); fig. a brave, fearless man (Olm.). Plur. cuecuetlachtin.
4523: Pret. OGUETLALHUI: nitla — to wither something. R. cuetlaui.
4524: Pret. OGUETLAN, v. n. To diminish, subside, calm down, speaking of an illness, the wind, etc.; to burn, to throw great flames; cuetlani, ocuetlan in eecatl, the wind calms down, has calmed down.
4525: Pret. OCUETLANI: nitla — to break wood or another long object. R. cuetlani.
4526: Noisy, crackling, speaking of fire; weakened, diminished, lessened, when referring to an illness. R. cuetlani.
4527: Withered, etiolated, faded, dejected. R. cuetlaui.
4528: Pret. OCUETLAUH (Olm.), v. n. To wither, to etiolate, to waste away, to decline; fig. ocuetlauh in quauitl (Olm.), he has fallen into decline.
4529: Pret. OCUETLAUIAG or OGUETLAUIX: nino — to wither, to fade easily. R. cuetlaui.
4530: That which ordinarily withers. R. cuetlauia.
4532: That which fades, withers easily. R. cuetlau.
4535: Pret. OCUETLAXYAMANI: nitla — to tan, to curry hides. RR. cuetlaxtli, yamania.
4537: Pret. OCUETLAXIUH: nino — to become discouraged, to be weak, without energy, to become careless, to let oneself be overcome by sadness.
4538: Discouragement, dejection, melancholy, sadness. R. cuetlaxiui.
4539: Discouraged, wounded at heart, sad, dejected. R. cuetlaxiui.
4541: Strap, cord, leather thong, etc. RR. cuetlaxtli, mecatl.
4542: Pret. OCUETLAXO: nino — to lose courage, to grieve, to be desolate. Nite — to cause someone to lose courage, to sadden, to drive to despair, to distress someone, etc.
4543: A Chichimec princess who married the Acolhua chief Acolhuatzin (Clav.). RR. cuetlaui, xochitl.
4544: Tanned hide, prepared leather. R. cuetlaziui.
4545: Pret. OCUETLAXUAUAN: M — to tan hides. RR. cuetlaxtli, uauana.
4547: n. v. Tanner, currier. R. cuetlaxuauana.
4548: Pret. OCUETLAXUI: a thing made with leather, with hide. R. cuetlaxtli.
4550: Stellion, small lizard. Cal. fourth day of the month and twelfth sign in judicial astrology (Sah., Clav.); nitla — to adorn; ye in ipan ni-tlacat cuetzpalin, I was born on the day of the lizard.
4552: (Olm.). One who has a skirt, a robe; fig. chief, governor, protector, etc. R. cuexantli.
4553: Pret. OGUEXANO: nitla — to carry a thing in one's robe. R. cuexantli. D — to eat:
4554: Skirt, robe, lap, the space between the waist and the knees. In comp. nocuexan, my skirt. With the postp. co: nocuexanco, in my skirts; tocuexanco, in our skirt, in our bosom; fig. in incuexanco, in immamalhuazco mo-tlalico in Teteoc (Chim.), it is thanks to them that Teleoc was established.
4555: Back of the neck, nape. In comp. nocuexcochteuh, my nape; tocuexcochteuh, our nape, the nape in general. RR. cuexcochtli, tetl.
4556: Pret. OGUEXCOCHTLAOCOX: to have quinsy. RR. cuexcochtli, tlaocoya.
4558: Nape, occiput, back of the head. In comp. nocuexcoch, my nape; tocuexcoch, our occiput, the occiput in general.
4559: A lock of hair that the Indians left on the occiput of children.
4584: n. v. Composer of song. R. cuicayocoya.
4586: n. v. One who goes out of tune, who disrupts the song. R. cuicaitlacoa.
4587: Pret. OGUICAITLACO: ni — to go out of tune, to sing off-key, to disrupt the song, to destroy the harmony. RR. cuicatl, itlacoa.
4590: v. Singer who intones, who sets the pitch. R. cuicaitoa.
4591: Pret. OGUICAITO: ni — to intone, to set the pitch. RR. cuicatl, itoa.
4592: Beginning of a song, the act of intoning, of setting the pitch. R. cuicaitoa.
4595: Pret. OGUICAMACAC: nite — to set the pitch for others, to intone. RR. cuicatl, maca.
4596: Musician, one who knows or is learning music. RR. cuicatl, mati.
4597: Pret. OGUICANAMICTI: nite — to set the pitch for others, to lead, to direct the song.
4599: Singer; cuicani tototl, a singing bird, one that warbles a great deal. R. cuica.
4600: n. v. Composer of song, of music. R. cuicapiqui.
4601: Pret. OCUICAPIC: ni — to compose a piece of song, of music. RR. cuicatl, piqui.
4604: Pret. OCUICATI: te — to give a concert, to make music for others. R. cuica.
4605: (Chim.). With or in song; in oqu-itlac ye tlatlacahui tlatzotzonaliztica, cuicatica (Chim.), when he saw that it was going badly in the music, in the song. RR. cuicatl, ca.
4606: Pret. OCUICATIUITZA (Par.): to come singing. RR. cuica, uitz.
4607: Song, music, chanson. In comp. nocuic, my song. R. cuica.
4608: Pret. OCUICATLAZ: ni — to intone, to give the pitch. RR. cuicatl, tlaça.
4609: The act of intoning, of giving the pitch. R. cuicatlaça.
4610: Composer of song, of music. R. cuicatlalia.
4611: Pret. OCUICATLALI: ni — to compose a song, music, etc. RR. cuicatl, tlalia.
4612: Science of song, musical art. RR. cuicatl, tlamatiliztli.
4616: Pret. OCUICATLAN: nite — to make someone sing, to tell them to sing. RR. cuicatl, tlani.
4617: Singer who intones, who gives the pitch. R. cuicatlaca. CUICENCA? adv. Is it then so? cuicenca aya temico tic-mati? Are you then as if numbed by sleep? cuicenca aya temico ti-tlachia? Are you then looking as if you were drowsy? RR. cuix, cenca.
4619: Pret. OCUICHEUH: nino — to blacken, to smoke. RR. cuichtli, eua.
4620: Covered with soot, having soot. R. cuichtli.
4643: Brother and successor of Cacamatzin on the throne of Acolhuacan, in 1520 (Clav.).
4644: pret. OGUICUIUI: mitla — to cheep, to squeak. GUITLAUILTIA R. cuica.
4651: Anus, buttock, backside. In comp. tocuilchil, our anus, the anus in general.
4652: pret. OCUILCHILQUIXTI: NO — to have hemorrhoids. RR. cuilchilli, quixtia.
4653: pret. OCuILHUI: wtella — to write, to paint something for someone. Rev. of cuiLoa.
4655: pret. OCUILI: micno — to take a thing, to appropriate it for oneself, to attribute it to oneself. Mtetla or nicte — to take, to remove a thing from someone; miquinte-cuilia in totoltin (Olm.), I take someone's hens; niquintla-cuilia in maceualtin (Olm.), I take a thing from the subjects; niquin-cuilia in maceualtin in intotolhuan (Olm.), I take the subjects' hens from them. Pass. cuililo: nitla-cuililo (Olm.), something is taken from me; on-cuililoc ce amail (Car.), a book was stolen from me; ote-cuililor ce quaquahue (Par.), an ox was stolen from someone. Rev. of eur.
4657: pret. OCUILILTI: to value a thing greatly, to exaggerate its price.
4660: pret. OCUILO: nilla — to write, to paint something; pani nitla-cuiloa, to write on top. Rev. cuilhuia or cuilhuilia. nüla — to outbid, to raise the price—
4662: Illicit sexual relations between men, sodomy. R. cuiloni.
4482: pret. OCUELPACHO: to line clothes or other things; oc ceppa nitla- nitla — to fold, cuelpachoa, to redouble a thing. RR. cueloa, pachoa.
4484: : to plow, to dig, to make furrows. RR. cuematlauhth, chiua.
4485: Furrow, plot, garden bed of earth. RR. cuemul, atlauhtli.
4486: Property, plowed land, garden bed, plot of earth. In comp. nocuen, my field.
4487: pret. OGUENCHIUH: ni — to plow, to work the land. RR. cuemtl, chiua.
4488: Plowing, agriculture, the art of cultivating the land. R. cuenchiualizthi.
4489: n. v. Work of the land, plowing. R. cuenchiua.
4490: n. v. Gardener, cultivator, plowman, peasant. R. cuenchiua.
4491: pret. OCUENCHOLHUI: nitla — to cross over a garden bed, a stream, a ditch, etc. RR. cuemitl, cholhuia.
4492: pret. OGUENTATACAC: nitla — to make beds or furrows, to prepare, to work the land for sowing. RR. cuemitl, tataca. CUENTAXTLI, n. Rosary beads, prayer beads. RR. cuenta, act.
4493: pret. OGUENTECAC: mo — to have garden beds made for oneself. Nitla — to make garden beds, to work the land. RR. cuenutl, teca.
4496: pret. OCUEOLOLO: nino — to tuck up one's skirts, petticoats. Nite or nic — to tuck up, to lift the dress or skirts of a woman. RR. cueitl, ololoa.
4497: pret. OOUEP: nino — to return, to go back; yuhquin tetl mo-cuepa iyollo, he is obstinate, hardened; lit. his heart is, becomes like a stone; mo-cuepatiuh, he goes or will go to take revenge. Nite — to make someone return, to bring someone back, to escort them back. Notla or nic — to turn over, to change something; to explain oneself, to make excuses, not to go along with; to appeal, to translate, to decant, etc.; qualli ipan nic-cuepa, to put things on the right side, in the best place, to place them well; çan tlein ipan nic-cuepa, to place a thing badly; nic-cuepa in notlatol or in notlatlalil, to report, to change a judgment, a sentence, a decree; tepan nitla or nic-cuepa, to put the blame on someone, on the one who accuses, etc.; to take revenge for an injury, to apply the law of retaliation, to repay what was done to us; nic-cuepa tlatolli, to translate from one language into another. Rev. cuepilia, cuepiltia (Olm.).
4498: n. v. Used in comp. nocuepca, my return, my tour; icuepca mo-chiuhtica, adv. expr., on the contrary. R. cuepa.
4499: pret. OCUEPCACHIUH: nitla — to do something badly, crookedly, in reverse. RR. cuepa, chiua.
4500: pret. OCUEPCAYOAC, v. n. To be paid, rendered, or restored, speaking of a thing. R. cuepa.
4501: pret. OCUEPCAYOTI: nitla or nic — to use reprisals, to give or send something in return; to pay back, to repay in kind. R. cuepcayoa.
4683: Action of lowering oneself, of withdrawing. R. cuitlacaxæoa.
4684: adj. v. Lowered, bent, who strives to avoid blows. R. cuitlacaxxoa.
4685: Pret. OCUITLACHIUI: nite — to accuse someone, to affront them, to cause them an undeserved harm.
4687: An ear of corn that is bad, poorly developed, different from the others. RR. cuitlatl, cochi.
4688: adj. v. Blighted, dry, withered. RR. cuitlatl, cochi.
4689: Pret. OCUITLACOYONI: to make a hole in something, such as a house, a barrel,
4690: a sack, etc. RR. cuitlatl, coyonia.
4695: adj. v. Lazy, careless, soft, negligent. R. cuitlacotlaua.
4696: Pret. OCUITLAÇOTLAUAC: to lack courage, to be without strength, to let oneself be beaten down, to be lazy, negligent. RR. cuitlatl, cotlaua.
4697: adj. v. Lazy, soft, negligent. R. cuitlaçotlaua.
4700: : ni — to walk without courage, with nonchalance. RR. cuitlaçotlaua, nemi.
4702: Pret. OCUITLACPEUH: nitla — to defile a thing; fig. to sow discord, to spread disunion (Olm.). RR. cuitlatl, peua.
4664: Pret. OCUILONTI: nie — to maintain a sinful relationship, to be a sodomite. R. cuiloni.
4666: Pret. OCUILTONO: nino — to have many goods, to be rich, to live in abundance, to rejoice greatly. Ninotla or nieno — to enjoy, to take pleasure in something. Nite — to enrich someone, to make them happy; in tecuiltono (Olm.), he who consoles and gladdens. Rev. cuiltonotzinoa: timo-cuiltonotzinoz, you will rejoice; ticmo-cuiltonotzinoz in Dios (Par.), you will enjoy the sight of God.
4667: Pret. OCUILTONOLHUI: to enrich someone. R. cuiltonoa.
4668: Pret. OGUILTONOLLAN: nino — to desire fortune, well-being; otito-cuiltonollanque, we desired to be rich and happy. RR. cuil- ninote — tonoa, tlani.
4670: n. (Olm.). Master, leader of song. Plural: cuiqueque. R. cuicatl.
4671: Pret. OCUIQUEUR: nino — to sing a poem in one's own praise. Nite — to sing of someone's deeds. Nitla — to sing something. RR. cuicatl, eua.
4672: Pret. OCUITI: nicno — to acknowledge someone as one's master, one's lord; to confess, to admit a fault; tiemo-cuitia in motlatlacol (Par.), you confess your sins; anicno-cuitia, to deny something. Rev. of cui.
4673: Pret. OCUITIQUIZ: nitla or nice — to take, remove, or seize something in great haste, with precipitation. RR. cui, quiça.
4676: Pret. OCUITIUETZ: nino — to turn, to overturn oneself; to be startled, to be frightened by a noise, thunder, etc. Nite or nic — to attack, to throw oneself upon someone; to hold back someone who is about to fall; to seduce, to violate a young girl, etc. Nitla or nic — to take, to seize, to steal something hastily; qui-cuitiuetz in ueuetl (Chim.), he quickly seized the drum. Rev. cuihituetzi (Par.), cuitiuechilia. Pass. cuitiuecho. RR. cui, uetzi.
4677: Pret. OCUITIUETZIZNEC: nite — to want to seize someone, to try to restrain them. RR. cuitiuetzi, nequi.
4678: n. Latrines, privy. RR. cuitlatl, calli.
4679: Pret. OCUITLACAPAN, v. n. To fester, to cause pain, to ripen, to form, speaking of a tumor; ye cuitlacapaniz, the tumor is about to burst, the pus is on the point of coming out. RR. cuitlatl, capani.
4680: Loins. In comp. tocuitlacaxiuhyan, our loins, the loins in general.
4682: Pret. OCUITLACAXXO: to stoop down on account of the lashes of a whip one receives.
4360: pret. ocozTIAC, etc.: ni — to become yellow, to turn yellow.
4361: Yellow; coztic teocuitlacoualoni, gold coin; coztic patli, coztic xihuitl, medicinal plants (Hern.). R. coztia.
4362: Yellow; used in comp. icoztica; totoltetl icoztica, egg yolk. R. coztia.
4363: Yellow, a yellow object, the state of that which is yellow. In comp. icozticayo. R. coztia.
4364: adj. Yellowish-green, when speaking of bile. RR. coztic, alauac.
4366: adj. v. Yellow, golden, brilliant. RR. coztic, petzoa.
4367: Cornarina mexicana (Hern.). A yellow stone to which the Indians attributed the property of curing heart ailments. RR. coztic, tecpatl.
4368: Large gold necklace. RR. cozticteocuitlatl, cozcapetlatl.
4369: Necklace, gold jewel. RR. cozticteocuitlatl, cozcatl.
4371: : ni — to assay gold, to hallmark it. R. cozticteocuitlamachiyotl.
4372: n. Carat, hallmark of gold. RR. cozticteocuitlatl, machiyotl.
4374: Alloy, mixture with gold. RR. cozticteocuitlatl, neloa.
4376: n. v. Goldsmith who works with gold. RR. cozticteocuitlatl, pitza.
4377: n. Slag, dross of gold. RR. cozticteocuitlatl, popogoquillotl.
4378: n. v. Gold mine. RR. cozticteocuitlatl, quixtia, yan.
4704: Pret. OCUITLACUEP to recoil, to turn one's back. RR. cuitlatl, cuepa. : nino — to flee,
4705: situated on a small island in Lake Chalco (Clav.).
4706: where CUITLAHUATZIN, son of King Axayacatl, was first lord of Iztapalapan, then king of Mexico (Clav.).
4709: Pret. OCUITLAYOAG: » — to cover oneself with filth, with refuse, to befoul oneself. R. cuitlatl.
4710: Pret. OCUITLALPI: Nino — to gird oneself, to put on a belt, to bind one's loins. Nite- or nic — to gird someone; nic-cuitlalpia in cauallo, to girth a horse. RR. cuitlatl, ilpia.
4711: Belt, girth. In comp. icuitlalpica, his belt; cauallo icuitlalpica, horse girth. R. cuitlalpia.
4712: (Clav.). Governor of the coast near Vera Cruz who, together with Teuhtlile, welcomed Hernán Cortés.
4713: Large grey lion resembling a wolf (Hern.). RR. cuitlachtli, miztli.
4714: Soft, lazy, negligent. RR. cuitlatl, momotzoa.
4715: Softness, laziness, negligence. RR. cuitlatl, momotzoa.
4716: adj. v. Soft, lazy, negligent. RR. cuitlatl, nana.
4718: pret. — to walk slowly, with extreme noncha-
4719: : lance, to be soft, lazy. R. cuitlananaca.
4720: adj. v. Very fat, fleshy. R. cuitlananaca.
4721: n. freq. of the following. Great stoutness, extreme fatness.
4724: Pret. OGUITLANEXPOLO: MINO — to roll in filth; in yuh coyametl mo-cuitla-nexpoloa (Olm.), like the pig, it wallows in filth. RR. cuilatl, nextli, poloa.
4725: Pret. OCUITLAPAMMAUNTI: nino — to be frightened of something, speaking of a woman. RR. cuitlapantli, mauhtia.
4726: n. Back, spine of an animal. RR. cuitlapantli, mimiliuhcayotl.
4727: Pret. OCUITLAPAMPETLAUH: nino — to uncover oneself, to bare the shoulders. RR. cuitlapanthi, petlaua.
4728: Worn out, having strained loins, a broken spine. R. cuitlapam-purtequi.
4729: Pret. OCUITLAPAMPUZTEC: nino — to dislocate, to break one's loins. Mite — to break someone's body, to break their loins.
4731: Cesspool, latrine; lazy, negligent. See Cuircars.
4733: Back, spine of the animal. RR. cuitlapantli, ciyotcayotl (?).
4734: (Olm.). One who has shoulders; fig. wel cuitlapane, chief, governor, support, etc. RR. cuitla- R. cuitlapantli.
4735: Back, spine of the animal. RR. cuitlapanili, quauhyotl.
4736: Pret. OCUITLAPANQUAUUTIC: ni — to be worn out, to have back pain, a pain in the shoulders or back. RR. cuitlapantli, quauhti.
4737: Spine, backbone. In comp. tocuilapanteputzchichiquil, our spine, the spine in general. RR. cuitlapanthi, teputztli, chichiquilh.
4738: Afflicted with pain in the loins or shoulder. RR. cuilapantli, tlatla.
4739: Pain in the loins or shoulder. RR. cuitlapantli, tlatla.
4740: n. See. Pain in the loins or shoulder. RR. cuitlapantli, tleualani.
4741: Ill, suffering from the loins, etc. RR. cuitlapantli, tleualani.
4742: n. Shoulder, back, spine, loins. In comp. nocuitlapan, my spine, my shoulders; nocuitlapan tlatla, tleualani or toneua, my shoulders hurt me; tocuitlapan, our shoulders, shoulders in general; icuilapan, his back, his shoulders. With the postposition pa: cuitlapampa, behind, from behind; nocuitlapampa nic-tlaça, I put it behind me, I reject it, I want nothing to do with it; tocuitlapampa, toward our shoulders, on our shoulders, behind in general; tlacuitlapampa, behind something. RR. euitlatl, pan.
4743: adj. v. Sick, suffering from kidney/lower back pain. RR. cuitlapantli, toneua.
4744: Pain in the lower back/kidneys. RR. cuitlapantli, toneua.
4745: Tuft of hair between the shoulders. RR. cuitlapantli, tzontli.
4746: Pret. OCUITLAPANUI: nite — To be, to come behind someone; te-cuitlapanuia ehecatl, a tailwind, wind coming from behind. Nitla — to fall behind; used both lit. and fig. R. cuitlapantli.
4747: Pret. OCUITLAPANUITEC: nite — to exhaust someone, to strike them on the shoulders.
4749: Back, spine of an animal. RR. cuitlapantli, cilotl.
4750: Pret. OCUITLAPECHO: ni — to pull back the body, to crouch, to move away, said of someone who is being flogged. RR. cuitlatl, pechoa.
4751: The act of withdrawing, moving away, or escaping blows. R. cuitlapechoa.
4752: Withdrawn, crouched, striving to avoid blows. R. cuitlapechoa.
4753: A very common small fish in the lakes of Mexico (Hern.).
4754: or CUITLAPITZ, adj. v. Obese, pot-bellied, fat. RR. cuitlatl, metzoa.
4755: Pret. OCUITLAPILAYACACHCO: nino — to wag, to shake the tail, said of a dog or any other animal. RR. cuitlapilli, ayacachoa.
4756: Pret. OCUITLAPILAN: nite or nitla — to cut, remove, or pull off the tail of an animal.
4758: Mouse, rat; lit. one who has a long tail. RR. cuitlapilli, ueyac.
4759: Pret. OCUITLAPILHUIUITLAC: nitla or nic — to pluck the rump of a bird, to pull out its tail feathers. RR. cuitlapilli, uiuitla.
4760: Pret. OCUITLAPILLAZ: nino — to wag, to shake the tail, said of an animal.
4762: Pret. OCUITLAPILLALAZ, freq. of the preceding: nino — to wag, to shake the tail repeatedly, said of an animal.
4763: Tail of an animal, of a bird; fig. a coarse man, peasant, laborer; cuitlapilli atlapalh, vassal, common people. In comp. mocuitlapil, rev. mocuitlapiltzin, your tail. RR. cuitlatl, pill.
4764: pret. OCUITLAPILTEC: nitla — to cut off, to tear off the tail of an animal.
4766: Pret. OCUITLAPILTI: to put on a tail; to crack open, to split, to burst, speaking of a pit or seed; fig. mo-cuitlapiltia nino — (Olm.), he is rich, prosperous, or possesses what is necessary. R. cuitlapilli.
4768: Pret. OCUITLAPUZTEC: nite — to break the spine, the back of someone. RR. cuitlatl, puztequi.
4772: Province situated between the State of Michuacan to the north and the Pacific Ocean to the south; capital Mexcaltepec (Clav.).
4773: Inhabitant of the province of Cuitlatecapan. Plural: Cuitlateca.
4774: Belly, stomach, crop. In comp. tocuitlatecon, our belly, the belly in general. RR. cuitlatl, tecomatl.
4775: dim. of the preceding. Small belly, stomach, crop. Mol. gives cuitlatecontli.
4776: Pret. OCUITLATECPICHAUE: (v.) — to have colic, intestinal pain. RR. cuitlatl, tecpichaui.
4778: One who suffers from colic. RR. cuitlatl, tepitzaui.
4779: Pret. OCUITLATETECUICAC, v.n. To cause great pain, speaking of a wound, a tumor, etc. RR. cuitlatl, tetecuica.
4780: Spine, backbone. In comp. tocuitlatelepun, our spine, the spine in general. RR. cuitlatl, tetepuntli.
4782: — to walk very slowly, listlessly, negligently, with extreme laziness. v. Colic.
4843: pret. OCULHUI: nilella — to lead someone by detours to some place, to make circuits in order not to pass through a place where someone is. R. culua.
4846: See Corzrere, and likewise for words beginning in the same manner. E, a suffix indicating the vocative or marking possession: nopiltzin, my son; nopiltzine, and by syncope nopillze, to my son; cihuatl, woman; cihuatle, to a woman; pl. cihuae, to women; — tlaolli, maize; ilaolle, master, possessor of maize; voc. tlaollee; etc. E, in numeration, is used for ei. See the latter word. As an adv., e stands in place of ye: im manele (for üm manel ye), although; im manele nech-apizmic-tizque, ca yeppa 12 nino-cahuaz (Car.), though they make me die of hunger, I will nonetheless remain here. E, reduplicative vowel in frequentatives: eeço, covered in blood; n-eeua, to flee, to move away continually; eexcan, in each of three parts; etc.
4847: Fly-whisk, fly-swatter, fan. RR. ecatl, ceua.
4848: adj. That which brings the storm; ecacoayomixtli, storm cloud, waterspout. R. ecacoatl.
4849: Waterspout; a kind of rattlesnake (Hern.). RR. ecatl, coatl.
4850: Vein. In comp. yecayo, its vein; tell yecayo, vein of stone. R. ecatl.
4852: Long conversation; muzzle, bridle. In comp. yecamecayo, its bridle; yecamecayo cauallo, horse bridle. R. ecamecatl.
4853: Long and wide-ranging conversation on various subjects. RR. ecatl, mecatl.
4854: Wood, place planted with laurels. RR. ecapatli, quauhtla.
4855: Laurel; medicinal plant, parva sambucus (Hern.), to which various names were given.
4857: Air, breeze, light, cool and gentle wind. RR. ecatl, peyactli (?).
4858: pret. OECAPEUI: to swat flies, to fan oneself, to make air for oneself. nite — to fan someone. RR. ecatl, peuia.
4860: pret. OECAQUETZ: to throw, to expose a thing to the wind. RR. ecatl, quetza. nin (for nino) — nitla — to winnow, to ripen a thing.
4861: pret. OCUXILTI: nila — something. R. icuci.
4862: pret. OCUXITI: to cook a thing, to prepare a dish. R. icuci. nitla — to ripen, to bring to maturity.
4805: Softness, nonchalance, laxity, laziness, negligence. R. cuitlatzolli.
4806: Pret. OCUITLATZOLTITINEN: ni — to go softly, slowly, with nonchalance.
4808: Or CUITLATZUNTLI, n. Tuft of hair. RR. cuitlatl, tzontli.
4809: Pret. OCUITLAUI: mino — to invite oneself to a meal. Minote or nicno — to take care of someone; quimo-cuitlauia in nopilizin (Par.), they take care of my son; with the particle on: nimitzonno-cuitlauia (Car.), I take care of you; niquimonno-cuitlauia (Car.), I take care of them. Ninotla — to take care of a thing, to look after it; quimo-cuitlauia, he takes care of a thing, or one who tends, a chamberlain. Pass. necuitlauilo: necuitlauilozque in nopilhuan (Par.), my sons will be cared for. Impers. nelecuitlauilo or netlacuitlauilo.
4811: Pret. OCUITLAUILTI: to advise, lead, compel someone, to seduce; nitla — to smoke the nile or nice — to violate. Nicte — to press, provoke, compel someone to do or give something. R. cuitlauia.
4812: Pret. OCUITLAUITEC: nite — to break someone's back. RR. cuitlatl, uitequi.
4813: In comp. nocuitlaxayac, my hips; tocuitlaxayac, our hips, buttocks. Referring to the hips, the hips in general. RR. cuitlatl, xayacatl. *CUITLAXAYATL, n. A small, thin garment (manta delgada ó que cuelgan della algunas correas). Small mantle, garment. RR. cuitlatl, xaya.
4814: Pret. OCUITLAXAPOTLAC: nitla — to pierce, make a hole in something. RR. cuitlatl, xapotla.
4815: A small village in the very place where the Spanish founded, in 1531, Puebla de los Ángeles (Clav.). RR. cuitlaxcoli, apan.
4816: Pret. OCUITLAXCOLCOCOYAC: ni — to have, to experience intestinal pains. RR. cuitlaxcolli, cocoya.
4817: Suffering from intestinal pains. R. cuitlaxcolcocoya.
4818: Pret. OCUITLAXCOLICOYOCAC: ni — to have intestinal pains, to make noise, speaking of the intestines. RR. cuitlaxcolli, icoyoca.
4819: Glutton, gourmand, one who is all belly. RR. cuitlaxcolli, tlaueliloc.
4821: Intestines, bowels, entrails, belly. In comp. nocuitlaxcol, my entrails; tocuitlaxcol, our entrails, the entrails in general; nocuitlaxcol mo-cocoa, I have intestinal pains; nocuitlaxcol choca or tecoyoa, I have borborygmi, my belly rumbles. RR. cuitlatl, ... (?).
4822: (Olm.). That which concerns the entrails, the intestines; fig. principle of generation, chief, lord, governor. R. cuitlaxcolli.
4884: conj. Nevertheless, however; used in comparatives and superlatives: ece ye qualh y (Olm.), that is better; ece oc tlapanauia inic qualli y (Olm.), that is very good.
4885: or EHCO, pret. oEcoc, etc.: n (for ni) — ehcoque (Chim.), to arrive (legar, used in tierra caliente, Par.); inic n (for ni) — ehcoque (Chim.), when they arrived.
4886: Bloody, covered with blood. R. ezth.
4887: pret. oEçoac, etc.: n (for ni) — to cover oneself with blood. See — to be a relative of someone; te-eçoa (Olm.), he is of someone's blood, he is their relative. RR. ezthi, çoa.
4888: pret. oEcori, etc.: nin (for nino) — to cover oneself with blood. nitla — to cover something with blood. R. eçoa.
4889: Blood of man. In comp. neço or nezço (for no-eço, etc.), my blood, the blood that flows in my veins; fig. teeço tetlapallo, son or daughter of great lords; lit. the blood, the color of someone; mimegço (for nimo-eço), I am your son. R. ezth. ECUE! (Olm.), interj. to express complaint, pain.
4890: Pret. oECUEN: n (for ni) — to prepare plots of earth, a field for sowing beans, etc. R. ecuemutl.
4894: pret. oecac, etc., v. n. To blow, to be windy, speaking of the wind. R. eecatl.
4895: Pret. oEEGACHICHIN: n (for ni) — to inhale air; to soar, to take flight again, speaking of a bird. RR. eecatl, chichina.
4896: n. (Clav.). Rattlesnake, so named because of the speed of its movements. RR. eecatl, coatl. n (for ni) — to sneeze.
4897: Full of wind, stormy, tempestuous; eecayo quiyauitl, storm, rain with strong wind. R. eecatl.
4899: Headwind, contrary wind. RR. eecatl, ixnamiquiliatli.
4900: Pret. oEECANAMICTI: m (for mo) — to be assailed by the wind, by the storm; m-eecanamictia tacal, the storm assails our vessel.
4902: freq. of EcateyacTli. Air, breeze, gentle, fresh, light wind.
4784: See OCUITLATEXCALHUAC: ni — to have colic. RR. cuitlatl, texcalhuaqui.
4788: Pret. OCUITLATITICAC, v. n. To cause suffering, speaking of an infected wound or an abscess forming; noyollo cuitlatitica, my heart is sick, my heart hurts.
4789: One who suffers from a wound or an abscess. R. cuitlatitica.
4790: n. Pain caused by an infected wound or by a forming abscess. R. cuitlatitica.
4791: Excrement, dung, filth, residue; wound, tumor, abscess; fig. fault. In comp. nocuitl, my excrement; mocuitl, icuitl, etc.; lztacteocuitlatl icuitl, silver dross; quaquaue icuitl, dung, cow dung. With the postposition pan: cuitlapan, in the filth, behind, in one's absence; cuitlapan ychan (Olm.), slave, or one who leads a coarse, brutish life; icuitlapan in tepetl (Olm.), behind the mountain; tecuitlapan, behind people, in someone's absence or in the absence of others; tecuitlapan nite-chicoitoa (Par.), I speak ill of others in their absence; tlacuitlapan, behind a thing. See Cuitlapanrui.
4792: Pret. OCUITLATLAZ: nite — to deliver someone from evil, from servitude. RR. cuitlatl, tlaça.
4794: augm. Very large, thick, enormous. RR. cuitlatl, tolontic.
4795: Pret. OCUITLATZAQUILI: nite — to atone for the faults of others. RR. cuitlatl, tzacuilia.
4796: Pret. OCUITLATZAYAN: ni — to burst through the belly; yuhquin cuitlatzayani noyollo, to be seized with great fright; lit. my heart tears apart like entrails. RR. cuitlatl, tzayani.
4797: freq. of the following. Very lazy, negligent.
4802: adj. Lazy, soft, negligent, careless; nicuitlatzol, I am lazy, negligent. R. cuitlaxoa.
4903: Pret. OEECAPOLIUH: to disperse, dissipate, destroy, speaking of the wind; eecapoliui im mixth, the wind dissipates the clouds. RR. eecatl, poliui.
4904: on the northern shore of the lagoon of Tetzcuco (Clav.). RR. eecatl, tepetl, c.
4905: With wind, with air. RR. eecatl, ca.
4906: freq. of Ecatl. Wind, air; uei eecatl, strong wind, storm, squall, whirlwind; eecatl niqu-ittitia, to air something, to expose it, to put it in the wind; eecatl lopam mo-quelza, to weather a storm; lit. the wind rises upon us. Cal. second day of the month (Clav.). In comp. yeecauh, his wind; fig. ye nican uitz in yeecauh (Olm.), already his punishment comes here.
4907: Pret. oecarTococ, freq. of EcaAToco: (for ni) — to be carried away, lifted up by the wind.
4908: (Clav.). Third age of the world, sun of wind, of air. RR. eecatl, tonatiuh.
4909: pl. dim. of eecatl. (Sah., Clav.). Small idols or images of children made for the feast of the mountains in the month of tepeilhuitl.
4911: : (for ni) — to shelter oneself, to protect oneself from the wind.
4912: One of the seven chiefs who led the Toltecs from Ueue Tlapallan to Tollantzinco (Clav.). R. eecatl.
4913: Chief of Tlatelulco, who was condemned to punishment by Axayacatl (Clav.). RR. eecatl, taitzimitl.
4914: Pret. OEECAUATZ, freq. of ECAUATZA: nitla — to place, to expose something to the air.
4916: Pret. OEECAUILOC: to weather a gust of wind, a storm at sea; t-een (for ni) — cauilo (for ti-eecauilo), you are exposed or we are exposed to the storm, we are weathering a storm; ot-eecauiloque, we have endured, weathered the storm. R. eecatl.
4917: freq. of ecaxoctiui. Air, gentle, fresh, light breeze.
4918: adj. freq. of eço. Full of, covered with blood.
4919: Pret. ogeçoac, freq. of eço: (for ni or nino) — to cover oneself with blood.
4920: Pret. ogeçoti, freq. of EÇOTIA: nitla — to cover something with blood.
4921: freq. of el, used only with the possessives no, mo, i, etc.: neel, I am diligent, active, eager; meel, yeel, teel or teeltin, ameel or ameeltin, ymeel or yeeltin. With negation, one says: aneel, I am lazy; ameel, you are lazy; etc.
4922: Pret. OELELEUI, freq. of ELEUIA: nino (for nino) or nitla — to tyrannize; m-eeleuia, he tyrannizes or one who tyrannizes. See MEELEUIANI.
4823: Small intestine, thin, long intestines. RR. cuitlaszcoll, pitzactli.
4824: n. v. Gurgling, borborygmi. RR. cuitlaxcolli, tecoyoca. The belly rumbles.
4827: Pret. OGUITLAXELO: nite — To throw oneself, rush, push in, slip in among others. RR. cuitlatl, æeloa.
4830: Pret. OCUITLAXITIN: Ni — To burst through the belly. RR. cuitlatl, xitini.
4831: Pret. OCUITLAXO: nitla — To soften, moisten a thing.
4832: adj. v. Heavy, very weighty. R. cuitlaxoa.
4838: A kind of bread that the Indians offered to idols. CUIX? adv. often followed by ma. By chance? is it that? cuix titlaquaznequi? (Car.), do you want to eat? cuix ma omomiquili in notalzin? (Car.), is your father dead? cuix aca? is it someone? cuix ace or aço ye? who knows? who is it? oquimecahuitecque ce tlacatl, cuix ace itoca Pedro? (Car.), they flogged a man, who knows if his name is Peter? cuix amo? is it not? cuix çan? is it then? cuix ça ça tlan? is it something? cuix ça oc? by chance again? perhaps still? is it still? cuix ila? is it something? cuix mo? cuix monel? is it not? cuix mo or monel n-iaz? shall I not go? cuix mo, without interrogation: no; a cuix? when? at what time? how long? Preceded by in, cuix ceases to be interrogative and marks doubt, uncertainty: ca yehuantin in cuix teciuhtlazque teciuhpehuique, ihuan in aquique in cuix quinnolza in ahuaque in tlaloque, niman ye in cuix nanahualtin, tlahuipochtin (Car.), these are those called exorcists, or those who are in contact with the water gods, or those who profess to be sorcerers, magicians.
4839: (Aub.), n. Kite [bird of prey]. R. cw.
4863: month. With the postposition ipac: ecatipac, high in the air.
4864: A type of spider, also called ocelotocall (Hern.). RR. ecatl, tocatl.
4865: pret. OECATOCOC: pushed, carried away by the wind; yuhquin n-ecatoco, (n for ni) — to run lightly, I go as if carried away by the wind. Wind, air. Cal. second day of the [calendar]. RR. ecatl, toca.
4866: Carried away, lifted by the wind; yuhquin ecatoconi, a light runner; lit. as if lifted by the wind. R. ecatoco.
4867: A type of bird of which several species are distinguished (Hern.). RR. ecatl, tototl.
4868: pret. OECATZACUILI: nitla (n for ni) — to shelter a thing, to protect it from the wind. nino (nin for nino) — to take shelter from the wind. nite — to shelter someone from the wind. R. ecatzaqua.
4869: pret. OECATZACU: nitla (n for ni) — to shelter a thing, to protect it from the wind, to stop the wind, to act as a barrier against the wind. RR. ecatl, tzaqua.
4870: pret. OECAUATZ: nitla — to expose, to put a thing in the open air, to dry it. RR. ecatl, uatza.
4871: n. Portable wooden ladder, etc. R. ecauatza.
4872: adj. and n. That which gives shade, that has shade; fig. chief, protector, guardian, father, mother, etc. (Olm.). R. ecauhyotl.
4873: pret. OECAUHYOTI: nitla — to shade a thing. R. ecauhyotl.
4874: pret. OECAUHYOTICAYA: to have shade, to be in the shade. RR. ecauhyotl, ca.
4875: pret. OECAUHYOTICACA, v. n. To cast shade, to give shadow, to shade, speaking of a tree. RR. ecauhyotl, icac.
4877: pret. OECAUI: nino (nin for nino) — to invite oneself; ni (n for ni) — to a meal. nite — to bring someone along. R. eco.
4878: pret. OECAUILI: to put in the shade. R. ecaui (?).
4879: pret. OECAUILLOTI: nino (nin for nino) — to put oneself in the shade. nitla — to put a thing in the shade, to shade it. R. ecauillotl.
4880: Shadow, shade; with the postposition tlan: ecauillotitlan, in the shade, etc. R. ecauihia.
4881: adj. and n. (Olm.). That which gives shade; fig. governor, chief, protector. R. ecauillotl.
4882: n. Air, breeze, gentle wind, light and cool wind.
4983: Pret. ELLELACITINEN (Olm.): # (for ni) — to be in distress, to live in sadness, in grief. RR. ellelaci, nemi.
4984: Pret. OELLELAXITI: to cause distress, to cause grief to someone, nite — to set, give a bad example (Olm.). R. ellelaci.
4985: Pret. OELLELCEUL: someone who is irritated. RR. ellelli, cewa.
4986: (Olm.). Pain, grief. In comp. nellel (for no-ellel), my pain; mellel (for mo-ellel), your suffering. R. elleloa.
4987: Pret. OFLLELMACHITI: to make someone angry, to make them cross, to irritate them. R. ellelmati.
4988: Pret. OFLLELMA: min (for nino) — nite — to calm; nie — to feel disgust for something, to receive grief from it. RR. ellell, mat.
4989: Pret. OELLELO: nitla — to give, procure, cause grief.
4990: Pret. OELLELQUIZ: n (for ni) — to rejoice, to recreate oneself; to experience, to have great grief; n-ellelquiça in ni-choca, to weep greatly, to feel keen grief. RR. ellelli, quiça.
4991: Pret. OELLELQUIXTI: nino — to recreate oneself, to rest; nepantla tonatiuh nin-ellelquixtia (for nin), to take a nap, a midday rest, to rest at midday; tetech nin-ellelquixtia, to stop, to restrain someone's anger. Nite — to recreate, to delight someone. RR. ellelli, quixtia.
4992: Pret. OELLELTEN: % (for #) — to be overwhelmed with grief, with pain. RR. ellelh, temi.
4993: Tormented, grieved, deeply afflicted. R. elleltemi.
4994: Pret. OELLELTENTICATCA: n (for ni) — to be overwhelmed with grief, with pain, to experience great suffering. RR. elleltemi, ca.
4995: Pret. OELLELTI: min (for nino) — to repent, to feel regret, to restrain oneself, to abstain, etc.; tit-elleltia (for tito-elleltia), we do not agree, we do not get along; with the particle on: oc achi ic ou oc itla ic nonn-elleltia, to be hindered, stopped by obstacles, difficulties, etc. Nite — to dissuade someone, to prevent them from doing something; itla nech-elleltia, the thing prevents me. R. elleloa.
4996: Liver, chest, stomach. In comp. nel (for no-el), my liver; yel, his/her liver; tel (for to-el), our liver, our chest, the liver, the chest in general; eel, someone's liver. With the postp. pan: telpan, on the chest.
4999: n. Epiploon, membrane that envelops the intestines. In comp. telmatl (for to-elmatl), our epiploon, the epiploon in general. RR. elli, matlatl.
5001: Pret. OELMIMIC: to stutter, to stammer. RR. elli, miqui.
5002: Impers. of the irreg. verb ca (Olm.).
5003: Leaf, husk of the green ear of corn. RR. elotl, izhuatl.
5004: erba peniculata (Hern.). Medicinal plant used against pleurisy or matlal- n (for ni) — to be totonqui. RR. elotl, quiltic.
5005: Ear of green corn whose kernels are nonetheless formed.
5006: Leaf, husk of the still-green ear of corn. RR. elotl, totomochth.
5007: Bird of which several species are known, living mainly at the time of the corn harvest; hence its name (Hern.). RR. elotl, tototl.
5008: Plant whose root is used as a remedy (Hern.). RR. elotl, æochutl.
5009: Pret. OELPAMPETLAUR: nin (for nino) — to bare one's chest. Nite — to uncover someone's chest. RR. elpanili, petlaua.
5010: Pret. OELPANTLATI: nite — to cause someone pain, to cause them grief or irritation. RR. elpantli, tlatia.
5011: Pret. OELPANTLATLAC: n (for ni) — to suffer from the stomach, to have stomach pains from having eaten too much. RR. elpantli, tlatla.
5012: One who suffers from stomach ailments, etc. R. elpantlatla.
5014: Stomach, chest. In comp. telpan (for to-elpan), our chest, the chest in general. See Euur. RR. el, tepanth.
5015: Pret. OELPANUI: nitla — to carry something on one's chest; to make the flaps of a garment flutter while walking quickly (haldear). R. elpantli.
5016: Pret. OELPANXOCOYAC: n (for ni) — to have heartburn, to suffer from the stomach. RR. elpantli, xocoya.
5017: Xiphoid cartilage. In comp. telpapalouh (for to-elpapalouh), our cartilage, the cartilage of the stomach. RR. elli, papalotl.
5018: Pret. OELPATZO: nite — to break, to fracture someone's ribs. RR. elli, patzoa.
5019: Pret. OELPETLAUR: nin (for nino) — to bare one's chest, one's stomach. Nite — to uncover someone's chest. RR. elli, petlaua.
5020: Pret. OELPOPOÇAUR: nin (for nino) — to become angry, to lose one's temper. RR. elli, popoçaua.
5021: Ring. In comp. yelquauhyo, its ring; quauhtemalacatl yelquauhyo, hub, ring of a wheel, that which holds it fixed to the axle.
4963: One who desires, wishes; teaxca eleuiani, envious of another's possessions. R. eleuia.
4964: Pret. OELEUILI: niqu or nicte — to wish good things for someone, to want them to obtain something. R. eleuia.
4965: Covetousness, ardent desire; teaxca elewihztli, covetousness of another's possessions. R. eleuia.
4966: Pret. OELEUILLAN (Par.): to wish to desire something. RR. eleuia, tlam.
4968: Desirable, worthy of envy. R. nitla — eleuia.
4969: Pret. OELEUILTI: ninote — to make oneself desired by others. Mcte — to make someone desire something. R. eleuia.
4971: Pret. OELILPI: nin (for nino) — to wrap oneself, to cover one's chest. Nite — to cover, to wrap someone in this manner. RR. elh, ilpia.
4972: n. v. Laborer, cultivator, peasant. R. elimiqui.
4973: Pret. OELIMIG: » (for mo) — to be plowed, to be prepared, speaking of land before sowing. N (for ni) — to plow the land; am-elimiqui (for an-elimiqui), you plow; elimiqui in quaquaueque, the oxen plow.
4974: Plow, plowshare. Used in comp. only: quaquaue yelimiquia, plowshare, that which serves for the plowing of the ox. R. elimiqui.
4977: Pret. OELINCO: min (for nino) — to have indigestion, to eat too much. Mte — to make someone eat too much. RR. elli, .... (2).
4978: Pret. OELIXUITI: mn (for nino) — to have eaten too much, to have indigestion. Nite — to make someone eat too much. RR. elli, ituitia.
4979: Pret. OELLAQUAUR: in (for nino) — to exert oneself, to stir oneself up, to become animated. Nite — to animate, to excite someone. RR. elli, tlaquaua.
4980: Pret. OELLATZILHUI: to cause someone extreme distress. RR. elli, tlatzilhuia.
4981: Half mad, bewildered. RR. elli, tlaueliloc.
4982: Pret. OELLELACGIC: n (for ni) — to experience great pain, deep affliction; n-el- nite — to hate; lelaci in ni-choca, to weep greatly. RR. ellelli, aci.
4943: Pret. OELCHIQUIUHEUX: to tire someone out. RR. elchiquiuitl, eua.
4944: Pret. OELCHIQUIUHPETLAUH: nin (for nino) — to uncover one's stomach. Nite — to uncover someone else in this way. RR. elchiquiuitl, petlaua.
4945: Stomach, chest. In comp. nelchiquiuh (for no-elchiquiuh), my chest; telchiquiuh (for to-elchiquiuh), our stomach, our chest, the stomach or chest in general; fig. aqutlaçotlac in yelchiquiuh (Olm.), he put himself in danger, threw himself into adversity. RR. elli, chiquiuitl.
4947: Pret. OELCICIUH: n (for ni) or non — to sigh; ma nen on-elciciuh (Car.), do not sigh.
4948: — Rev. elcicuilia, elciciuiltia or elciciuitia. Impersonal: elcicioua (Car.). RR. elli, ciciui.
4950: While sighing, with a sigh. RR. elciciuilizth, ca.
4953: Used in comp. only: yelciciutca, stomach of birds, breast of poultry (pechuga de ave). R. elciciui.
4954: Pret. OELCIN: nin (for nino) — to choke while eating, to suffocate, to drown. RR. elli, cima.
4955: Pit of the stomach. In comp. telcocoyan (for to-elcocoyan), our hollow, the pit of the stomach in general. RR. elli, cocoya.
4956: Pret. OELÇOÇOLO: n (for ni) — to sigh. RR. elli, çoloni.
4957: Spleen. In comp. nelcomal (for no-elcomal), my spleen; telcomal (for to-elcomal), our spleen, the spleen in general. RR. elli, comalli. ELELE! interj. expressing lamentation, suffering: Ah! Ah!
4958: Pret. OELELELTI: nin (for nino) — to differ in the valuation of a merchandise.
4959: Pret. OELELEUÍ: nitla — to complain, to show or express one's pain; teuan nitla-eleleuia, to groan with someone, to share in their pain, to lament with them. R. elele.
4960: n. v. (Olm.). Laborer, cultivator, peasant, man of low condition. R. elemiqui.
4961: Pret. OELEMIC: to work the land.
4962: Pret. OELEUI: nite — to desire ardently; n (for ni) — to till the land, a person. Nitla or niqu — to desire, to covet something intensely; niqu-eleuia in mahuiçotl (Par.), I desire honor. Pass. eleuilo.
4926: See Eeca, and likewise for words beginning in the same way.
4927: EY or YEI, adj. n. used to count animate beings and flat, thin objects. Three; ic e or iquei, third; inic ei or iniquei, the third. Plur. eirtin; im eixtin, rev. im eixtintzitzin, all three. In comp. e: eilhuitl, three days; sometimes e is used alone: oc e, three others, three more. With the postpositions Can, Campa, pa: excan or yexcan, in three parts, places, ways, etc.; excan tlapanqui or tzayanqui, broken, split, divided into three; excan icac, which exists in three ways; ox excan, in three other parts, etc.; freq. eexcan, in each set of three parts or portions; yexcampa, from three parts or places; yexcampa nacace machiotl, triangular figure, having three sides, three faces; yexcampa tlamachiotilli or tlanacazmachio-till, figured, drawn with three angles or three sides; expa or yexpa, three times; yexpa yei, three times three; oc yexpa, oc expa or oquexpa, three more times; yexpa, oc expa or oquexpa ixquich, triple; yexpa ixquich nic-cui, to overcharge, to take three times the value; freq. eexpa, every three times; oc eexpa, three other times each.
4928: Pret. OEILHUITI: n (for ni) — to spend, to stop for three days in a place. R. eilhuul.
4929: On the third day. RR. eilhuitl, ca.
4931: Quarter, quarterly thing, lasting three months. R. eimetztli.
4932: In the third month, within three months; eimetztica mo-chiua tlaolli, maize that grows and ripens within the space of three months. RR. eimetztli, ca.
4935: theol. There are three (persons); cuix hc-neltoca ca inic personas eittitica? (J. B.), do you believe that there are three persons in God? RR. ei, ica.
4936: n. Three nights. RR. ei, yualh.
4938: Or ELH, adj., used only with the possessives no, mo, i, etc.: nel, I am diligent, active, eager, careful, etc.; mel, yel, tel or teltin, amel or ameltin, ymel or yeltin (Olm.). With the negative, one says: anel, I am lazy; amel, you are lazy; etc.
4939: Pret. OELAC: n (for ni) — to be afflicted, saddened. RR. el, aqui.
4940: Pret. OBLCACATZCAC: n (for ni) — to suffer from the stomach from having eaten too much. RR. elli, cacatza.
4941: One who suffers from the stomach from having eaten too much. R. elcacatzca.
4942: n. v. Stomachache caused by overindulgence. R. elcacatzca.
5023: Pret. OELQUEQUEZ: to break someone's ribs. RR. elli, quequeca.
5024: Liver. In comp. neltapach (for no-eltapach), my liver; teltapach (for to-eltapach), nite — to break, our liver, the liver in general; nimeltapach (for nimo-eltapach), I am your liver, your son, that is to say you treat me as such (Olm.). RR. el, tapachtli.
5025: Pret. OELTEN: in the stomach, to be suffocated, replete, to have eaten too much.
5028: Replete, having a full stomach, having eaten too much. R. eltenu.
5029: Pret. OELTEPETLAC: n (for ni) — to stammer, to be a stutterer. RR. elli, tepetla. n (for ni) — to have pain in
5030: Pret. OELTEPINI: nino (for nino) — to beat one's chest. RR. elli, tepinia.
5031: Xiphoid cartilage, gizzard of birds. In comp. teltepitz (for to-eltepitz), our cartilage, the stomach cartilage in general; yeltepitz, its stomach. RR. elli, tepitztli.
5033: etc.: nino (for nino) — to have a blocked throat, to choke. RR. elli, tepotlamia.
5034: Pret. OGURIC: n (for ni) — to be careful, eager, active, diligent, skillful. R. el.
5035: Pret. OUI: nite — to force, to compel someone. R. elt.
5038: Diligence, care, skill in doing something. R. elti.
5040: Pret. OELTZACU: n (for ni) — to stammer, to be a stutterer. RR. elli, tzacui.
5041: Entrails, intestines. In comp. neltzacuilhuaz (for no-eltzacuilhuaz), my entrails; teltzacuilhuaz (for to-eltzacuilhuaz), our entrails, entrails in general. RR. eltzacui, uatza.
5042: Pret. OELTZACU: to have a blocked throat. RR. el, tzaqua.
5083: Pret. OBTICAMA: mic — to believe, to judge that something is heavy. RR. etic, mati.
5084: Pret. OETICIUH, v. n. often preceded by the adv. ye, already. To settle, to consolidate, to become secure on its base, speaking of a construction. R. etic.
5085: Pret. OETICIUITI: mn (for nino) — to become heavy. Mite — to be heavy for someone, to give them too heavy a weight, to impose an excessive burden on them. R. eticiui.
5086: Pret. OgriLi: mite — to be heavy for someone, to give them too heavy a weight. R. etia.
5088: Pret. OETIXTIMOTLALI, v. n. To become, to be heavy, speaking of an object; nopan etixti- motlalia, upon me is placed a very heavy thing.
5090: Bean or broad bean. In comp. neuh (for no-euh), my beans.
5091: Field, place planted with beans or broad beans. RR. eil, la.
5092: Pret. OETLAZ: n (for ni) — to sow beans, broad beans. Impers. etlaço, one sows, everyone sows beans. RR. etl, tlaça.
5093: In three ways, in three parts. RR. etlamantli, ca.
5094: Or YETLAMANTLI, adj. n. Three things, three pairs or parts, three different objects; yetlamanthi ic tlachuhth, made in three ways; ic ellamantli, third part; inic etlamantli, the third part. Plur. yetlamanirtün, all three things. RR. ei, tlamanth.
5095: (Sah., Clav.). A type of porridge that was eaten on the day of the third feast of the god of waters, Tlaloc, in the month of etzalqualizthi.
5096: V. (Sah., Clav.). Sixth month of the year. RR. etzalli, qua.
5097: Rev. of the irreg. verb ca.
5098: Pret. OEUNH or EUAC: n (for ni) — to rise, to go away, to depart, to flee, to fly away; nouic eua in tlaqualli, food disgusts me; notlocpa eua, he sides with me, he takes my party; teuic or teca n-eua, to attack someone, to throw oneself upon them, to show disrespect toward them; teirco or teixco teicpac n-eua, to be impolite, uncivil toward someone, to show them disrespect, to offend them; teixpampa n-eua, to flee, to avoid someone, to distance oneself from them; imicpampa x-eua (for xt-eua) in ahuilnenque (Par.), flee the presence of dissolute people; teixpampa t-eua (for ti-eua), we retreat, we flee the enemy, etc.; teuán or teuicpa n-eua, to side with someone, to be factious, etc.; tlaixco eua or yeua, filth, what is on the surface of things (limpiaduras); tepal n-eua, to help someone, to pull them from danger; tepan n-eua, to protect, to help, to favor someone; temacpan-eua, to escape, to evade, to flee from someone's hands; with the particle on: non or ye non-eua, to depart, to go somewhere; to have what is necessary; atle on-eua, he is poor, does not have what is necessary; uel on-eua, to arrive at the right moment, speaking of a thing, it is just, sufficient. Min (for nino) — to rise, to stand up when lying down; to depart, to be ready, prepared; mipiltontli ipan nin-eua, to call oneself, to pass oneself off as young, as less old than one is; aoc m-eua, he is slow, he has not yet departed; am-eua, he is heavy, sluggish. Nic — to raise someone who is lying down. Nila or niqu — to have the strength, the courage to carry a heavy object; to sing a song; amo nic-eua, to not have the strength to take, to carry something. Eua in comp. indicates haste: nino-quelzteua (Olm.), to rise quickly, to depart promptly; nite-nauatiteua, to give someone an order while speaking; qu-itoteuac, he said something while leaving. Rev. eualta, euitia. Impers. eohua, one rises, one departs.
5099: Parchment; lit. leaf, paper of leather. RR. euatl, amatl. EUAC; adj. v. Departed, gone out; temacpa euac, escaped from someone's hands. R. eua.
5100: Soles, leather footwear. RR. euatl, cacthi.
5101: That which is removed; pani euacayotl, filth, refuse (limpiaduras). R. eua.
5102: Saddle; euaicpalli chiuhqui, saddle maker. RR. euatl, icpalli.
5043: Xiphoid cartilage. In comp. teltzaqualhuaz (for to-eltzaqualhuaz), our cartilage, the cartilage of the stomach in general. RR. nin (for nino) — elzaqua, uatza.
5045: Pret. OELTZATZACU, freq. of ELTza- eu: n (for ni) — to stammer a great deal, to be extremely tongue-tied.
5046: Pret. OELTZITZICAC: to have a stomachache, to be full, suffocated, to have n (for ni) — eaten too much. RR. el, taizica.
5047: One who suffers from stomach pain, who has stomachaches from having eaten too much. R. eltaitaica.
5049: One who has a hairy chest, covered with hair. R. eltzonyotl.
5050: Chest hair. In comp. teltzonyo (for t-eltzonyo), hair covering the chest. RR. elli, tzonyotl.

5053: Field of kidney beans or broad beans. RR. eil, mil.

5057: A type of edible herb (epazote); it is used against dysentery and inflammations (Hern.).

5059: That which is in three rows; epantitoc mesa, three rows of tables. RR. epantl, onoc.
5060: n. Three rows, lines, files, etc.
5062: Small fox, a quadruped with a strong fetid odor; — croton vulpinum (yerva del zorrillo), a medicinal plant used as a purgative and emetic.
5063: Lentils; àn ipampa aquiton epatzacthi (Gar.), for a little lentils. RR. etl, patzaua.
5064: Dish made of kidney beans, broad beans.
5066: Cooked kidney bean, broad bean. RR. etl, pauaci.
5067: 5. (Sah.). Master of religious ceremonies. Torq. calls it epquahztli, Hern. epoaquacuiliztli and Clav. epcoacuiltzin.
5068: Precious pearl, mother-of-pearl. RR. eptli, yollotli.
5070: n. for counting animate beings and flat, thin objects. Sixty; lit. three-twenties. Plural epoalixtin; im epoalixtin, all sixty. With the postpositions can, pa: epoalcan, in sixty parts, places, etc.; oc epoalcan, in sixty other parts; freq. eepoalcan, in sixty parts, places, etc.; epoalpa, sixty times; oc epoalpa or oquepoalpa, sixty other times; freq. eepoalpa or yeepoalpa, every sixty times; oc eepoalpa, etc., again every sixty times. RR. ei, poalli.
5072: Shell, seashell. *ESPADAANA, pret. OESPADAAN: v. (for ni) — to unsheathe, seize, draw the sword. RR. espada, ana. *ESPADAANALIZTLI, n. v. Action of unsheathing, of drawing the sword. R. espadaana. *ESPADACOPINA, pret. OESPADACOPIN: v. (for ni) — to unsheathe, draw the sword. RR. espada, copina. *ESPADACOPINALIZTLI, n. v. Action of unsheathing, of drawing the sword. R. espadacopina.
5073: n. v. One who picks beans, kidney beans, etc. R. etequi.

5077: Action of picking kidney beans, broad beans. R. etequi.

5103: Pret. OEUAYOTEC: nitla — to cut leather, to cut hides. RR. euayotl, tequi.
5104: Pret. OEUAYOTI: nitla — to cover, to furnish a thing with leather. R. euayotl.
5105: Pret. OEUAYOTILAN: (for ni) — to stretch hides. RR. euayotl, tilana.

5107: or YEUAYOTLACÇA, pret. OEUAYO-TLAZ, etc.: nitla — to peel something, to remove the bark, skin, scale, etc. RR. euayotl, tlaça.

Nitla — to send, to dispatch a thing. Nitetla — to reprimand someone, to reproach an ingrate, to reproach a benefaction. Rev. of eua.
5111: n. Strap, thong, leather lace. RR. euatl, mecatl.
5112: Leather shop, place where hides are sold. RR. euatl, namacoyan.
5113: adj. v. Departed; teixco euani, uncivil, rude, audacious, impolite, one who offends; temacpa or teixpampa euani, escaped, having gotten away from someone's hands; tepan euani, protector, one who helps, supports someone; teuic or teuicpa euani, impolite, rude, rebellious, seditious. R. eua.
5114: Garment of skin. RR. euatl, quemitl.
5115: Pret. OEUAQUIMILO: nitla — to cover, to wrap, to furnish with leather, to fur, to line with skins. RR. euatl, quimiloa.
5117: Pret. OEUATICATCA: n (for ni) — to be seated; çan t-euaticatca (Par.), you were always seated. Rev. euititica: ma xim-euititie (Olm.), good day, be seated. R. eua, ca.
5118: Garment of skin (of lamb, etc.). RR. euatl, tilmatli.
5119: Pret. OEUATINEN: n or non — to live in abundance; amo n-, anon- or atle non-euatinemi, to be in misery, in poverty, to live unhappily. RR. eua, nemi.
5122: Pret. OEUATIQUETZ: nino (for nino) — to rise, to stand up, to rear up; oc ceppa nin-euatiquetza, to rise another time; teuan nin-euatiquetza, to rise together with someone. Nitla or nic — to lift, to raise, to set up a thing; fig. mixtli, puctli, ayauitl qu-euatiquetzaco (Olm.), he came to establish a new doctrine. Rev. euatiquechilia. RR. eua, quetza.
5144: Female drum player (tañedora de pandero). R. euaueuetzotzona.
5145: pret. oEuaur: aila — to double, to line a thing with skin. R. euatl.
5147: pret. (for ni) — to play the musette. RR. euatl, uilacapitzoa.
5150: wineskin; can pan qualli euaxiquipilli, small wineskin; euaxiquipilh, wineskin for wine. RR. euatl, xiquipilli.

5154: (for euhti-eua), pret. (for ni) — to leave running, to go quickly to a place. Nin (for nino) — to get out of bed quickly, to rise in haste. RR. eua, eua.
5155: pret. OEUtI, etc.: ninolla or nino- tlatla — to beg, to ask for alms; tepal nino-
5156: tlatla-yeuia, to beg for charity from door to door.
5157: pret. OEUILOTLATI: nite — to engage in some kind of sorcery.
5158: Or YEUITIA, pret. OEUITI, etc.: min (for mno) — to strive, to excite oneself; lhuicacpa hual m-ehuitie in totetlatzontequilicatzin (Par.), from heaven our judge will come. Vite — to send messengers; to animate, excite, comfort, restore someone with food; amo onte-yeuitia, it is insufficient. Rev. of eua.
5159: Rev. of the irreg. v. EuaTICA.
5160: pret. OBUIUITLAC: n (for ni) — to pull up beans by the roots. RR. etl, uiuilla.
5161: One who picks or pulls up beans, etc. R. euiuilla.
5162: Beans that have been gathered. R. euwiuitla.
5163: One who picks beans, etc. R. euiuitla.
5164: Or EZXAXAUANI, pret. OEXAXAUAN, etc.: v. (for ni) — to lose blood. RR. eztli, xaxauania.
5165: Or EZXAXAUANILIZTLI, n. See. Flux, loss of blood. R. ezxaxauani.
5167: Pret. OEXIUNTI: v. (for ni) — to stay, to reside for three years in a place. R. exiutl.
5168: At the end of three years, in the third year, after a period of three years. RR. exiuitl, ca.
5170: One who picks still-green beans. R. exotequi.
5171: Pret. OEXOTEC: v. (for ni) — to pick green beans. RR. exotl, tequi.

5174: Pret. OEXOUI: still-green kidney beans. R. exotl.
5175: Pret. OEXOUIUITLAC: to pick green beans. RR. exotl, uiuitla.
5176: Or EXOUIUITLANI, n. v. One who picks green beans. R. exouiuitla.
5178: Pret. OEXPAUI: nitla — to give a third cultivation. R. expa.
5179: n. (Clav.). Fish pond, reservoir inside the great temple of Mexico. It was stained with the blood of priests who bathed there after having, as penance, pierced various parts of their body with maguey thorns. RR. eztli, apan.
5180: Scab or scabies crust. RR. eztli, cacauatl.
5186: (Clav.). Commander-in-chief, supreme commander. RR. eztli, uaqui.
5187: Pret. OEZYOAC: v. (for ni) — to cover oneself with blood. R. eztli.
5188: Pret. OEZMOLON: v. (for ni) — to lose blood. RR. eztli, moloni.
5189: Pearl, flux, flow of blood. R. ezmoloni.
5190: Pret. OEZNELO: to cover with blood. nite — to cover someone with blood. nin (for nino) — to cover oneself with blood. nitla — to make bloody, to cover a thing with blood.

5193: A type of apple or red liquor called dragon's blood (Clav.); a plant from warm lands good against dysentery and breast ailments (Sah., Hern.). RR. eztli, patli.
5196: Pret. OEZPIPICAC: v. (for ni) — to lose blood. RR. eztli, pipica.
5197: n. v. Loss, flux, flow of blood. R. ezpipica.
5198: Large tree that produces the gum called ezpatl (Hern., Clav.). RR. eztli, quauitl.
5199: Pret. OEZQUIZ: v. (for ni) — to have one's period, one's menstruation. RR. eztli, quiça.
5200: Catamenial flux, period, menstruation. R. ezquiça.
5201: Pret. OEZQUIXTI: nite — to wound someone by blows, to make their blood flow by striking them. RR. eztli, quixtia.
