5203: A kind of red jasper or green-spotted agate. The Mexicans attached this stone to their wrist and claimed it stopped hemorrhages (Hern., Bét.). RR. eztli, tecpatl. H, a letter used by various authors: 1° to distinguish in certain cases the plural from the singular: tlatlacoani, the sinner; tlatlacoanih, the sinners; te-tlaçotla, he loves someone; te-tlaçotlah, they love someone or people; 2° to mark aspiration in the middle or at the end of words: uitl, day; nite-milhtia, I work for someone's benefit; nocalh, my house; etc. — It is not generally used as an initial letter; thus for words that would be spelled as in hual, huel, Huitzilopochtli, etc., look under the vowel that immediately follows the h, except for the few following words. — The letter h is joined especially to c to represent the sound ch: chantli (for tchantli), dwelling; pepechtli (for pepetchili), id.; etc.
5204: HA HA! interj. to express bursts of laughter.
5205: pret. OEZTEN: nite — to bruise someone. RR. eztli, tema.
5206: pret. OEZTEN: to be bruised, to have bruises. RR. eztli, temi.
5208: A kind of jasper used to stop hemorrhages. RR. eztli, tetl. — to be bruised (for nino).
5209: Blood; eztli nic-apilza or nic-noquia, to have a flux of blood; aocmo qualli eztli or tlacauhqui eztli, corrupted blood; eztli ioui, blood vessel; lit. blood its path; eztli tlapalli, noble, illustrious blood. In comp. nez (for no-ez), my blood. See Eztli.
5210: pret. OEZUI: nin (for nino) — to cover oneself with blood. nitla or nic — to bloody a thing, to cover it with blood; niqu-ezuia notilma (Olm.), I bloody my garment. Rev. ezuilia. R. eztli.
5211: pret. OEZUILI: nicno — to bloody a thing; nicno-ezuilia notilma (Olm.), I bloody my garment. Rev. of ezuia.
5212: pret. OEZUITON: her monthly periods, her menses. RR. eztli, uitomi.
5213: Catamenial flux, monthly periods, menses. R. ezuitomi.
5215: See Ezaxauaniliztli. — (for ni) — to have. HE! interj. Oh! cry of pain.
5216: HI HI! interj. to express bursts of laughter. HICOX, n. Fig; çan cuel mochiuani hicox, early fig; hicox texoxoctli or xoxoctetl, green figs, not yet ripe; hicox uacqui, tetzolli or uatzalli, dried figs; hicox uatzalli tlatepitatentli, dried, flattened figs. HICOXQUAUHTLA, n. Orchard, a place planted with fig trees. RR. hicoxquautl, tla. HICOXQUAUITL, n. Fig tree. RR. hicox, quauitl.
5217: HO HO! interj. to express bursts of laughter. HUI! interj. used to call someone. Hey there! hui, nican oticatca, nocniuhtzine! (Par.), hey, how did you come to be here, my friend! Y and I (These letters are used interchangeably; but in general y is placed before a vowel.) I, adj. poss. His, her, its, of him, of her, etc.; used only in composition with nouns and postpositions: ita or rev. itatzin, his father; icolhuan, his ancestors; ipan, upon him; ipan in tepetl, upon the mountain; before a, there is elision: ixpan (for iixpan) in nociatzin (Par.), in the presence of my grandmother. Plur. in or im before a vowel and the consonants m, p: incolhuan, their ancestors; imamauh (Olm.), their paper; imaxca in nocihuamonhuan (Par.), the property of my daughters-in-law; immon, their son-in-law; impan, upon them. Before u, in disappears: yuilox (Olm.), their pigeons. I, pret. OIC: nitla — to drink, to swallow. Rev. ia, ilua, itia. Pass. ihua. I or IA, irreg. verb conjugated with the poss. no, mo, i, etc. and the postp. pan; it means to suit, to fit, to go well, speaking of a thing: indicative present, nopan i or ia, mopan i or ia, ipan i or ia, topan i or ia, amopan i or ia, inpan i or ia; uel nopan i in notilma, my garment fits me well; imperfect nopan ia or iaya, etc.; pret. onopan ic or ix, etc.; pluperfect onopan iaca or ixca, etc.; fut. nopan iz or iaz, etc.; imp. ma nopan i or ia, etc. Impers. tepan i or ia; uel otepan ic in tlaqualli, everyone had an equal share of food (Olm.). See Ii, Iia and Ti, Tia.
5219: (?), n. used in comp.: noyaca, my offense, that is to say the offense for which I have been condemned to death (delicto por el qual soy sentenciado a muerte).
5221: Cartilage separating the two nostrils. In comp.: noyacacelica, my cartilage; toyacacelica, our cartilage, the cartilage of the nose. RR. yacatl, celicayotl.

5233: (Sah.). A kind of corn bread. RR. yacatl, coloa, tamalli.
5234: One who has had the nose cut off. R. yacacotona.

5306: adj. v. Blunted, pointed off. R. yacatetepunoa.

OYACATI: to arrive, to occur; nopan mo-yacatia, to receive, to experience great grief; cococ teopouhqui nopan omo-yacati, a great misfortune has befallen me, I have suffered extreme hardship. 5340: One who has a large nose, a long nose. R. yacauitzoa.

5302: Pret. OYACATETECUINO: nitla — to blunt a point. RR. yacatl, tetecuinoa.
5263: Pret. OYAGANTICACA: to be the first among those who are standing. RR. yacana, icac.
5264: Pret. OYACANTIA: to go guiding someone, leading a blind person. R. yacana.
5265: Sebaceous matter nearly solid, lodging in the pores, particularly of the skin of the nose, and ending on the outside in a small blackish point; lit. worm of the nose. RR. yacatl, ocuilin.
5266: adj. v. Pug-nosed, having a turned-up nose. RR. yacatl, ololtic.
5268: : noyacapannemac, my right of primogeniture; oqui-na-macac in yacapannemac (Car.), he sold his right of primogeniture. RR. yacapanili, nemactli.
5269: Right of primogeniture. In comp.: iyacapantlatqui, his right of primogeniture. RR. yacapantli, tlatquitl.
5270: Eldest, firstborn. In comp.: noyacapan, my firstborn; plur. noyacapahuan, my eldest sons; teyacapan, someone's firstborn; tiyacapan, you are the eldest son. RR. yacatl, tepantli.
5271: adj. and n. Broad at the tip, having a large beak; a type of wild duck (Hern.); necoc yacapatlauac tepuzteximaloni, hammer, a stonecutter's tool. RR. yacatl, patlauac.
5272: Having a straight and long nose.
5274: conquered by the Mexican monarch Moteuhçoma I (Clav.). RR. yacapitzaua, tlan.
5275: or YACAPITZAUAC, adj. v. Pointed, sharpened; tepuzmacquauitl yacapitzauac, a sharpened sword. R. yacapitzaua.
5276: Pret. OYACAPITZAUH: nitla — to sharpen a thing. RR. yacatl, pitzaua.
5277: n. God of merchants (Sah.); — a type of small white fish abundant in the lakes of Chalco, Chapallan, etc. (Clav.); wild duck (Hern.). See Yacapitzactic. R. yacapitzaua.
5278: Pret. OYACAPOCQUI: to blow smoke at someone's nose. RR. yacatl, pocuia.
5279: Pret. OYACAPOPOUH: nino — to blow one's nose, to clean one's nose. RR. yacatl, popoa.
5280: Odor that comes from the nose. nite — to cast a smell toward the nose. RR. yacatl, potoniliztli.
5281: Having a cut-off nose. R. yacapuztequi.
5282: Pret. OYACAPUZTEC, v. n. To lose its point, speaking of an object. nite — to cut off someone's nose. nitla — to blunt a thing, to cut or break the tip of an object. RR. yacatl, puztequi.
5343: n. Fineness of a point. In comp.: yacauitzauhca, its point. R. yacauitzauhqui.
5344: Having a sharp, fine, tapered point. R. yacauitzauhqui.
5345: Pret. OYACAUITZAUH, v. To sharpen, to become pointed. RR. yacatl, uitzaui.
5346: Pret. OYACAUITZO: nitla — to make a point, to sharpen it. RR. yacatl, uitzoa.
5347: adj. v. Sharp, pointed, or having a long nose; yacauitetie mil, a sharp-tipped dart. R. yacauitzoa.
5350: Turquoise, a precious stone which people of quality used to adorn their nose. RR. yacatl, xiuitl.
5351: [Imperfect indicative of the irregular verb yaun.
5352: Weakness of one who suffers, debility, languor. R. yaqui.
5353: Pret. OYAYACAN, freq. of yaana: nite — to guide, to lead someone frequently.
5356: n. v. freq. of yaziztli. The act of going, movement, continual walking.
5357: freq. of yamaztic. Tender, soft, supple.
5358: Pret. OYAYAOTLAC, freq. of yaotla: lo — to skirmish, to engage in skirmishes.
5359: adj. v. freq. of yapalectic. Covered with freckles.
5360: freq. of YAPALEUALIZTLI. Spots on the face or body, bruising, ecchymosis, marks left by blows on the skin.
5361: Pret. OYAYATICACA: ni — to totter, to sway while standing. RR. yauh, cac.
5362: Pret. OYAYATINEN: to go about continuously. RR. yayatiuh, nemi.
5404: 5. First-born, boy or girl. In comp.: noyancuiyo, my first-born; iyancuiyo, his/her first-born. nitla — R. yancuic.
5405: Pret. OYANCUILI. Nite — to renew someone. Nitla — to renew a thing. nino — to renew oneself. R. yancuic.
5407: Elder brother of the king of Tetztuco, Neçahualcoyotl (Bét.).
5408: Recent, fresh, new. RR. yancuic, paie.
5409: from the north of the province of Mixtecapan (Clav.).
5410: n. v. Pilgrim, traveler; auic yani, vagabond, wanderer, one who goes here and there; cauallo ipan yani, one who travels on horseback; oui ipan yani, unfortunate, one who falls into misfortune; tepan yani, adulterer, one who violates the marriage bond. R. yauh.
5413: Pret. OYAOAN: nite — to make someone a prisoner of war, to seize an enemy.
5416: Pret. OYAOCALGENCAUH: nino — to provision a fortress, to bring there everything that is necessary. RR. yaocalli, cencaua.
5417: Pret. OYAOCALCHICAUH: nino — to fortify, to repair a fort. RR. yaocalli, chicaua.
5418: Pret. OYAOCALLAPALTILI: nino — same meaning as the preceding. RR. yaocalli, tlapaltilia.
5419: Office of governor of a fortress. RR. yaocalli, tlapializtli.
5420: Same meaning as the preceding. R. yaocallapixqui.
5421: n. v. Governor of a town or fortress. RR. yaocalli, tlapixqui.
5422: Fortress, structure built for war. RR. yaoyotl, calli.
5443: Pret. OYAOQUIZGAYACAN: nino — to command troops, warriors. RR. yaoquizqui, yacana.
5445: Captain, chief, commander of troops. R. yaoquizcayana.
5448: Pret. OYAOQUIZCATEGAC: nino — to establish, set up, pitch a camp, take position.
5451: n. v. Captain, chief, commander of troops. R. yaoquizcatepachoa.
5452: Pret. OYAOQUIZGATEPACHO: nino — to command troops, warriors.
5456: Office of captain, military command. R. yaoquizcatepachoa.
5457: Pret. OYAOQUIZCATLALI: nino — to establish, pitch a camp. RR. yaoquizqui, tlalia.
5458: Military pay, soldier's wages. RR. yaoquizqui, tlaxtlauilli.
5460: ni — to earn military pay, to receive wages. RR. yaoquizqui, tlaxtlauilo.
5461: Pret. OYAOQUIZCATZACU: nino — to pitch, establish a camp; tito-yaoquizcatzaqua, we take position. RR. yaoquizqui, tzaqua.
5462: Soldier, warrior. Pl. yaoquizque, squadron, battalion, military maneuver; yaoquizque incenyeliz or intlatequiliz, military camp. RR. yaoyotl, quiça.
5463: Captain, chief, commander of troops. RR. yaoyotl, tachcauh.
2579: pret. ocemonoria, v. n. To be equal, well in tune, speaking of singing. R. cemonoc.
2580: pret. OCEMPAOTI: — to give oneself over to pleasures, to joy, to gladness, to enjoy something; nic-cempactia in noyollo, to rejoice from the bottom of one's heart, to give oneself over to joy wholeheartedly. RR. cen, pactia.
2581: Pret. OCEMPANAUI: nitla — to surpass a thing; tla-cempanauia, it exceeds all things, it is above everything; used in superlatives: tla-cempanauia ic uei, very great, the greatest; in cepayahuitl tla-cempanahuia inic iztac (Car.), snow is the whitest thing there is. RR. cem, panauia.
2582: Pret. OGEMPANAUILI: to surpass something; motla-cempanahuilia in totecuiyo Dios inc cenhueliilicecatzinthi (Car.), God, Our Lord, is sovereignly powerful. Rev. of cempa-
2584: pret. OCEMPANQUIZ: # — to go in procession. RR. cempantli, quiça.
2586: n. A file, a row of persons or things, a line of writing, etc.
2588: pret. OCEMPOUH: speaking of a count. Mila ou nic — to make the total, mo — to be added up, to count the whole of it. RR. cen, poa.
2591: n. Four hundred (cloaks, mats, sheets of paper, etc.); lit. twenty score.
2594: n. Twenty objects, pairs, or parts; ic cempoallamanthi, the twentieth part; inic cempoallamantli, the twentieth part, the twentieth object, etc. RR. cempoalli, tlamantli.
2595: from the province of Totonacapan, to the S.W. of Vera-Cruz; the first conquest of the Spaniards (Clav., Bét.). RR. cempoalli, tlan.
2596: n. used for counting animate beings and flat, thin objects; 2° order of units. Twenty, that is to say the complete count of the fingers, serving as the base of the numeral system; followed by the adj. n. oce omome, ome, onnaut, ommacuilli, onchiquace, onchicome, onchisuet, onchicunaui, ommatlactli, ommatlactli oce, ommatlactli omome, ommatlactli omei, ommatlactli onnaui, oncaxtolli, oncaxtolli oce, oncaxtolli omome, oncaxtolli omei, oncaxtolli onnaui, this adjective is used to designate the numbers from twenty-one to thirty-nine; ic cempoalli, twentieth; enic cempoalli, the twentieth; in cempoalli ce, twenty-one; in cempoalli ome, twenty-two; etc. Plural cempoalixtin: in cempoalixtin, all twenty. In comp. cempoal: cempoalilhuitl, twenty days; cempoalxiuitl, twenty years. With the postp. can, pa: cempoalcan, in twenty parts; oc cempoalcan, in twenty other parts; cempoalcan ommatlaccan, in thirty parts; cempoalpa, twenty times; cempoalpa oceppa, twenty-one times; cempoalpa ommacuilpa, twenty-five times; cempoalpa ixquich, twenty times as much. R. cempoa.
2597: n. Twenty rows, files, lines, etc. RR. cempoalli, tepantli.
5384: Medicinal plant. See Criommecararer. RR. yamanqui, patli.
5385: (Olm.). A gentle, peaceful, moderate person. RR. yamanqui, tlacatl.
5386: dim. of yamanqui. Somewhat tender, soft, fine.
5387: pret. OYAMANIX or OYAMANIAG: nū — to be moderate, calm, peaceful; fig. to be rich or well-off (Olm.). Nite — to soften someone. Nitla — to soften, to make supple, to prepare something, such as leather, etc., to make lukewarm, to cool, to soften by fire, to melt, etc.; tla-yamania, the weather is fine; lit. (the weather) is softened, calm, fair, etc.
5388: pret. OYAMANILI: nitla — to soften, to cool something, to make it lukewarm. R. yamania.
5389: Softly, finely, delicately. RR. yamaniliatli, ca.
5391: pret. OYOMANILTI (Olm.) to do good to someone; onech-yamanilti in patli nie — (Olm.), the remedy was beneficial to me. R. yamania.
5392: Tender, soft, supple, mellow, fine, delicate; yamanqui pepechtli, soft bed; yamanqui tilmatli, supple garment; yamanqui ichcatl, sheep with fine fleece; yamanqui ichcatomitl, soft, fine wool; in aic yamanqui in itlatol (Olm.), surly, whose speech is never gentle. R. yamania.
5393: pret. OYAMWAZTIZ: to tenderize. R. yamania.
5394: Soft, tender, fine, supple, mellow. R. yamaztia.
5395: Suppleness, fineness, tenderness, etc. R. yamaztia.
5396: dim. of yamaztic. Somewhat tender, soft, fine, supple, etc.: M — to soften, to become—
5397: marking place and joining the impersonal form of verbs, as in tepilolo, one hangs, tepiloloyan, place of hanging; tlaxcalchiualo, one makes bread, tlaxcalchiualoyan, bakery; tlaxcalnamaco, one sells bread, tlaxcalnamacoyan, place where bread is sold; cochiua, one sleeps, cochiuayan, dormitory; tlacela, everything turns green again, tlaceliayan, place where everything turns green; etc., etc. It also joins preterites ending in a consonant, but in that case the word, a true noun, is preceded by one of the possessives no, mo, i, etc.: atl iceuhyan (pres. chui), place where the waters rest; ye imochiuhyan in xocotl (Par.), it is already the season of fruits; atle nican imochiuhyan, nothing comes here, nothing grows here. Finally, it serves as a suffix to certain place names: Aacuihuayan, now Tacubaya; Tlaximaloyan; etc.
5398: pret. OYAN: nin (for nino) — to take shelter, to protect oneself from the rain. See Ixaya.
5399: adj. New, novel, fresh, recent; novice, beginner; yancuic metztli, new moon; yancuic chichiualayotlatetzauhtli, fresh cheese; in iquac yancuican huiloac yancuice Mexico, quin ye yuh nimatlacxiuhtia (Car.), the first time one came to the new Mexico, I must have been about ten years old.
5400: Newly, recently, for the first time; yancuicam moichoti or moquichuati, newly married woman, who has recently taken a husband; yancuicam mociuauali, newly married man, who has recently taken a wife; yancuicam pilhua, woman who has given birth to her first child; yancuican nic-cui, to start something, to barely begin it; yancuican nitla-tequi, to begin to harvest fruits, to cut, to start something; in iquac yancuican acico in Caxtilteca, huel quimmauhhaya, quim-içahuiaya in Mexica in tlequiquiztli (Car.), the first time the Spaniards arrived, firearms greatly frightened and astonished the Mexicans; amo quin yancuican noca timo-cayahua (Car.), it is not the first time you have mocked me. RR. yancuic, can.
5401: pret. OYANCUICANTILI: to renew something. R. yancuican.
